### DavidL — 2015-01-21T04:45:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153494>

You have my attention. This is not an area I've ever really thought about, given that my mobility and strength "have always been good"....note that I am really not assuming this. Josh we talked about Jaromir Jagr and his incredible longevity in the NHL. You mentioned that is his focus on this area of strength and mobility...he's going to pass Gordie Howe eventually in the scoring and land himself behind Wayne Gretzky and Mark Messier...incredible.

When I was my fittest it was when I was moving naturally...at 25 years old I was still climbing trees, doing parkour (even though it didn't exist yet) and doing other very natural movements. Makes sense...recently living the keto/epi-paleo lifestyle I became over excited in the strength gains I made (doubled every previous max) and maybe got a little carried away. I quickly packed on muscle, faster than I ever had before....now I'm feeling heavier and a little less mobile than I was before...trying to get the agility and flexibility I had 10 years ago in the most effective efficient way possible.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-21T04:51:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153495>

> [David Limacher said:](/goto/post?id=153494)
>
> You have my attention. This is not an area I've ever really thought about, given that my mobility and strength "have always been good"....note that I am really not assuming this. Josh we talked about Jaromir Jagr and his incredible longevity in the NHL. You mentioned that is his focus on this area of strength and mobility...he's going to pass Gordie Howe eventually in the scoring and land himself behind Wayne Gretzky and Mark Messier...incredible.
>
> When I was my fittest it was when I was moving naturally...at 25 years old I was still climbing trees, doing parkour (even though it didn't exist yet) and doing other very natural movements. Makes sense.
>
> I am in search of optimum and you guys have my attention....

strength is centration and centration is strength, nothing more. it has nothing to do with muscle size or mass.
strength is the ability to maintain optimal joint congruency throughout a range of positions under varying loads and forces. this is why jagr has the "strong torso" you were talking about when you describe his skating. he does a lot of work with pavel kolar who is the founder of DNS and the head guy at the prague school of rehabilitation. kolar is a pioneer in his own right, he spent every day for a year and a half videoing his son and analysing innate human motor patterning, then wrote one of the most [amazing books of the decade](http://www.rehabps.com/REHABILITATION/Clinical_Rehabilitation_textbook.html) in this field

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-21T12:02:50+0200

Also, as a child grows limb lengths will change?
>
> Strength has nothing to do with size/mass?? Define strength... if additional mass affects leverages in a favourable manner which allows more weight to be moved, have I increased strength?

hypertrophy by definition shows inefficiency. it is mass equivalence at work. one can increase ocllagen cytoarchitecture and crosslinking without increasing muscle mass.
bodybuilders choose the most inefficient means of lifting something in order to hypertrophy muscles for competition.
"optimal movement patterns" do change as a result of a modern world, not by default of growth.
changes in limb lengths make no difference whatsoever as you would see in the case of many of the DNS lecturers, they have the same imprinted pattern, so long as movement patterning remians the constant during development through to old age. of course the modern lifestyle will remove this constant.

what we are looking for is functional ideal, it is rarely seen in modern man.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-21T12:19:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153523>

The contractile apparatus is the least sophisticated part of the entire motor system. Consider what happens when the spinal cord is severed, the brain's inhibitory signals cannot reach the contractile apparatus and the result is spastic paralysis (concentric activation of flexion, internal rotation and adduction of affected limbs)
Thus concentric contraction is the default setting before higher center control is installed. So why is everyone obsessed with training concentrically the dumbest least sophisticated part of the system when this is not where the intelligence lies????

### SeaHorse — 2015-01-21T16:30:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153534>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=153486)
>
> again, there is no point challenging a poor pattern, as you end up engramming the poor pattern into the neural system
>
> development is the pointer toward functional ideal, to regain it we must return to these developmental positions and regain proficiency in all of them

So how does this apply to balance boards etc….now I'm thinking that being able to balance and have the board stabile would not necessarily mean you are accessing your functional ideal…it might mean you are getting good at poor patterning….

Applying this to horse riding….a rider and horse can meet a workable compromise and learn to move together on a straight line, but it wouldn't mean either one was in a functionally ideal pattern….

Something I've been learning the past few years is how differently a horse moves when their thought initiates a movement…vs mechanical aids which create compliance. The elusive "softness" that is visible in muscles (no matter how hard they are working) just doesn't happen with most horse/human interactions. And I guess I should add that it needs to be a thought within the realm of feeling safe…otherwise we're moving into the sympathetic nervous system areas of fight flight freeze and collapse

All this to say that I think I've become pretty good at moving badly both on the ground and on horseback

### JoeBranca — 2015-01-21T20:11:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153544>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=153534)
>
> So how does this apply to balance boards etc….now I'm thinking that being able to balance and have the board stabile would not necessarily mean you are accessing your functional ideal…it might mean you are getting good at poor patterning….

I think balance can be looked at as the outcome of motor stability within basic posture in both low and upright positions and movements, eventually on a narrow or some sort of self limiting surface. Josh can correct me if I'm off, balance can refer to both internal coordination and the quality of the observed movement pattern which is something later "earned" by the child through trial and error as stabilizations are expressed in the later stages of development appropriate to the movements (sitting upright to standing upright with upper body supports to less upper body support to walking, etc).

In retraining movement in adults, balance is a _skill set_ that should start from the ground up, so to speak, with the simplest challenges to postural stability, and assessing the appropriate mix of tension and relaxation for postures and locomotive movements. In upright, standing and walking are probably the two first "balance" skills to revisit, not to mention transitions from ground to upright and vice versa.

Proprioception has to be regained through basic balance progressions. I think there is eventually a place for balance boards and other unstable surfaces, but s_tarting_ with those seems to me like putting the horse before the cart in terms of balance complexity, since it skips past the basics of stabilization in static posture and locomotion, and entrains compensatory correctives which would tend toward inefficient reflexes in any situation demanding balance.

### dantothep — 2015-01-21T22:04:44+0200

Josh I think the articles on IAP just solved a riddle I had been trying to solve in the gymnastics dish/hollow position. Gymnasts always tuck in the abs on holding this position; however I found i very restrictive and was unable to breath easily or hold for any great length. I tested with the same pattern I have used for squatting (loaded) and boom instant strength; and a release in my tight SI joint.

I like the poster with the DNS poses - are they published text or video somewhere so one can incorporate them into a daily practice. They appear similar to Todd Hargreaves (better movement blog) feldenkrais audio programmes, which essentially cover a lot the same early months motor patterning.

### Da-mo — 2015-01-22T11:49:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153586>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=153534)
>
> Applying this to horse riding….a rider and horse can meet a workable compromise and learn to move together on a straight line, but it wouldn't mean either one was in a functionally ideal pattern….

It fits in with the stuff written here. Dr Deb. Bennett is one of my favourites on this topic. From studying her discussions - it seems the basis of a good seat is the ilio-psoas muscles. The seat starts to deteriorate once external muscles start getting recruited.
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Also, from my limited understanding, the iliopsoas can either work beneficially or destructively depending on what the other muscles around it are doing. i.e they can flex the lumbars either way. So if training these muscles you need to be very careful.

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=153534)
>
> Something I've been learning the past few years is how differently a horse moves when their thought initiates a movement…vs mechanical aids which create compliance. The elusive "softness" that is visible in muscles (no matter how hard they are working) just doesn't happen with most horse/human interactions. And I guess I should add that it needs to be a thought within the realm of feeling safe…otherwise we're moving into the sympathetic nervous system areas of fight flight freeze and collapse.

The best horseman I've seen don't use aids as such - they think what they want and the horse picks up on that and does it - I'm pretty sure this is one of the closest things to entanglement I've witnessed. It could probably be described as riding the inside of the horse rather than the outside. Tom Dorrance used to refer to those that approached horses from the outside as "surface workers".

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=153534)
>
> All this to say that I think I've become pretty good at moving badly both on the ground and on horseback

Horse/rider pairs can feed into each other's crookedness. My daughter and her horse are crooked the same way so if not mindful can spiral each other into further crookedness. I'm crooked the opposite way so when I ride her horse it straightens us both out.

It could probably be described as riding the inside of the horse rather than the outside. Tom Dorrance used to refer to those that approached horses from the outside as "surface workers".
>

> Horse/rider pairs can feed into each other's crookedness. My daughter and her horse are crooked the same way so if not mindful can spiral each other into further crookedness. I'm crooked the opposite way so when I ride her horse it straightens us both out.

Great image! Thanks. I know this is a key muscle and I was instructed very badly for years on it's use.

I love Dr. Deb's work too….she's got some pictures of my Canadians…I hope they show up in her latest articles about the origin of different horse breeds.

The guy I've been working with for the past three years, Harry Whitney worked with Tom Dorrance…blows up most of everything you might know from traditional education doesn't it? Tom once said of Harry…"Harry rides the whole horse." and I think that is just what you are talking about. It can't really be separated into parts, but it is always amazing to watch the transformations that happen and the changes in the quality of movement and expression when a horse is reached from the inside out.

I've been reading the articles Josh posted and proper breathing would also be a really good place to start….after years of early instruction about "postures," I've got some bad breathing patterns…I would imagine Martial arts would have been a better path for you regarding breathing and proper core stability.

### Linz — 2015-01-22T15:05:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153594>

Seahorse/Da-mo,

My education has been blown up too!

I am working with several horses , all with a strong block in that thought interaction which only became obvious when they started ridden work. I discovered that they have all had to compromise for early (and not obvious) injuries. In general trainers suggest manipulation to produce the picture they want (submission) and vets will 'suppress' the injured area (or whole horse) in a variety of ways but no proper solutions. Big changes with cranio-sacral work and red light (thank you Sarah). Josh, I don't want to de rail the topic (my down under muscles and rider straightness need help too) but those core stabilisation pictures really make me want to know how a flight animal - that can run fast a few hours after birth and doesn't spend months lying on its back and front - builds its stabilising neural connections after spending months folded up in utero. And how to unwind and re-connect.

Da-mo, I am straight on the straight horse and crooked on the crooked horse. If I try to sit straight on the crooked one I'm back to the loss of thought connection as my attempted straightness manipulates the horse. How to correct the core from within?

### Da-mo — 2015-01-22T16:35:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153602>

Linz, work on straightening the horse. The horse is probably breaking the connection because your straight position makes it more difficult for it to go incorrectly. One idea would be to find which diagonal the horse prefers you to post on at the trot and then work on building strength on the diagonal less preferred. A gentler way would be untracking the horse whether mounted or on line - shoulder-in/lateral movement at a walk. One step at a time. (see Dr. Deb's website for info on untracking).

Seahorse - I'm jealous of you working with HW. For breathing . . . I watched Mark Rashid through 2 clinics over 4 days and every session began with rider breathing. He could tell how a rider was breathing by watching and listening to the horse. He has an Aikido background. Basically you want to breath with your diaphragm (like a baby does) instead of your chest (panic breathing like when you get awoken by the alarm clock) and this also helps create the IAP they've been talking about in this thread - your diaphragm breathing connects to your pelvic floor . . . . . . etc.

### SeaHorse — 2015-01-22T17:27:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153606>

Yes, feeling very lucky to work with Harry…I've seen MR's clinics too…the breathing is so important. But misinformation abounds. I've been aware of and working on breathing for years and my idea of diaphragm breathing and what I was taught was what people call "belly breathing" and it isn't correct…reading the Han's article Josh posted made that very clear…Followed Mary Wanlass and her work messed things up big time for me…."bearing down" instead of more fluid IAP ....elevating upper chest instead of having the diaphragm on an even plane…it goes on and on LOL….anyway, when you know better, you do better and I was practicing IAP while I chopped ice this morning.

And to link back in with DNS and developmental stages, I know I never really crawled, just scooted around and then got up and walked early…I feel I must start there to get to the fundamentals again.

I was just shown an exercise to isolate the psoas….it feels like it might be Josh approved, but I'd be interested in finding out

You lie on your back with legs straight and then try to bring your heels in towards your butt without engaging your abs. The only way I could do this at first was to think of raising my knees and do one leg at a time….anyway it's suppose to isolate psoas….comments??

### Linz — 2015-01-22T18:59:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153612>

Thanks Da-mo, and yes Dr Debs one step at a time has helped progress already. The real problem is unravelling the underlying bad patterns due to injury. While these remain, most 'normal' exercises induce further compensation with many of those grooves that Josh discusses appearing. Dr Deb's 'birdy' in that document is interesting and very relevant to the horse just beginning to pull forward and rush past danger rather than shrinking back and spinning away

### JoeBranca — 2015-01-22T21:06:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153621>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=153562)
>
> i have done erwans movnat course and i can say they really dont understand breathing-stability. i tried several times to get them to investigate what is innate in this realm and they didnt seem at all interested in that kind of exploration.

can you expand on this? in my own movnat training i really began to understand the proper yoking of conscious breathing techniques to the movement pattern at hand - understanding the TVA's role, coupling the inhalation with abduction movements to ensure appropriate extension and stability, and exhalation tracking with flexion of the body, etc. if this is just incomplete or contrary to what is innate, i'd definitely want to explore that more.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-23T00:30:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153632>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=153621)
>
> can you expand on this? in my own movnat training i really began to understand the proper yoking of conscious breathing techniques to the movement pattern at hand - understanding the TVA's role, coupling the inhalation with abduction movements to ensure appropriate extension and stability, and exhalation tracking with flexion of the body, etc. if this is just incomplete or contrary to what is innate, i'd definitely want to explore that more.

they misundertand TVA, because they talk about concentrically activating it, rather than the eccentric and then isometric role of TVA in maintaining IAP as it is directed form the diaphragm
the diaphragm has a dual role of breath and stability
ie it is able to "pucker" to maintain stability whilst exhaling
in this regard it is not correct to say that exhalation is coupled to flexion etc, as one should be bale to breathe in or out at will so long as the diaphragm is still maintaining its stabilizing role

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=153606)
>
> Yes, feeling very lucky to work with Harry…I've seen MR's clinics too…the breathing is so important. But misinformation abounds. I've been aware of and working on breathing for years and my idea of diaphragm breathing and what I was taught was what people call "belly breathing" and it isn't correct…reading the Han's article Josh posted made that very clear…Followed Mary Wanlass and her work messed things up big time for me…."bearing down" instead of more fluid IAP ....elevating upper chest instead of having the diaphragm on an even plane…it goes on and on LOL….anyway, when you know better, you do better and I was practicing IAP while I chopped ice this morning.
>
> And to link back in with DNS and developmental stages, I know I never really crawled, just scooted around and then got up and walked early…I feel I must start there to get to the fundamentals again.
>
> I was just shown an exercise to isolate the psoas….it feels like it might be Josh approved, but I'd be interested in finding out
>
> You lie on your back with legs straight and then try to bring your heels in towards your butt without engaging your abs. The only way I could do this at first was to think of raising my knees and do one leg at a time….anyway it's suppose to isolate psoas….comments??

why on earth one would want to isolate the psoas is beyond me, it simply does not function in isolation.
my wife skipped the crawling stage and her gait somewhat still displays that of a toddler when you know what youre looking at...
she finds it difficult now to do the "wheelbarrow" test as her CNS simply cannot compute what to do with the arms
it usually results in some internal rotation at the hips when one misses this stage

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-23T00:32:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-153633>

i think it is safe to say that the more hyperspecialized one becomes to a particular disclipline (ie sport or movement pattern) the further one gets from functional ideal
this is where my coleagues and i are having great success with sports people, in re-training functional ideal within their discipline, and outside of it so they have a "center" to return to

mark is a consultant at hawthorn FC who are the current premiers 2 years running

### SeaHorse — 2015-01-23T00:51:32+0200

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=154164)

fwiw the archery accuracy is impressive but his locomotive and jumping techniques do not look stable and safe to me, he really should bring those skills up to par with the archery itself to avoid injury.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-30T00:00:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-154224>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=154220)
>
> fwiw the archery accuracy is impressive but his locomotive and jumping techniques do not look stable and safe to me, he really should bring those skills up to par with the archery itself to avoid injury.

Great point to bring up!

I actually thought that the movement of his feet and in general seemed very awkward. It just didn't look dynamic.

But while I was reading Pavel Kolar's 'Clinical Rehab Textbook', I was thinking that maybe the "awkward" movement displayed in the video isn't actually bad.

In this context, I think that posture has to be taken into account given the fact that the archer is holding and possibly trying to fire arrows from a bow. I think "good posture" and "safe movement" in this case means being able to:

-   Get into the stable static positions needed to fire that bow
-   Maintain those positions through the needed joints (like the shoulder, upper spine, etc ...) to keep the stable static position despite external forces
-   Have the rest of the body (like the legs) react to movement requirements in a way that do not compromise the stable position of bow firing

Of all of his movements, I actually think that he's pretty good at maintaining stability throughout the body. I like looking at this example, because the bow gives a nice reference point for how the rest of the body is aligned at a particular moment in time. Most of the time, I feel like I'm looking at stable spine, head, and shoulder position.

I think his movements feel awkward because he's taking very small steps to decrease the required force needed to maintain the stable bow firing position with each step. Each step lacks a lot of force, and I also think lacks a lot of movement efficiency, meaning that the way he's stepping isn't optimal for getting from point A to point B, but is designed again for keeping the stable bow firing position.

I think he could do the landing (from jumps) better, in that he's seems to be keeping a rigid spine in effort to stabilise the bow firing position, and therefore transferring too much load burden to the knees and ankles during landing. Again, I think that's motivated by trying to keep the stable bow firing position at all costs, which may not be the right strategy all the time.

\----

I could be wrong about all of this BTW, since I'm getting my head all confused by Pavel's textbook . I'll probably revisit this after more discussion and information. I can also rely on @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) can also tell me that I'm wrong

I need to get a bow and actually try some of this shit out though . Experiencing the bow firing position feels like yet another way to understand how movement works (and therefore how to do it more safely and more efficiently)

...

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-30T11:17:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12621/post-154258>

Great thread,

speaking of hyperspecialization and unnatural movements, some high level skateboarders have some of the poorest posture and motor patterns I've ever seen in "athletes", but the ones that last long and push it to new levels are not the hunchback stoner crack babies from NYC, but guys like Brandon Westgate who works ina cranberry bog and builds shit with his hands for fun.

As far as skateboarders go, he exhibits some of the most amazing strength, power, and mechanical proficiency with no wasted energy into "style" like so many skaters nowadays. He takes some of the landings on smaller drops rather rigidly, but Brandon really seems to have amazing neuromusclular control and decent mechanics. What ya think, Josh (Paleo Osteo)?

# Thread: "down under" muscles
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12622/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2015-01-15T10:28:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-under-muscles.12622/post-152831>

I have been doing a lot of reading about our pelvic floor muscles and their huge importance in our everyday life. I have really been taken them for granted .....so much I hadn't thought about.
These "down under" muscles are un recognized, multi tasking achievers, thanklessly coordinating various tasks every day of our life.
We all know they help maintain continence, and support pelvic organs etc. but I never thought about them protecting the spine and contributing to sexual sensation.

I am shocked by how many women have horrible issues with pain etc that Doctors know very little about.....and women are often told it is all in their head.

How are your muscles down under?
Are you able to recognize the correct muscles?

I almost always hold my waist in - life long habit ....and that is working the wrong muscles and not letting the pelvic floor do its job......

# Thread: Coffee pods and capsules .....danger lurking?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-pods-and-capsules-danger-lurking.12629/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2015-01-16T06:01:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-pods-and-capsules-danger-lurking.12629/post-152974>

Does anyone have any thought on pods or capsules?

I was skyping wth Josh the other day and kinda had a lite bulb moment or two. Josh knows that I drink a lot of coffee ...and I use capsules. My lips were a little swollen and a bit numb ...and it isn't from too much kissing .....I don't think ...lol

You might wonder why I don't pay attention to this ....but since I crashed and burned in the street a year ago and damaged some nerves in the left side of my mouth - I am always a little numb on that side anyway

What are the ramifications .... if any??? The ones I use are plastic. I used to use Nespresso which are aluminum.

Josh was talking to me about metal toxicity ..... but plastic is probably much worse - even if it is BPA free. there is something about synthetic estrogen....
And even if it is a metal capsule - there are plastic parts in the machine. Apparently an espresso capsule is punctured 28 times ...so what would be leaching into the coffee?

I suppose to use a capsule everyone once in awhile wouldn't be a problem ....but here in Australia - sales of these things are thru the roof ....

As of yesterday I stopped using them - they are way too expensive anyway....

Any thoughts?

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-16T23:08:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serum-ige-flagged-as-high-antigen-as-of-yet-unknown.12633/post-153063>

Earlier I was at a pre-BHRT gyn appointment and my doctor had my health records up on her screen to show me something. I noticed my allergy RAST blood test results were in (but not yet available on my own online health portal). She was kind enough to go over them with me. Unfortunately, my GP did not specify they do the FOOD allergy RAST, so they only tested danders, mites/roaches, penicillin, and pollens.

\*sigh\*

I was not allergic to any of those BUT my serum IgE was flagged high nonetheless at 142 (reference range: < 115 IU/mL).

I have known for awhile that I am allergic to shellfish because I had a terrible reaction to clams once and had to go to the hospital when my throat closed and my body rejected the food in every way it knew how (\*eek\*).. but I do not know specifically what kind, or what other seafood is included in this allergy. But because it causes anaphylaxis, I need to know precisely before I begin trying to find sources of DHA to add into my diet.

Anaphylaxis is bad enough but I am also allergic to benadryl, so cannot use that in case of a problem, and we live half an hour from the nearest hospital. When calling 911 years ago for my husband, we learned that too has about a 10-15 minute wait before response by volunteer EMTs who I would rather not have do an emergency tracheotomy on me...

Anyway, you can see why it is imperative that I find out exactly what I'm allergic to WITHOUT doing a food challenge or even a skin test, because I have such bad reactions to whatever shellfish it is that I react to. (I am seeing my doctor about an epi pen on the 22nd so that I will at least have SOME sort of emergency response option.)

**SO, for anyone who stuck with me this long to see what my question actually is, here you go:

Does the presence of serum IgE mean that I was recently exposed to whichever antigen my body is reacting to?

OR

will it ALWAYS be present now regardless of whether recently exposed because my body is now primed and ready with little waiting soldiers regardless of the presence of the antigen?**

I know I did not eat any shellfish or seafood prior to my test (which was done after 12 hours of fasting because I also had a lipid test that morning).

So now I am wondering if this means I must also have some OTHER food allergy..?

Many thanks to any who can help me to figure this one out, seeing as how I do not currently have an open allergy tests going on and I don't know any more now than I did before this particular bout of testing, yeesh.. LOL!

# Thread: Calorie Curiosity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calorie-curiosity.12640/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Haley — 2015-01-17T23:43:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calorie-curiosity.12640/post-153193>

I know we all know calories are not the end all- be all in losing weight or becoming optimal. But I can't help my curiosity with them. Im wondering if any of you intellectuals could break it down for me.

Why is it that when I eat carbs my body wants less calories?
And when I shoot for ketosis i become ravenous? And usually am putting out less energy (because of lethargy).

I understand there is a deep shift happening in my body and there are several mechanisms taking place. But I would like to hear the scientific reasons as to why carbs are fueling me more "efficiently" (even if it is a superficial efficiency).

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-18T05:23:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calorie-curiosity.12640/post-153220>

IMO, calories give you rough information for "amount of food" consumed, and tell you nothing about how the contained energy (light) is dealt with in the body

Increased hunger on a high-fat diet likely means that complex II activity is broken to some degree -- ie: you cannot use fat for fuel in the mitochondria. The reason for this was covered in EMF4 ([http://jackkruse.com/emf-4-why-might-you-need-carbs-for-performance/](http://jackkruse.com/emf-4-why-might-you-need-carbs-for-performance/)), but basically, nn-EMF can cause a redox shift that forces you to rely on Complex I for energy. Carbs go straight to complex I, and can be thought of as "fast energy" that burns bright and fizzles out pretty quick .

Your location is the most likely reason for this redox shift happening. Being at elevation (and yours is above 2000m) forces the SCN to speed up, and if it can't, major circadian mismatches will occur. This was covered in the January Webinar (my notes here: [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/jk-webinar-jan-2015](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/jk-webinar-jan-2015) and I give some speculative thoughts here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/page-3#post-152928](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/page-3#post-152928))

Here's a question, do you see this issue more so in the winter time or summer time?

\----

EDIT: fixed "can be fat" to "cannot burn fat"

### Haley — 2015-01-18T05:47:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calorie-curiosity.12640/post-153224>

Yewwei.tan-thanks for your input!

Yes I am at about 7000ft in elevation since June (coming from Houston) and noticed an immediate decrease in hunger when I moved up here. That is when I decreased my intake back to roughly 1300 cals /day..and that just kind of happened.

Do you mean that if my complex II isn't working my mitochondria canNOT burn fat for fuel?

I will read those blogs shortly when I get to my laptop. Do you know how I can access the webinar a by the way?

And unfortunately I don't think I can answer your question accurately because the only other time I truly tried ketosis was in winter as well. But I will note that after doing so is when I stared binging again after not doing so for 3 years. And in no way am I discrediting the fact that when I have been in a binge phase carbs absolutely do make me hungrier, too.

I guess part of me is trying to also understand the general cycle I have been in for the last 7 years with restriction/binging.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-18T06:17:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calorie-curiosity.12640/post-153226>

> [Haley said:](/goto/post?id=153224)
>
> Yewwei.tan-thanks for your input!
>
> Yes I am at about 7000ft in elevation since June (coming from Houston) and noticed an immediate decrease in hunger when I moved up here. That is when I decreased my intake back to roughly 1300 cals /day..and that just kind of happened.
>
> Do you mean that if my complex II isn't working my mitochondria canNOT burn fat for fuel?
>
> I will read those blogs shortly when I get to my laptop. Do you know how I can access the webinar a by the way?
>
> And unfortunately I don't think I can answer your question accurately because the only other time I truly tried ketosis was in winter as well. But I will note that after doing so is when I stared binging again after not doing so for 3 years. And in no way am I discrediting the fact that when I have been in a binge phase carbs absolutely do make me hungrier, too.
>
> I guess part of me is trying to also understand the general cycle I have been in for the last 7 years with restriction/binging.

Yup, mistake on my part , meant to say that the mitochondria cannot use fat.

You need to be a member to access webinars unfortunately

The binge eating on carbs is a sign of issues with reward pathways in the brain (they're not getting enough electrons). Ideally, you'll want to be ketotic in winter time where you're at (and possibly even through spring and autumn), but I think a transition plan is needed. I'll have to follow your log to comment further

### Haley — 2015-01-18T06:41:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calorie-curiosity.12640/post-153227>

Thank you yewwei.tan. My hope is I can pull through and not get intense enough cravings that it drives me over the edge. I do need a transition plan

Would appreciate any of your input as I have followed some of your posts & feel I could learn a lot from you!

# Thread: Sore Toes & Numbness in Hands

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dantothep — 2015-01-21T19:55:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-toes-numbness-in-hands.12661/post-153543>

I know this has been covered here and a few other places - [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/raynaud-thread.4235/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/raynaud-thread.4235/)

I am three weeks into CT:

week 1 mostly face dunks for 10 mins or so.
week 2 was CT over tight stretchy top (not compression vest though)
week 3 is ice cubs in bags on torso (25 mins) + face dunks AM.

Throughout the weeks I have experienced sore toes, red swollen, throbbing sometimes itchy. Seems like mild frostbite not Reynaulds as I haven't noticed any white patches. I am now getting intermittent numbness in the hands.

Thing is my feet haven't been near the cold. And I am definitely still averse to the cold.

My SHBG was very high last week, could this be related to high levels of Estrogen which is being naturally released as a Ca channel blocker due to my high EMF environment?

# Thread: Effectiveness of Laser treatment at acupuncture sites compared to traditional acupuncture in the tre
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effectiveness-of-laser-treatment-at-acupuncture-sites-compared-to-traditional-acupuncture-in-the-tre.12669/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2015-01-23T01:02:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effectiveness-of-laser-treatment-at-acupuncture-sites-compared-to-traditional-acupuncture-in-the-tre.12669/post-153639>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21095914](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21095914)

Cardiovascular diseases are the main causes of mortality, not only in Brazil, but around the world. The use of acupuncture as a complementary and alternative treatment for cardiovascular diseases has been suggested for animals as well as human beings. Possible advantages in using acupuncture are the low cost of treatment and low risks of collateral damage from a combination of acupuncture with other medical treatments. The purpose of this study is to assess the effect of traditional acupuncture with needles and laser acupuncture on arterial pressure and peripheral circulation of inferior limbs in patients with circulatory deficiency. Ten acupuncture points were stimulated in 40 individuals, being that 20 were stimulated by Aluminum gallium arsenide (AlGaAs) laser with emission in the red spectral region (650 nm) using an energy density of 2,4 J/cm2, and 20 were stimulated by systemic acupuncture needles. The analysis of peripheral circulation was performed based on the measurement of arterial pressure of the ankle and arms, using sphygmomanometer and Doppler equipment. The variance of the revascularization index of the laser acupuncture group was 0,057, and 0.030 for the needles group with p= 0,006. These results show that only those treated with laser acupuncture exhibit a significant increase in systolic pressure of their lower limbs, with a consequent improvement of the Revascularization Index, suggesting that different stimuli on acupuncture points generate different variations of peripheral resistance of lower limbs. is column.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dantothep — 2015-01-26T15:06:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-a-self-referral-lab-understanding-gp-physio-chiro.12696/post-153896>

Hi All in the UK,

Anyone found a kind Doctor/Physio/Chiro that will refer people? UK law means people cannot test themselves....

We have some good labs in the UK but they are all referral based e.g.

[http://www.biolab.co.uk/](http://www.biolab.co.uk/)
[https://www.gdx.net/uk/](https://www.gdx.net/uk/)

Edit: As I was writing I found these guys called them and they can offer some custom lab tests that aren't on their main site,

[https://www.medichecks.com/](https://www.medichecks.com/)

Will let you know what they say as I sent them this list of labs to see if they can offer it:

HS CRP
Vitamin D 25-hyroxel
Thyroid panel (TSH, Free T4, Free T3, reverse T3)
Serum Potassium
Timed cortisol and melatonin
DHEA-S
Progesterone
Pregnenolone
highly sensitive Estradiol
Intracellular Glutathanone
BUN-Creatine
Serum Iron, TIBC, Serum ferritin, Iron Sat
Lipid analysis panel (full FA breakdown. Similar to VAP or NMR)
HomoCysteine

Dan

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/has-anyone-experienced-numbness-and-edema-in-their-lower-legs.12709/post-154017>

If so what did you do to alleviate it?

I was diagnosed with late stage lyme disease based on my symptoms and a CD-57 test of only 17. I have been taking a lot of herbs and essential oils so I think I have overwhelmed my kidneys/liver.

I am going to try a short water fast to alleviate the edema.

Any other ideas would be appreciated.

these muscles impact on so much ...like the spine

there is a thread in the cave ....the muscles down under - lots of great info from Paleo Josh and others....

### Lahelada — 2015-01-31T03:06:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-or-almost-none-emf-place.12749/post-154315>

Good question. I would think big parts of Africa. Much closer Jachal, san juan.
My partners son went there andnparents were advised not to expect any form of "modern" communication.
By the way I saw your post on my profile. After the weekend I will have a netbook again and will respond properly.

### Erik — 2015-01-31T17:26:10+0200

### Erik — 2015-02-01T20:25:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-or-almost-none-emf-place.12749/post-154467>

@[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) thank you, i will check it out

### Lahelada — 2015-02-01T22:10:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-or-almost-none-emf-place.12749/post-154482>

Putting it in general terms .
You need to look for non affluent places that are also strategically unimportant.
In other words a 3 hut settlement could still be a hotbed for EMF if it is near a major road that links commercial centres. A good example is the New York Chicago corridor. To get the required network resilience to lnk the two stock exchanges a lot of hotspots were created in otherwise commercially really uninteresting land. So I hear anyway.

### nonchalant — 2015-02-01T22:34:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-or-almost-none-emf-place.12749/post-154483>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=154482)
>
> Putting it in general terms .
> You need to look for non affluent places that are also strategically unimportant.
> In other words a 3 hut settlement could still be a hotbed for EMF if it is near a major road that links commercial centres. A good example is the New York Chicago corridor. To get the required network resilience to lnk the two stock exchanges a lot of hotspots were created in otherwise commercially really uninteresting land. So I hear anyway.

Exactly. You can look for a place with very low phone signal, very few towers, only to find that in a couple years, a huge tower is erected nearby. Probably low population would be some future protection.

# Thread: a simple genetics question about X, Y, and 23andme
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-simple-genetics-question-about-x-y-and-23andme.12763/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-31T16:26:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-simple-genetics-question-about-x-y-and-23andme.12763/post-154372>

I'm copying this from my blog this morning because I really want to understand this and would love some feedback please. My education in science is far behind where I want it, but please don't think this means I'm terribly dimwitted or anything!

\--------------------
\*sigh\* a friend just confused the crap out of me.

I was saying that i'm upset at 23andme because they don't tell you until after they receive your sample that women can't have their paternal line analyzed, they'll only get maternal line results.

This upsets me because all of the health issues I'm concerned about come from my father's side. So I said I would have to get my son to give his sample for me to get a picture of my own paternal genetics, because this is what video 5 of 5 on 23andme says for female members to do. (Edited to add: Perhaps they only mean that paternal ancestry can only be determined from a male, and are not referring to DNA/SNPs?)

And my friend said, why would that even matter for you, you don't get your father's Y chromosome anyway, you only get his X - cuz you're a female - so why would genetic issues related to his Y even be pertinent to you.

And then I said, &%(^)(&$(!! YOU HAVE CONFUSED ME.

and so here I am. Anyone able to explain this to me? I told him it matters because I got half my DNA from dad and half from mom and just because I have no Y I have nothing for them to test to reveal to me what that DNA from my dad contains, but it doens't mean it's not there. I just can't test it in myself, that's all.

Is this at all right?

Hi Guys,

So I've started my journey with Cold Thermogenesis and have a few questions. My story: I've been doing cold water facial immersion nightly before bed, and can handle 10+minute sessions and is not uncomfortable at this point. On top of this, I've been taking cold showers daily, starting out warm and finishing up around 3-5 minutes in the coldest setting. It's gotten easier every time and I focus the water on my face, back of neck, and chest.

Having said that, how should I transition into baths? -- Just start doing it once-twice a week?
Secondly, how do you all go about getting the amount of ice necessary to take cold baths?

# Thread: Viral loads
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viral-loads.12783/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2015-02-03T01:06:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viral-loads.12783/post-154564>

I have a hard time understanding how to keep a viral load in check. Redox seems an obvious answer but there have to be specifics to this particular shit storm I am just not understanding.

About 5 years ago my kids/family were sick from September through March...non stop bouts of strep, ear infections, colds etc. Constant antibiotics and nebulizer treatments.

In recent years we are almost never ill, I cant remember the last time anyone has taken an antibiotic. However, we seem to constantly fight these feelings of general malaise. Low energy is a chronic issue. Often fevers will appear as well either as nagging low grade or fast and furious higher temps.

We have tested positive for HHV6, CMV, EBV....and other random crap.

What am I missing in the battle to defeat these gnarly things? Is it simply a redox balance game to see who is control? If so, any tips for keeping things in our favor or fighting back when its not? Im just done with this and would like to move the hell on...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-03T01:44:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viral-loads.12783/post-154565>

Heheh, I was just reading a little bit about this topic yesterday , so I have thoughts brewing. But I think I'll have to leave it for later in my day to fully explain a bunch of mechanisms that lead me to think what I currently think.

Winning the "Redox Battle" is right, but that battle includes both the ability to modulate charge on the cell membranes to become either stronger or weaker in response to stimulus. Jack basically gave me a reply to a question on viral entry mechanics to say that high charge on the outer cell membrane can be a way to allow for viral entry --http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/is-it-the-vaccine-or-is-it-something-ancient.11972/#post-144945 . It's gonna get complicated in that regard

What I do know is that the key here is light emission. This study linked to by @[CindyB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5731/) contains basically all the details, and I intend to do a proper breakdown in the coming days -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...dy-for-you-comments.12507/page-10#post-153032](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-13-is-live-and-ready-for-you-comments.12507/page-10#post-153032)

The short answer though, is that monochromatic near infrared light in the 1020nm to 1190nm "de-activates" certain viruses. The viruses are then likely cleaned up through autophagic processes, which would more easily detect and target these viruses (due to different biophoton emissions when they are "de-activated").

I think that a well functioning set of mitochondria would be able to uncouple respiration as needed (requires higher delta-psi), and emit the necessary monochromatic IR light to cause this "viral deactivation".

Introduction of that IR light directly via something like IR lasers will have the same effect. Using un-coherent light will not work as well, but I suspect that it will still have a positive effect.

So what I currently think is the best treatment is to introduce as much of this near IR band of heat to the cells as possible, either as direct treatment using a laser, or by enhancing cellular uncoupling as much as possible.

If I were to come down with a virus:

-   CT with fournier effect. (Contrast Showers are my personal go-to). Both the hot and cold stimulate uncoupling.
-   2mg Nicotine + high dose Bitter Melon, both of which are known uncouplers

Any other herbal additions may also be helpful, depending on in which tissues the virus is located

Hopefully I'll get to more stuff later

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-03T02:08:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viral-loads.12783/post-154569>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=154566)

> nicotine gum??

Yeah, that's my preferred method for getting a quick dose directly into the blood. I chew vigorously (as opposed to the package instructions ), and that seems to get the effects more quickly.

Only precaution is to not get a gum with phenylalanine. I just use the nicorette 2mg pieces.

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=154568)
>
> so stomach flu

> high fat electron dense epipaleo diet, cold RO water
> fournier effect CT
> bittermelon or nicotine...

And go get a red-laser helmet as well

Bitter Melon can also apparent give some people hypoglycemia-like symptoms. I'm personally experienced some headiness when trying to dose bitter melon via naturally brewed soup/tea (buy bitter melon, boil it down in concentrate stock), but when I checked my blood glucose levels, it was normal enough (slightly less than 5mmol/L or 90mg/dL).

I don't get that reaction with crushed up supplemental bitter melon extract pills. Example, right now I'd crush up 4 of these pills -- [http://www.iherb.com/Himalaya-Herbal-Healthcare-Bitter-Melon-60-Caplets/3690](http://www.iherb.com/Himalaya-Herbal-Healthcare-Bitter-Melon-60-Caplets/3690)

From an traditional Chinese perspective, I've always been told while growing up that Bitter Melon (we call it Bitter Gourd) was supposed to ward away the flu. I'll eat it any time though  ....

### cantweight — 2015-02-03T03:49:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viral-loads.12783/post-154572>

@[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/)
Thank you so much for the input it is appreciated.

Bitter melon and nicotine make sense. Safe to assume they are ok for children? They are older 9, 12, 14 and all near or over 100lbs.

My knowledge of light is very limited. Right now I have the ct tank set up with an infrared light over it. It is a giant 250 watt clear heat lamp that one would use for a chicken coop....having bought an older home with a farm it was one of the things we found in the basement. We also have the very 70's red heat lamps in our bathrooms. Aaaaand we have 2 large grow light units for plants "full spectrum" tube lights. Are any of those what you are talking about????

They are also on high fat low carb dha rich diet...

Very safe assumption we are dealing with mitochondrially defunct humans but they have come a long long way from where they were.....arthritis, migraines, motor and vocal tics.....GONE

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-03T04:43:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viral-loads.12783/post-154576>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=154572)
>
> @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/)
> Thank you so much for the input it is appreciated.
>
> Bitter melon and nicotine make sense. Safe to assume they are ok for children? They are older 9, 12, 14 and all near or over 100lbs.
>
>
> My knowledge of light is very limited. Right now I have the ct tank set up with an infrared light over it. It is a giant 250 watt clear heat lamp that one would use for a chicken coop....having bought an older home with a farm it was one of the things we found in the basement. We also have the very 70's red heat lamps in our bathrooms. Aaaaand we have 2 large grow light units for plants "full spectrum" tube lights. Are any of those what you are talking about????
>
> They are also on high fat low carb dha rich diet...
>
> Very safe assumption we are dealing with mitochondrially defunct humans but they have come a long long way from where they were.....arthritis, migraines, motor and vocal tics.....GONE

Bitter Melon I would say is probably OK for kids. Well, at least I know that us Chinese feed the whole fruit to kids . The extract I linked above is presumably just dried, powdered, and compressed bitter melon fruit and stems, so it should be decently close to the real thing. Dosing will probably have to be adjusted accordingly, but I think this is a pretty natural remedy with not many side effects.

Obviously, test on yourself before attempting with kids.

\----

The nicotine I will advise against dosing to children if possible.

You will see very significant effects with 1mg of nicotine in adults. I think the uncoupling effects of nicotine will continue to be prevalent in any human, and nicotine works through specific receptors on adipose tissue.

The issue that I have with making recommendations to children is the addictive potential of nicotine. With adults, I can safely recommend up to 4mg of nicotine a day for up to 14 days without any addictive properties. Taking a break for 7 days after that will negate any addictive potential. I have no clue where the addiction threshold stands with kids.

Some adults even report feelings of dizziness with high dose nicotine. Personally, 2mg doesn't really give me any issues, but a 4mg dose gets me pretty high .

Some random notes regarding nicotine on John Kiefer's nicotine book -- [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/kiefer-nicotine](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/kiefer-nicotine) (there is a bias towards athletic performance in this book)

One thing that I will note in there is that nicotine can cause a transient triglyceride dump. This is fine so long as your body is great at using free-fatty acids. I wouldn't eat carbs together with nicotine though.

\----

I think the heat lamps are great so long as you can ensure that the nn-EMF risk is mitigated.

....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sad-news.12784/post-154577>

Very sad news from Alison Heath organiser of [http://www.takebackyourpower.net/](http://www.takebackyourpower.net/). Her websites are [http://longevityrescuer.com/](http://longevityrescuer.com/) and [https://www.yourvibranthealthsecrets.com/index.php](https://www.yourvibranthealthsecrets.com/index.php) and interview on her EMF Sensitivity with is [here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jAMgABUssGQ)

Dear Paul,

It is with deepest sorrow that I relay the loss of my beloved life partner.

He had become highly sensitive to electromagnetic radiation while working in
various locations across the country and felt he was being zapped whenever he
was exposed to WiFi. Together these past few months we worked diligently on
shielding our living space and had just ordered a BEMER.

He was regaining his strength by grounding barefoot on the beach, eating
superfoods drinking green juices and organic smoothies, rebounding, etc.

A complete examination of this heart several months ago including a 72 hour heart
monitoring indicated his heart was fine. However he suffered a cardiac arrest in the
presence of two paramedic teams and was without a pulse for nearly 30 minutes
before they were able to resuscitate him.

Within 24 hours it became apparent he had suffered a massive brain infarct and he
was declared brain dead.

A form of consciousness remained as his heart did beat stronger when he was
surrounded with loved ones in the hospital. I was able to sleep in his arms in the
intensive care unit on the last day of his life.

In death as in life, he was able to be of service to others donating his liver,
kidneys and pancreas to give the gift of life.

I will be taking some time off to recover from this shock.

The grand finale for the complete season of the Best of the Best of Your Vibrant
Health Secrets will air at a later date.

Those of you subscribed to the EMF summit can expect more to come. My
dedication to get this information on protecting ourselves has been renewed by this
tragic loss.

I will also keep the special price of $77 for the complete bundle of these
18 life-saving interviews available for a while longer. Please get informed and
share the information with others.

As always, sending you loads of love and great energy.
This time I request some of your love and healing energy back.

Alison Heath

host of the EMF summit and Your Vibrant Health Secrets

Your Vibrant Health Secrets

P O Box 20082
Kelowna, BC
V1Y 9H2
CA

[info@yourvibranthealthsecrets.com](mailto:info@yourvibranthealthsecrets.com)
250-769-8804

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-after-death-quantum-theory.12801/post-154685>

[Scientists Claim That Quantum Theory Proves Consciousness Moves To Another Universe At Death](http://higherperspective.com/2015/01/consciousness-death.html)

How Life and Consciousness Are the Keys to Understanding the Nature of the Universe
"In one universe, the body can be dead. And in another it continues to exist, absorbing consciousness which migrated into this universe. This means that a dead person while traveling through the same tunnel ends up not in hell or in heaven, but in a similar world he or she once inhabited, but this time alive. And so on, infinitely. It’s almost like a cosmic Russian doll afterlife effect."

[http://higherperspective.com/2015/01/consciousness-death.html#sQpqtmeO9xKmWcZD.99](http://higherperspective.com/2015/01/consciousness-death.html#sQpqtmeO9xKmWcZD.99)

.

### nonchalant — 2015-02-04T19:21:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-154732>

I think its about time for me to reread CT7 again, my most-read JK blog.
Because melatonin is a nightime-only hormone, and is the opposite of cortisol, in some respects, cortisol really has to be a daytime thing. And I think I remember that a cortisol peak is necessary for some other hormones, and probably other bodily/cellular processes.

So we can do it the JK way, get our AM sunshine, and peak that baby first thing in the morning, and reap the benefits of the cortisol spike then and throughout the day. Or we can do it the way we used to, avoiding sunshine but drinking plenty of coffee/soda/chocolate? Or perhaps we rely on stress at work to do it for us? Procrastinating until a looming deadline spikes cortisol and we get the satisfaction of a job completed (and perhaps the creation of more hormones?) How about getting angry with someone? Surely that would do the trick. Maybe I understand bar fights better now. ;-)

If we don't peak cortisol somehow, it will continue to rob energy from our collagen until it gets enough to peak itself. Just before noon, iirc. Well, maybe for someone with enough energy stored. What about the rest of us? Does cortisol not get enough energy to peak by itself? Do we need malicious gossip instead? Road rage maybe? Do we need to be a troll to get our fix? Someone out there, somewhere, is WRONG on the internet!

Maybe that's why outdoor occupations (farming, fishing, construction) have some of the highest ratings for satisfaction. They get their fix naturally, and don't need to get in someone's face to feel better.

has anyone used one?

### nicld — 2015-02-08T20:17:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-155054>

^^^^ Good question. I have a light box and during the week use it while getting ready for work and I have to leave the house just as it is starting to get light out. I have thought about getting a smaller one for when I am at work. As much as I would like to be more matched, I have to somewhat "conform" to modern life. On the weekends when it is too cold to sit outside for very long I use it after 8 am when it is light out.

I have - the one I bought was only sold via a Doctor's Office - not the cheapo units you find online. I did saliva adrenal ASI testing before using a light box & after using a light box. My melatonin levels were so high in the A.M. hours it was off the chart  after using the light box my cortisol & melatonin are right in line to normal. Where I live the sun doesn't even come up til around 9 am & then factor in rain/clouds ect - there may not be any bright sunlight until after 11 am. It's nearly impossible to extremely bright sunlight at 6 am.

### BTA — 2015-02-09T02:19:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-155089>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=155072)
>
> i am quite sure i have heard you talk on one of the Q and As about this...what i didnt take from that was at what time you used it, and what was the initial mismatch?

I use the light box the exact second I wake up - my eyes open and I've got the light box next to the bed. A bit rough to wake up to staring at it but it's worth it. I use it for 40 minutes. My "before use" melatonin level at 10 am was higher than my 10 pm level  I was shocked to see that. After about a month of daily use I retested my melatonin level. Now it was perfect. Just right in the am and raises at night like it should. I feel better & sleep like a rock. Best $100 I've ever spent.

### Sue-UK — 2015-02-10T12:50:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-155207>

I have in the past, but not for several years. It was OK in the short term for an acute situation, but not adaptable enough for me longer term, it was the same artificial "sun" every time I put it on. ### SeaHorse — 2015-02-10T13:44:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-155217>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=155207)

It was OK in the short term for an acute situation, but not adaptable enough for me longer term, it was the same artificial "sun" every time I put it on. I was signalling summer when it was winter, and got no gradual transitions like with natural daylight, I intuitively dropped it after a while.

I too, used to use a light box when we lived in Newfoundland….the morning light was helpful, but I used it as my desk light and then would put it on at other times of the day and evening….my fault for not realizing how powerful it was and it contributed to trashed sleep for a number of years. It seems to me that getting to point where you can adapt to your environment long term is ultimately the most healthy approach….so short term…"get out of Dodge" kind of use for SAD would make sense, but being able to thrive in the dark is what I'm aiming for…..and realizing I may not get there in this lifetime and will need to move to an environment that supports my condition better.

### Sue-UK — 2015-02-10T14:35:34+0200

I think it would be interesting to find out when a particular model of light box was "matched" to my environment, if it was used at say 6 a.m.. Assuming it was roughly matched at least once in the year for a few days, my thought experiment was that if I was then to repeat that every day, even only using it at the same time, what effect would that have on my perception of time passing, or how would the organ clocks see it .... A bit like the astronauts seeing too many sunsets and sunrises ..... ? Slopes off to get cold dark and boring .....

### SeaHorse — 2015-02-10T15:06:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-155221>

Also interesting to figure out a way to adjust light boxes individually….not only calibrated for different environments and seasons, but perhaps to specific needs…i.e. a person may need to titrate up to the level of light in their chosen environment at first so as not to get overloaded, and then as things improve the intensities could change….Set your longitude and latitude and date and away you go for the year….could you live in one latitude and be entrained to another? sounds a little creepy to me.

Of course the crazy thing about light boxes is that they are standing in for the sun to begin with and are a faint approximation of what all the sun does…No heat, no all over body photons, etc. etc….but I can see how a more subtle and refined box might be a good workaround for environments that have somethings to offer (maybe low emf) but not a lot of available sun like many maritime areas that are cloudy a lot of the year. Still, considering it is an electrical devise, the emf will always be an issue. Never felt comfortable with the headlight devises for this reason.

### Sue-UK — 2015-02-11T13:49:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.12815/post-155349>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=155221)
>
> Also interesting to figure out a way to adjust light boxes individually….not only calibrated for different environments and seasons, but perhaps to specific needs…i.e. a person may need to titrate up to the level of light in their chosen environment at first so as not to get overloaded, and then as things improve the intensities could change….Set your longitude and latitude and date and away you go for the year….could you live in one latitude and be entrained to another? sounds a little creepy to me.
>
> Of course the crazy thing about light boxes is that they are standing in for the sun to begin with and are a faint approximation of what all the sun does…No heat, no all over body photons, etc. etc….but I can see how a more subtle and refined box might be a good workaround for environments that have somethings to offer (maybe low emf) but not a lot of available sun like many maritime areas that are cloudy a lot of the year. Still, considering it is an electrical devise, the emf will always be an issue. Never felt comfortable with the headlight devises for this reason.

As I was heading into winter I would often use an infra red light to warm the bedroom while I was getting dressed after CT, but then didn't seem to need it. I re-read Tensegrity 2 Cortisol yesterday and reconnected some dots ....

I usually CT outside submerged when its thick cloud, just want to get it over with, not my favourite conditions, but this morning your post had me thinking ..... I stayed submerged part of the time, and then positioned myself floating on the top and cloud bathed . I could picture Sally's drawing with a layer of cloud obscuring the sun, but letting photons through, the thicker the cloud the longer it might take to get enough photons to reboot the system, also depending on the amount of skin exposed. It turned into a really good session.

If I couldn't get outside at all for long periods of time I might be tempted to create a "sun room" to use in the morning - use a full spectrum light box, (not a blue light device) with my UV facial tanner, and my infra red lamp to illuminate a room, (making different photons available in the room rather than looking at an artificial sun). I wouldn't need to be close to the source of EMF. Turn the devices on, step into the room nude and just relax, or perhaps do a few stretches to help retighten the tensegrity system .....

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=154826)
>
> I lived in Singapore, not Bali. I personally found outSE Asia is not my cup of coffee. I visited Bali and I personally did not like it much, Lombok felt wonderfull...

### Ben1983 — 2015-02-05T19:16:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bali.12829/post-154830>

I'm looking for somewhere healthy to live for a year without working so I can just focus on healing. Bali is very cheap and wouldn't cost me much to save up enough to cover all expenses for a year off work.....plus it's only a 5.5 hour plane ride from Sydney.

### kovita — 2015-02-05T20:37:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bali.12829/post-154844>

Bali is very developed from the touristic point of view, very busy, you have to ytravel considerably to find yourself in wilderness. The good point, it is hinduistic island and you would find many people like you there. In general people are very nice and friendly. I felt particulary hassled by the neverending traffic, if you are beach person, the beaches and the ocean felt very powerless to me. Lombok is considerably less developed, easy to be in the middle of nowhere, more malaria though (I got falcifarum while living in Africa and nothing recommendable). Lombok feels magic, sea and beaches powerful, the whole island has different energy. There is some tourism but nothing comparable to Bali. People are more careful, keeping distance, but it is nothing important if you stay longterm and have time to develop relationships. The risk of dengue fever may be similar for both places, luckily I have so far no experience with dengue, but being viral, could be bigger s...t than malaria (artesunat works pretty well for malaria). I would recommend you east Africa. It is a truly magic piece of the Earth. I lived with my husband in Selous GR for a year. Lost between the wilderness. I almost died of malaria, our house burnt down to ashes, my husband was in plane accident, some pretty impossible human bastards around...too many to count...but we made some really extraordinary and unexpected friends there and would always come back despite of the disasters we lived through. It was truly healing place and life. It felt like a 'home'. I never felt anything even slightly comparable anywhere in Asia, but it may be just me, so no valid point.

### Ben1983 — 2015-02-06T02:18:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bali.12829/post-154873>

Wow that sounds like a wild ride. Were you working wherever you went or just going on various long term holidays? I'd like to be near the beach and somewhere cheap enough that I could save up a years worth of expenses to not have to work. Which country is Selous in....I couldn't find it on google
Maps for some reason. I'm also considering Nicaragua and other countries in that area too.

### Ben1983 — 2015-02-06T02:34:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bali.12829/post-154876>

How early on after first noticing symptoms did you realise you had malaria?

### caroline — 2015-02-06T02:36:45+0200

# Thread: Transcranial Light Therapy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcranial-light-therapy.12845/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2015-02-07T19:29:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcranial-light-therapy.12845/post-154977>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4207317/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4207317/)

> Bright light treatment is effective for seasonal affective disorder (SAD), although the mechanisms of action are still unknown. We investigated whether transcranial bright light via the ear canals has an antidepressant effect in the treatment of SAD.
>
> **Methods**
> During the four-week study period, 89 patients (67 females; 22 males, aged 22-65, mean { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
> ±
>
> SD age: 43.2
>
> ±
>
> 10.9 years) suffering from SAD were randomized to receive a 12-min daily dose of photic energy of one of three intensities (1 lumen/0.72 mW/cm2; 4 lumens/2.881 mW/cm2; 9 lumens/6.482 mW/cm2) via the ear canals. The light was produced using light-emitting diodes. The severity of depressive symptoms was assessed with the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale – Seasonal Affective Disorder (SIGH-SAD), the Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale (HAMA), and the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI). Cognitive performance was measured by the Trail Making Test (TMT). The within-group and between-group changes in these variables throughout the study were analysed with a repeated measures analysis of variance (ANOVA), whereas gender differences at baseline within the light groups were analysed using Student’s t-tests.
>
> **Results**
> Patients in all three groups showed significant decreases in their BDI, HAMA, and SIGH-SAD scores. Response rates, i.e., an at least 50% decrease of symptoms as measured by the BDI, were 74%-79% in the three treatment groups. Corresponding variations for the SIGH-SAD and the HAMA were 35-45% and 47-62%, respectively. No intensity-based dose-response relationships in the improvement of anxiety and depressive symptoms or cognitive performance between treatment groups were observed. Approximately one in four patients experienced mild adverse effects, of which the most common were headache, insomnia, and nausea.
>
> **Conclusions**
> These results suggests that transcranial bright light treatment may have antidepressant and anxiolytic effect in SAD patients, as both self- and psychiatrist-rated depressive and anxiety symptoms decreased in all treatment groups. These improvements are comparable to findings of earlier bright light studies that used conventional devices. The lack of dose response may be due to a saturation effect above a certain light intensity threshold. Further studies on the effects of transcranial bright light with an adequate placebo condition are needed.

# Thread: Just noticed I lost 5+lbs in 4wks of making these 3 changes, which one did it?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-noticed-i-lost-5-lbs-in-4wks-of-making-these-3-changes-which-one-did-it.12848/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-08T20:18:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-noticed-i-lost-5-lbs-in-4wks-of-making-these-3-changes-which-one-did-it.12848/post-155056>

I never weigh myself, I judge by how my clothes fit usually, but they weigh me at the doctor so that's why I know that I was just over 130 (don't remember if it was 132 or 135 or what, but it wasn't more) about 3 weeks ago, on January 16. At that time I was about one week in to changing a couple of things in my daily routine (will list them in a minute).

Yesterday I weighed myself and was 127.8.

This morning I weighed myself and was 124.8.

The changes that I made about four weeks ago are:

1\. Stopped eating 2 slices of white bread with margarine spread plus one greek yogurt for breakfast daily. Replaced this with a steak each morning.

2\. Swapped out my sweet 'n' low for stevia.

3\. Swapped out 2-3 cans of Diet soda daily for 2-3 bottles of spring or R/O water, depending on the brand available.

4\. (Forgot about this one when I made the thread title, sorry!) - swapped out most of my pretzel snacking in the evening for raw, organic nuts and dried berries.

\*\* (I will actually add in one more item, just in case: I normally take 2 ibuprofen every morning for arthritis in my big toe which always bothers me more in the winter, which it is here now. About 2 weeks ago I stopped doing this to see if it would make any difference. In under a week my digestive issues normalized completely! Last night I took 2 because my toe was really hurting a lot and this morning it's been all gorked up again. So, for YEARS now I've been messing up my gut with ibuprofen!

That was because I didn't apply common sense to my situation, instead I went off asking doctors for advice.

If I had used common sense, I would've asked myself, "What do I do about a swollen toe when I drop a can of soup on it? I put ice on it. Why? Because cold reduces inflammation." Then, had I been sensible, I would've realized to apply this to my whole body however possible to combat system-wide inflammation (which I have issues with apparently). So, CT is just common sense for people with inflammation issues, but I went off looking for high-tech, high-dollar answers by consulting physicians for something I should've known already. So silly of me. )

Anyway, sorry to go so long, just wondering which of these things you all think is the main contributing factor to this unexpected weight loss?

Thanks!

(oh.. I just realized.. could it be that I'm actually having less inflammation and that in and of itself accounts for much of it? Does inflammation result in actual weight gain, or just larger size?)

### kovita — 2015-02-08T21:07:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-noticed-i-lost-5-lbs-in-4wks-of-making-these-3-changes-which-one-did-it.12848/post-155062>

all of the changes you made were steps in the right direction. I would bet the biggest being the protein for breakfast and avoiding late afternoon/evening diabetes by giving up on many carbs you used to ingest during this time of the day regulary.
