### nicld — 2015-03-16T21:56:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-socks-for-air-travel.13316/post-158691>

Why yes, yes we are. We are planning on going to Eco Termales Friday night after spending a night in Torteguero. Saturday we are going to go repelling down waterfalls then zip lining. Sunday we are probably going to do a chocolate plantation tour then hike the La Fortuna waterfall before heading to the Pacific side.

That is if the volcano that erupted stays quiet and does not shut down the San Jose Airport on the 1st so we can get into the country.

We are so looking forward to it and have not heard anything bad about Costa Rica.

### cinnamon — 2015-03-17T00:41:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-socks-for-air-travel.13316/post-158705>

I haven't been to the Pacific side, but spent a week near Puerto Viejo on the Caribbean side a few years back. It was very laid back and awesome!

There was a tour company that could give you transportation between different locations where you spent part of the day whitewater rafting on the Pacuare River. It was a great way to move from one spot to another and we used it to go from Puerto Viejo to La Fortuna. They picked us up early in the morning, took us to a base camp near the river for breakfast, drove us up the river and loaded us into rafts. We stopped midway for a picnic lunch. When we finished rafting, we were back at the base camp where we could collect all our stuff and get on the bus or van that was going to our next location. I found the rafting itself pretty scary at times (having never done it before) but it was overall a fun day!

You are going to love Costa Rica! Pura vida!

# Thread: Nikken magnetic mattress info
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nikken-magnetic-mattress-info.13319/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-03-16T07:24:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nikken-magnetic-mattress-info.13319/post-158639>

My wife and I have been sleeping on a Nikken "Ultra Kenko Pad" for years.
I guess if it really was that good, I wouldn't be here!
I know some people have been wondering if there is a cheaper alternative to the Magnetico, so I thought I would report on this one. (I don't have a magnetico - yet)
Anyway, I used a magnetometer to measure the gauss of the mattress.
The meter reads like this:

(+20, +10, 0, -10, -20) - with the needle in the middle at zero when there is no field present.

Over the magnets the needle maxed out at +20 gauss on most of them but some were a bit weak.
In between the magnets it went back down to 0 gauss. It did not do the reverse polarity thing that people have been talking about, only if I pushed the meter into the mattress harder did the polarity reverse but not by much at all, maybe -2 gauss max. But it did drop down to 0 gauss in between the magnets for sure. This mattress is also quite old - at least 10 years old.
Another thing: when I lifted the meter away from the magnets, the reading dropped dramatically - from over +20 gauss to 0 gauss within an inch, so not a lot of penetration with this mattress I suppose.
So there's some statistics for you guys..

# Thread: Fish for Thought
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2015-03-17T06:45:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/post-158741>

> A fish is not a fish: Patterns in fatty acid composition of aquatic food may have had implications for hominin evolution
>
> From c. 2 Ma (millions of years ago) onwards, hominin brain size and cognition increased in an unprecedented fashion. The exploitation of high-quality food resources, notably from aquatic ecosystems, may have been a facilitator or driver of this phenomenon. The aim of this study is to contribute to the ongoing debate on the possible role of aquatic resources in hominin evolution by providing a more detailed nutritional context. So far, the debate has focused on the relative importance of terrestrial versus aquatic resources while no distinction has been made between different types of aquatic resources. Here we show that Indian Ocean reef fish and eastern African lake fish yield on average similarly high amounts of eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), and arachidonic acid (AA). Hence a shift from exploiting tropical marine to freshwater ecosystems (or vice versa) would entail no material difference in dietary long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid (LC-PUFA) availability. However, a shift to marine ecosystems would likely mean a major increase in access to brain-selective micronutrients such as iodine. Fatty fish from marine temperate/cold waters yield twice as much DHA and four times as much EPA as tropical fish, demonstrating that a latitudinal shift in exploitation of African coastal ecosystems could constitute a significant difference in LC-PUFA availability with possible implications for brain development and functioning. We conclude that exploitation of aquatic food resources could have facilitated the initial moderate hominin brain increase as observed in fossils dated to c. 2 Ma, but not the exceptional brain increase in later stages of hominin evolution. We propose that the significant expansion in hominin brain size and cognition later on may have been aided by strong directional selecting forces such as runaway sexual selection of intelligence, and nutritionally supported by exploitation of high-quality food resources in stable and productive aquatic ecosystems.

[http://ac.els-cdn.com/S004724841400...t=1426561005\_c435cf24323f546d448df03de04e70e2](http://ac.els-cdn.com/S0047248414000864/1-s2.0-S0047248414000864-main.pdf?_tid=d439af44-cc50-11e4-89ff-00000aacb35f&acdnat=1426561005_c435cf24323f546d448df03de04e70e2)

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-17T07:59:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/post-158743>

Although I _firmly believe_ that DHA has adequate evidence behind its incredible role in human neurodevelopment/regeneration, I believe the rebuttal arguments cited within the article you posted are worth reading. I'm using libgen to obtain full-texts for what I can't find, but the authors of the rebuttal arguments offer adequate evidence that, although preformed LCP are needed, a coastal/shore based environment is not; they don't even touch upon the energetic cost of brain structure remodeling, either, in those rebuttals.

I've been meaning to ask around here - if a shore based diet --> human brain, how did we consume sufficient calories without reaching our protein ceiling? Even fatty fish are remarkably low in calories compared to foods such as eggs, bone marrow, bellies, brain, etc. And even if, say, a cold environment permitted for an increased amount of protein intake, cultures such as the inuit ran into health problems when n3 intake got too high (like 10% of total calories), so it appears as though there is a U shaped bell-curve with n3 fats anyhow.

(by the way, this isn't meant to ridicule, merely to question and learn more. I'm a supporter of ketosis+epi-paleo, though I follow a more Kwasniewskian ketosis approach myself).

### PaulG — 2015-03-17T08:16:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/post-158745>

Nice article, thanks Josh, shame they didn't look at southern ocean caught fish

This stood out for me (Below), how awesome is North Sea [Mackeral](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mackerel) at topping the EPA/DHA charts. Though NS Mackeral is off the [ethical fish to eat list](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/earth/wildlife/9818669/Why-mackerel-has-been-taken-off-the-ethical-fish-to-eat-list.html).

There are over 30 species of Mackeral. According to this [website](http://www.australiantropicalfoods.com/index.php/australian-seafood/spanish-mackerel/) Spanish Mackeral from tropical Australian waters has EPA of 75mg/100g and DHA level is 281mg/100g compared to North Sea Mackeral of EPA of 788mg/100g and DHA of 1107mg/100g. That's a **BIG difference**.

Levels of EPA/DHA are determined by species of Algae eaten and Algae require light to live and grow..Australian light has higher and longer periods of UVA/UVB which I guess affects EPA/DHA levels, plus temperature of water Coastal vs Deep/Colder fishing where Colder=denser=more electrons=higher rates of metabolism=more growth.

**It has been noticed that EPA and DHA have differential effects in the human body (notably the brain). For instance, EPA appears to be more effective than DHA in combating depression in modern humans ([Martins, 2009](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#bib53), [Sublette et al., 2011](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#bib87), [Martins et al., 2012](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#bib54) and [Russell and Bürgin-Maunder, 2012](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#bib76)).** It is not yet known how such a shift in DHA/EPA ratio and in absolute amounts of EPA in the hominin diet would play out in terms of brain growth and development, but it is a factor to be taken into account in future evaluations when the role of EPA in brain functioning has been clarified in more detail. For instance, if hominins would start to preferentially predate on fatty coldwater fish (or seal, whale, etc.) with EPA contents comparable with those in mackerel (_S. scombrus_; see [Fig. 4](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#fig4)), such coldwater aquatic exploitation would, compared with tropical aquatic exploitation, entail a five-fold increase in the amount of EPA in the diet (as well as a two-fold increase in DHA). Shifts of this magnitude are known to influence cognitive abilities and survival in seabirds. For instance, when climate change induced a diet shift for Kittiwakes (_Rissa brevirostris_) from fatty fish to lean fish, this led to impaired cognitive and visual abilities and decreased survival ( [Kitaysky et al., 2006](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#bib38)), which can potentially be explained by the four-fold decrease in EPA and DHA availability resulting from the diet shift ( [Huynh and Kitts, 2009](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248414000864#bib31)). Hence, a five-fold increase in dietary EPA availability, accompanied by a two-fold increase in DHA, can be expected to have substantial beneficial effects on hominin brain development, brain functioning, cognition and survival.

### PaulG — 2015-03-17T08:26:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/post-158746>

Algae Quantum phenomena
[http://www.iflscience.com/physics/schrodingers-algae-control-quantum-behavior](http://www.iflscience.com/physics/schrodingers-algae-control-quantum-behavior)

> It's not clear what the advantage of doing without the additional efficiency coherence supplies might be. Curmi told IFLS, "First, we do not yet know whether quantum coherence is actually relevant to biology. It is possible that this is an evolutionary spandrel - a property that is a by product of the evolution of some other trait. Assuming it is not an evolutionary spandrel, we do not know what the function of quantum coherence may be. People have guessed "increasing efficiency" but there is no hard evidence for this." This makes it even harder to know whether there is an evolutionary advantage to its disruption. Moreover, Curmi says the team have not yet studied the Hemiselmis cryptophytes enough to know whether they have lost the capacity of coherence entirely. "It is possible that rather than losing quantum coherence, Hemiselmis has gained the ability to switch it on and off," he says. "We're currently investigating this."
>
> Curmi also plans to study differences in the habitats and ecological niches between the species with and without coherence. Coherence is much easier to achieve at lower temperatures, so it would not be surprising if species that operate in warmer conditions couldn't make it work, but that does not explain an adaptation to disrupt it, if that is what he has found.
>
> The discovery of biological coherence sparked interest in the possibility of incorporating the phenomenon into organic solar cells. Knowing how, and maybe even why, some species block it could help in this application.

[http://www.phys.nthu.edu.tw/seminar/AMO/2010F/PhotosynthesisElecCoherence-small.pdf](http://www.phys.nthu.edu.tw/seminar/AMO/2010F/PhotosynthesisElecCoherence-small.pdf)

### Inger — 2015-03-17T10:40:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/post-158753>

I must be lucky to eat almost only North Sea seafood because it is where I live.. mackerel and herring........ and oysters

### PaulG — 2015-03-18T06:28:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-thought.13337/post-158840>

This may be interesting for Aussies...
[http://frdc.com.au/research/Documents/Final\_reports/1999-331-DLD.pdf](http://frdc.com.au/research/Documents/Final_reports/1999-331-DLD.pdf)

More DHA in Australian fish than Northern hemisphere
EPA/DHA levels not affected by heating

**Some info on New Zealand Green Lipped Mussels and Tasmanian Blue Mussels (p.148 onwards)**

> In any marine source with the presence of unsaturated fatty acids, particularly long chain omega-3 PUFA, there is the risk of deterioration of lipid during storage. Both studies showed with increased storage time and decrease in temperature up to 35?C, there was an increase in oxidation by measures of thiobarbituric reactive substances (TBARS), a decrease in total lipid, polar lipids and PUFA in total and polar lipids and an increase in non-polar lipids and FFA. This data suggests the activity of various lipases and lipolytic enzymes responsible for enzymatic hydrolysis. In the study by Jeong et al. (1990), after 12 months of frozen storage there was a decrease in PL leading to an accumulation of FFA causing an increase in neutral lipids. Our study reported the presence of significant proportion of FFA in both the NZGLM and TBM. Since these were not present in lipid extracts for mussels which were placed into extraction solvents within 10 minutes of harvesting, it is likely that the FFA represent hydrolytic degradation as reported by others (Jeong et al. (1990); Jeong (1999). Perhaps in our study lipid hydrolysis was occurring during storage, which could account for the high levels of FFA in both mussels

> Fatty acids Nearly half of the fatty acids in each mussel species were PUFA, and up to 42% of the fatty acids in both mussels were omega-3 PUFA, whilst approximately 5% were omega-6 PUFA. The predominant n-3 PUFA were 20:5n-3 and 22:6n-3. The NZ mollusc Mytilus canaliculus contains a similar fatty acid profile, dominated by the PUFA, 20:5n-3 and 22:6n-3 (Nichols et al. (1997). Many marine species of molluscs are rich in 20:5n-3, with lower levels of 22:5n-3 and 22:6n-3. **It has been reported that it is unusual for molluscs to contain high amounts of 22:6n-3 as mussels are unable to biosynthesise it from 20:5n-3 (Perry 1977). Other bivalvia species, such as Ostrea lutaria and Crassostrea gigas, have also been found to contain high levels of 20:5n-3 and 22:6n- 3, with the former being the predominant PUFA. In our study, there was a significantly higher amount of 22:6n-3 (14-20%), particularly in the TBM. In contrast to previous studies, the high proportion of 22:6n-3 in the NZGLM and TBM is probably largely reflecting the composition of the planktonic diet. Various algal species are known to contain elevated 22:6n-3, including dinoflagellates, cryptomonads and certain thraustochytrids as well as zooplankton (Volkman et al. 1989; Lewis et al. 1999)**. Mollusc fatty acid profiles usually contain about 30-40% SFA (Joseph 1982; Gordon 1982; Phleger et al. 2001), a level which was found in the present study. The SFA profiles were similar for both species and also to profiles for other molluscs. The male and female Subninella undulata contained 16:0 and 18:0 as the main SFA, similarly the gastropod Austrocochlea constricta and the gastropod Telescopic creeper, contained approximately one quarter of total fatty acids as 16:0 (Perry 1977). The presence of 28:8n-3, along with the C30 sterol dinosterol, indicates a strong dietary influence, in particular for the TBM. The TBM was collected during an algal bloom of the dinoflagellate Gymnodinium catenatum that occurs in southern Tasmanian waters. G. catenatum has been recently reported to contain 28:8n-3 and cholesterol, as a major sterol (Mansour et al. 1999). Van Pelt et al. (1999) reported the presence of 28:8n-3 in the dinoflagellate Crypthecodinium cohnii. This dietary link is further supported by the presence of 24-Methyl-5? -cholest-7en3? -ol in the TBM. This sterol is also found in G. catenatum (Hallegraeff et al. 1991). **The higher amounts of 28:8n-3 in the TBM than in the NZGLM, suggests a greater dietary dinoflagellate contribution with this species**.

> The anti-inflammatory properties of fish oils are thought to be due to 20:5n-3 and 22:6n-3 (James and Cleland 1997), **however Whitehouse et al. (1997) have reported that the anti-inflammatory activity in the NZGLM is associated with PUFA with 4-, 5-, and 6- double bonds**. In the present study the total proportion of PUFA with 4-, 5-, and 6- double bonds was 40% in the NZGLM and 43% in the TBM (p=0.92), indicating little difference between these PUFA between species. **Several individual PUFA other than the more commonly reported EPA, DHA and AA have attracted attention due to their perceived health (beneficial) properties, or because they are present as main constituents in only a limited number of species. For the NZGLM, considerable interest exists in the level of eicosatetraenoic acid (ETA, 20:4). Product derived from NZGLM is stated to contain unique ETAs (delta7,11,14,17; delta5,9,12,15; delta5,9,12,16; [http://www.aomega.com/ahs/l1116b.htm](http://www.aomega.com/ahs/l1116b.htm)). This report also stated that the marine lipid complex rich in ETAs from the New Zealand green-lipped mussel Perna canaliculus is the most potent of the omega-3 lipids for the prevention of pain associated with inflammation, including arthritis**. Separation of ETAs other than AA (20:4n-6) and 20:4n-3 can be achieved using capillary GC (unpublished data). Using highly sensitive chemical-ionization GC-MS (CI-GC-MS), we sought to determine whether other ETAs were present in the NZGLM and TBM. AA levels in the NZGLM were higher than in the TBM (mean %: 2.4% and 1.7% respectively). Levels of 20:4n-3 were similar in the two mussels. In contrast to other reports ([http://www.aomega.com/ahs/l1116b.htm](http://www.aomega.com/ahs/l1116b.htm)), we could not detect any other ETAs in either mussel. Our detection limits were estimated to be equivalent to between 0.1-0.2% of the total fatty acids. The reason(s) for this apparent absence of these novel fatty acids remains to be determined.

# Thread: Magnesium Oil

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2015-03-17T10:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.13343/post-158752>

I've been applying magnesium oil in high doses (40-50) sprays with great results concerning heart palpitations. I never really noticed these results before using oral magnesium for some reason.

Anyway I have felt more dehydrated since using this, however I cannot simply solve this by drinking more water due to stomach problems. I have gone from bowel movements of roughly 1 per day to 1 every 3 days, my tongue gets drier quicker and the skin particularly on my hands that has come into contact with the lotion gets really dry.

Is the left over chloride (???) from magnesium oil drawing water out of my skin causing dehydration and if so would making the oil with some kind of moisturizer stop this?
OR is it the magnesium in my body causing more water to bind to it, temporarily requiring more water until the stores are repleted?

Also I have been getting hives from this. Do they go away after long term use?

### Inger — 2015-03-17T10:49:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.13343/post-158754>

good questions Sean. I have gotten some dry skin on my feet too..... like it peels off? Looks better now... I have had to make some pedicure more often tho to keep my feet pretty.. weird!

I am guessing the second guess of yours could be it? Like more magnesium in your bod makes you need more water too somehow, as it binds to the water? I have read people have had increased thirst from using magnesium oil too.....
interesting........
so it is maybe an issue if you cant drink then? Maybe try to sip on water over day day, frequently, small amounts....

So good it helped with the heart palps.. heart palps are no fun

### seanb4 — 2015-03-17T11:54:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.13343/post-158757>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=158754)
>
> good questions Sean. I have gotten some dry skin on my feet too..... like it peels off? Looks better now... I have had to make some pedicure more often tho to keep my feet pretty.. weird!
>
> I am guessing the second guess of yours could be it? Like more magnesium in your bod makes you need more water too somehow, as it binds to the water? I have read people have had increased thirst from using magnesium oil too.....
> interesting........
> so it is maybe an issue if you cant drink then? Maybe try to sip on water over day day, frequently, small amounts....
>
> So good it helped with the heart palps.. heart palps are no fun

Yea thanks for recommending it. The palps are a lot lower now. Still heart fluttering and occasional palps but nowhere near as bad.

I hope it is the magnesium binding more water as then hopefully my dehydration will reduce once my stores have been replenished.

I've also stained clothes unknowingly with it. But I know that it is one of the best way to get magnesium into your cells so it is something that I need to do more of. When I do use it I seem to get the occasional energy surge which is great. I just wish it would last.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-12-20T22:11:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.13343/post-184644>

> [lilreddgirl said:](/goto/post?id=184633)
>
> I've also used it - Magnesium Oil - in the past and it burned, then after a week of use did not burn anymore.

Maybe a continual burning sensation is a sign of using up more magnesium faster than it can be replaced (?). If we evolved around the oceans being constantly exposed to salt water (tons of magnesium) then our skin and our lungs would have always had magnesium to draw from. This maybe why we have such a hard time absorbing it internally.
Like Jack said "our external environment must match our internal environment" (not his exact words).
So we would have had a low, consistent dose of magnesium and other minerals all day long from the water, air and the food.

### Erik — 2015-03-19T16:25:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/second-but-real-ct.13381/post-158955>

Well this is awkward, yesterday i did my second CT, but instead of staying 15 minutes, i took it to the extreme and stayed 1 hour. It was pretty cold water added with 3 frozen bottles of water. I managed to put a table over the Tube so i could be in the water reading, and i took my phisics book and my harry potter book, to read and be able to stay for a while. When i got out, i started feeling like amazing. I was cold so i went to bed and kept reading. And when in bed, my eyes started falling and suddenly i was amazingly tired, i mean tired for good (i wish this happened every night) And i fell asleep, (strange on me, i could never have a nap). Half hour later i woke up, and even it was difficult, i went to have dinner, just to not mess up with my circadian cycle.... Today i feel super strong

### nonchalant — 2015-03-19T18:25:01+0200

I've experienced them with CT and with sunshine. I think Jack said that the body sometimes just has to take advantage of a good thing by getting in some sleep right away.

# Thread: Drinking water
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ON MY WAY — 2015-03-19T18:37:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158969>

Hi Friends,

I have a tough time drinking water. I don't want it. It doesn't make me feel great. I have regular city water that I put through a re-structuring unit (Natural Action Technologies We- Wei) to drink. Even when I buy the Mountain Valley Spring Water in the green bottle, I don't want to drink it. As a result, I am getting maybe 2 cups of water per day. Not good at all. I'm on the diet, though (BAB!), not eating after dark, throw the circuit breaker in my house when I sleep, lots of feet on earth and at ocean edge, raw oysters for breaky, meditation, blue blocking glasses, etc (all of which I have adopted in the last month! lots of changes). But the water. I just can't do it. Kombucha, however, I can do!  I often get headaches and think it may be water related (but actually I think it's much more due to hormones).

I wake up and drink a glass right away- have to kind of force it.

I'm working on getting better water to my house obviously, but the water quality doesn't seem to be an issue.

Any tips for me? Shared experience? Best way to trigger thirst? (Nope, sex doesn't do it!)

I'd so appreciate.

Thanks!

### fitness@home — 2015-03-19T19:32:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158973>

I use one of these to flavor my water:

[http://www.freshpreservingstore.com...620875/?\_ga=1.266998925.2037497269.1402588533](http://www.freshpreservingstore.com/ball-wide-mouth-jar-infuser/shop/620875/?_ga=1.266998925.2037497269.1402588533)

My favorite drink is filling the infuser with frozen organic red respberries. It actually turns the water pink and the flavor is wonderful. The berries flavor the water all day, even with refills.

### ON MY WAY — 2015-03-19T19:50:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158974>

Thanks for the tip, fitness@home! Sounds wonderful. I like to flavor water as well- I squeeze fresh turmeric in the water and add fresh lemon and mint from the garden-

...but I have a sense that my body needs water, though, not flavored water, but the pure stuff, totally unadulterated, you know? It's weird to never get thirsty. It's not really that I don't like water. It's that I never want it. I want to want it! So I can gobble it up!

Thanks,
Freya

### fitness@home — 2015-03-19T20:03:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158975>

Trigger thirst.... hmmmm, smoke a doob? (Just kidding ).

### nonchalant — 2015-03-19T20:41:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158979>

Hi Freya, welcome! I think your lack of thirst may be a problem with your hypothalamus. I know when I started taking homeopathic hcg, which affects the hypothalamus, it triggered a big thirst in me. Have you tried the Leptin Reset? It forces the hypothalamus to reset it's circadian cycle, and that organ controls thirst, hunger, satiety, body temp, & sleep.

### Inger — 2015-03-19T20:51:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158981>

I have read for some people magnesium oil on the skin makes the thirsty.......
would not hurt to try?

### Lahelada — 2015-03-19T22:50:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-water.13383/post-158986>

I am like you. I don't get thirsty but once I drink I want more. Not all waters do it for me,bubbly ones are usually a hit,spring water I prefer to RO water anr filtered water is only good with ice cubes. Experiment a little. Treat yourself to a San Pellegrino or Perrier,even just once to test your reaction to it.

What labs does CT affect in the short term i.e. upto 4 hours post CT.

I did 20 mins CT and then went to see a private GP (not cheap in the UK). He whipped my blood out pretty quickly and then sent off to the lab, I didn't consider that the CT might affect the labs but recall somewhere that Thyroid and HSCRP can be affected, does this mean I will have to pay for them again for fear of a false negative?  (labs were pretty good apart from Vit D, and LDL - see below)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cts-effects-on-labs.13400/post-201069>

**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Erik — 2015-03-21T23:26:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-159149>

Today, i did CT with cooler water. I added a ton of ice and it was freezing, but 8 minutes after, i started shaking a lot so i couldnt read ( i read while on ct), like 8 more minutes happened and was shaking a lot more so i had to get out.

My question: With time, will the shaking go off?? because its unbearable, doing more CT will improve my shaking response???

### Da-mo — 2015-03-22T07:05:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-159158>

Not sure what the right answer is - but the way I look at it is that you dont want to push so hard that it causes a stress response - that is gonna fill your system with cortisol.

### Erik — 2015-03-22T16:11:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-159172>

OOh thankss, im gonna go slower

### nicld — 2015-03-22T18:10:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-159198>

Yes, you are going to fast. Get out when you start shaking as I think I read it is not good. You did deep CT and one needs to work up to that point. Can you do a warmer bath for a longer time?

I do deep CT but I have been at it for over 2 years and listen to my body. It is ok to be a bit uncomfortable but your gut will tell you when it is enough and you have to listen to that and not your brain wanting to do more (been there, done that).

### Kathy1967 — 2015-04-10T08:10:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-160778>

Hi Guys! I'm newish here and I was wondering about the shivering afterwards! I don't mind the bath at all - I could stay in 40 - 60 minutes but I get out by 30 becuse the shivering afterwards for a couple hours is so tough to handle! I suppose I'm doing deep CT - temp is in the 40's (fahrenheit). I started in Jan with short cold showers then started with the cold baths in Feb.

### nicld — 2015-04-10T21:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-160856>

Do warmer baths then. Colder is not always better. I assume you are putting ice in the water to get it that cold?

### shah78 — 2015-04-10T23:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-160859>

Agree with Nicole. Don't even think about sub 50 degree "workouts" until the hot days of Summer. Even then, ease into any deep ct experiments.

### Kathy1967 — 2015-04-11T01:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-160868>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=160856)
>
> Do warmer baths then. Colder is not always better. I assume you are putting ice in the water to get it that cold?

Thanks - I WAS putting ice in but after I bought a temperature gun & realized that my skin was getting below what Dr. Kruse recommended I stopped adding ice and now I just do the cold water - I'm not sure what the temp is; I haven't checked! ### nicld — 2015-04-11T20:49:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unstoppable-shaking.13415/post-160917>

### Kathy1967 — 2015-04-12T18:39:01+0300

# Thread: Essential Fatty Acids tests
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-fatty-acids-tests.13422/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LisaLearning — 2015-03-23T00:36:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-fatty-acids-tests.13422/post-159237>

My doc is in Brussels (Hertoghe) and ordered this test for me, which looks different to the ones most have posted, and I think the ranges are slightly different. I am wondering what I can learn from the results.

My DHA levels are within the limits prescribed on the test, but does this mean they are optimal? I know I don't want too much of some of these.

I am low in both dihomo gamma linolenic acid and oleic acid, which both have anti-inflammatory actions. Dr Hertoghe said to take some borage oil, which I now understand to help the DGLA level, but is there something one does to raise oleic acid?

### JanSz — 2015-03-23T02:50:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-fatty-acids-tests.13422/post-159243>

I posted my Fatty Acids Analysis here (bottom of page #1 of the thread):

[PG/E2 ratio??](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/#post-101886)

I re-typed it into spreadsheet that helps me see how far each of the Fatty acids is from mid range.

It is a way that dr Patricia Kane is looking at those analysis.

I posted examples from her analysis in post #27 there.
\-------------------
Many people are low in GLA.
I am low in GLA and DGLA.

There are three supplements that I came across that are recommended for low GLA.

Borage Oil
Evening Primrose Oil
Black Currant oil

From my readings:
Most commonly mentioned is Evening Primrose Oil (EPO).
Borage oil's GLA content is very high (good), but it is often mentioned that it contains other undesirable content.

Only once I came across information that Black Currant oil in addition to GLA also have some DGLA content.
That is why I dropped EPO and use Black Currant now.
Label on my Black Currant oil lists LA and GLA, it does not lists DGLA in its content.
///

Scan your test page and post it here.

////

### LisaLearning — 2015-03-23T09:36:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-fatty-acids-tests.13422/post-159267>

Hi Jan,

Thanks for your reply - I had seen your thread. I am going to have another look at it now, and will scan in my results when I return home later.

Ought we to be aiming for midrange on all these results? Is that the consensus? My DHA result is very, very slightly below midrange. I was expecting it was going to be very low, given all my EMF and blue light exposure (which I have since radically altered, but tests were taken before the alterations).

A few of the results were in range but high. I will have another look.

### JanSz — 2015-03-23T17:14:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-fatty-acids-tests.13422/post-159278>

There is lots of talk about DHA but I have newer seen any hard numbers.
Please shear if you come across something in that regard.
The only hard numbers I came across is from dr Patricia Kane.
She is using specialized laboratory that she calls best available and she uses expanded range of fatty acids. You can see what she looks at in the sample report that she have on her web site. (below)
The units that the laboratory uses are different from those I am getting now from LabCorp/Mayo Clinic. They are also different from units Genova Diagnostics is using. I did my testing there in the past.
**Let me know if you can figure how** dr Kane creates spreadsheet function to get results for "Indexes" (box on the bottom of the bottom page)

Going by analogy with steroid hormones, some hormones people like to have high within range. Dr Kruse specifically singled out progesterone and likes it very high.

I have no better way to look at fatty acids so I follow the mid range way.
I am also assuming that very high DHA could possibly please dr Kruse.
That is not so sure because that would likely raise a question of where it came from.
If from natural food then more likely ok, if from supplementation that would raise a flag. Right now the good flag is sn-2 position, but there may be other.
I do not know if the DHA that is tested in blood is always sn-2 or not.

Dr Kane is more direct, and dislikes highly the unbalance created by fish/krill oil supplementation. She points to the fact that excess of omega3 suppresses omega6, specially the AA.
Her husband Ed Kane wrote article where hi is highly against proliferation of indiscriminate use of fish/krill oils supplementation.
The special culprit usually is high excess of EPA that suppresses AA ( likely via suppressing GLA and DGLA first).
I am not sure what to really think about EPA.
I know that it is bad when it comes from supplements
but
the best salmon and other seafood have also EPA in excess. I have not seen dr Kruse addressing that topic.
To make decision more difficult, there is also lots of research available specifically glorifying use of EPA, likely paid for by fish/krill oil industry, but not all.

There is also concept being discussed by dr Kruse, that caught my eye recently.
We need excess DHA today, because we must renew what we are loosing excessively due to current environment.
Environment created by invention of electricity, so only 150 y old.

Add to that that dr Kruse is de-emphasizing influence of food and underlining importance of environment.

So if one goes back to time before electricity, need for new DHA was much less.
Wild eggs, beef and pork contain/contained much more DHA than bought today in supermarket.
It follows that need for seafood only 150 years ago was not that great either.
This line of thought may continue, many healthy people may have done fine without seafood.

# Thread: DNA Hair Allergy Testing ... a few questions and confusion - anyone else done this?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-hair-allergy-testing-a-few-questions-and-confusion-anyone-else-done-this.13443/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sandra — 2015-03-24T08:57:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-hair-allergy-testing-a-few-questions-and-confusion-anyone-else-done-this.13443/post-159344>

Hello

I have just had an intolerance/allergy test done with a hair sample. Some people totally disagree with this testing and others seem to see merit in it, and even have the results line up with 'standard' medical tests.

So, I thought I'd give it a go and see it I found any more answers. I already had a couple of suspicions concerning dairy, eggs, red wine and chocolate. I was pretty sure about gluten.

I ran a little experiment on myself over the past few weeks after meeting Yew. I kept a food diary and waited for the results to arrive. During this time I avoided eggs, diary, chocolate and gluten. I was amazed with how I felt - lots more energy and not tired, as well as having far less abdominal bloating, and even some weight loss (about 3-4 kgs out of 69kgs)

I received the results today. I must admit I didn't really do a lot of research into what food types would be tested but just went on the naturopath's advice that perhaps it would be worth getting done.

These are the items that came as having 'tested reactive for your immune system and must be avoided' ...

\- celery
\- olives
\- shallots
\- avocado
\- cucumber
\- onion
\- tomato
\- cashews

\- lavish bread (no surprise there - gluten)
\- white bread (no surprise there - gluten)
\- wholemeal bread (no surprise there - gluten)

\- Jatz biscuits (no surprise there - gluten)
\- milk arrowroot biscuits (no surprise there - gluten)
\- são biscuits (no surprise there - gluten)
\- select chocolate creams (no surprise there - gluten)
\- vita wheat original (no surprise there - gluten)

\- pasta durham wheat (no surprise there - gluten) ... but not couscous - I thought that was made out of the same stuff and contained gluten?
\- white flour (no surprise there - gluten)
\- wholemeal flour (no surprise there - gluten)

\- chocolate (darrell lea brand)
\- chocolate (lindt brand)
\- chocolate (nestle brand)
\- chocolate (cadbury brand)
\- chocolate (dove brand) ..... assuming these commercial chocolates contain a soy product as carob and cocoa were fine
\- Licorice (no surprise there - gluten)

\- quite a few corn, oat, bran and wheat cereals - no major problem for me as I avoid these anyway ... but rice bran, rice bubbles, oat bran and polenta were OK

\- brown sugar
\- white sugar
\- raw sugar

\- beer (no loss)
\- red wine (what!!!!!!!)

\- apricots
\- bananas
\- kiwifruit
\- pawpaw

\- cola
\- drinking chocolate (cadbury)
\- fanta
\- ginger ale
\- milo (contains gluten)
\- ovaltine (contains gluten)

\- garlic
\- gelatine

\- shellfish (what!!!!!!)

\- ayam oyster sauce
\- soy sauce
\- tomato sauce
\- tomato paste
\- worcester sauce

\- butter
\- cows cheese
\- cows yoghurt
\- cream
\- eggs
\- icecream
\- full cream milk (but A2 is OK)
\- skim milk
\- soy milk

\- tofu
\- tempey

\- pork (no!!!! - I love bacon and crackling!!) - funnily enough, by avoiding eggs recently, I also avoided bacon ... and felt great

\- dove deodorant
\- mum deodorant

\- polyester (funny - I've always hated wearing this)

\- cigarette smoke (I've always hated this too - tried smoking once when I was 17 and vomited horribly ... never did it again and now can't stand the smell)

I'm wondering if anyone else has ever had a DNA Hair Allergy testing done? Were the results in line with other tests you had done? Did you feel better after avoiding the foods they suggested to avoid?

I'm also interested in what the connections are between the various foods, especially the fruit and vegetables. I thought eggplant might be off the list being a nightshade, as tomato is, but eggplant seemed to be fine.

I'm also wondering if this is a 'forever' thing, or if one day I'll be able to eat these foods again. I simply love shellfish and avocado ....

Thanks for any advice in advance.
Sandra

# Thread: ammonia/nail polish remover breath
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-nail-polish-remover-breath.13576/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-31T16:38:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-nail-polish-remover-breath.13576/post-159946>

Anyone have any guess as to why my breath has been having a taste/scent of ammonia or nail polish the past two days?

It started at the same time as I started a much more determined no-carb diet focused largely on morning steak with coconut oil and pink himalayan salt. I shouldn't say NO carb but it's a completely different diet than I've been used to for many years. No toast in the morning, no bread-wrapped lunch sandwich of some kind, and no TONS of pretzels before bed. Carbs limited to about two handfuls of pretzels and enough white rice for a few pieces of vegetable sushi.

Otherwise I'm not doing anything differently but I keep wondering what this smell is.. yesterday I dumped a cup of coffee out because I thought it somehow got some sort of cleaner in it! Then this morning I realized I can smell it again.. (and yeah I brush my teeth in the morning and before bed, so it's not like bacteria are doing something overnight and I wake up with funky breath, lol). I don't drink alcohol or smoke either, for what it's worth.

(note - I realize "dr google" suggests kidney failure for ammonia breath - probably not accurate for me, though my glaucoma medicine does warn of the most likely side effect being kidney stones and I have had absolutely horrid back pain on the left side every so often for about a week now, but not bad enough to even call a doctor so probably unrelated. I have no other funky stuff going on, and I would know if I had yeasties/a UTI, which I'm fairly certain I do not. Unless it's systemic candida, which maybe would explain the white layer on my tongue for many years.. but every time I see my GP he has me stick out my tongue so he can look in my throat so I'm sure if it was anything unusual he'd have mentioned it at some point in the past many years. (then he offers me antibiotics, antidepressants, or a referral to someone else.. I think our relationship is about over but that's a different story.)

Anyway - thoughts? I don't want to go back to my GP unless my face falls off into my cereal bowl so figured I'd be best off just asking here.

Thanks!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-31T16:57:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-nail-polish-remover-breath.13576/post-159948>

I'm going to hazard a guess that I should be tested for h. pylori. Wondering if this seems reasonable/likely to others, and whether anyone can recommend me the test to use, keeping in mind I'm in NY state which seems to think we're not to be trusted ordering our own tests in most circumstances.

edit: or perhaps it is just THAT easy to get myself into ketosis..? can you buy ketone test strips over the counter in a drugstore? I'll have to look today. (might be worth mentioning that my mom had diabetes and my oldest brother does as well. Neither drank or ate badly that I'm aware of but both got type 2 diabetes. Neither ever used/uses internet or any technology or anything. My brother doesn't smoke or anything, he's always lived very clean but works in a casino FWIW. I do have one brother who died of liver disease at age 50 as well - he was an alcoholic but not to the extent that one would expect would be necessary for this to be his fate (however I do have the SNP that causes 6x faster damage as it relates to alcoholic liver disease so I guess he did too). The more info I add, the less clear the answer to this thread becomes, which is why I am confused.)

apologies, and many thanks.

# Thread: Would nattokinase help a child with Hunter's syndrome who breaks down 0 GAGS?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-nattokinase-help-a-child-with-hunters-syndrome-who-breaks-down-0-gags.13598/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2015-04-01T18:07:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-nattokinase-help-a-child-with-hunters-syndrome-who-breaks-down-0-gags.13598/post-160069>

****Amyloid-degrading ability of nattokinase from Bacillus subtilis natto.**
[Hsu RL](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Hsu%20RL%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402)1, [Lee KT](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lee%20KT%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402), [Wang JH](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Wang%20JH%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402), [Lee LY](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lee%20LY%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402), [Chen RP](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chen%20RP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19117402#)**

**Abstract**
More than 20 unrelated proteins can form amyloid fibrils in vivo which are related to various diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, prion disease, and systematic amyloidosis. Amyloid fibrils are an ordered protein aggregate with a lamellar cross-beta structure. Enhancing amyloid clearance is one of the targets of the therapy of these amyloid-related diseases. Although there is debate on whether the toxicity is due to amyloids or their precursors, research on the degradation of amyloids may help prevent or alleviate these diseases. In this study, we explored the amyloid-degrading ability of nattokinase, a fibrinolytic subtilisin-like serine protease, and determined the optimal conditions for amyloid hydrolysis. This ability is shared by proteinase K and subtilisin Carlsberg, but not by trypsin or plasmin.

**

**Amyloidosis: new strategies for treatment.**
[Hirschfield GM](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Hirschfield%20GM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=12962700)1, [Hawkins PN](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Hawkins%20PN%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=12962700).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12962700#)**

**Abstract**
Amyloidosis is a disorder of protein folding in which normally soluble proteins are deposited extracellularly as insoluble fibrils, impairing tissue structure and function. Over 20 unrelated proteins form amyloid fibrils in vivo, with fibrils sharing a lamellar cross-beta sheet structure, composed of non-covalently associated protein or peptide subunits. Amyloidosis may be acquired or hereditary and local or systemic, and is defined according to the precursor protein. Of note, local amyloid deposition occurs in Alzheimer's disease (AD) and maturity onset diabetes but their precise role in the pathogenesis of these diseases remains uncertain. Glycosaminoglycans (GAG) and the pentraxin protein, serum amyloid P (SAP) component, are universal non-fibrillar constituents of amyloid deposits that contribute to fibrillogenesis. We review potential therapies for amyloidosis, which include measures to reduce the production of amyloidogenic precursor proteins, interference with fibrillogenesis, and enhancement of amyloid clearance, either by active or passive immunisation or by destabilising deposits through removal of serum amyloid P component.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Omega 6/3 ratio - is mine good enough for CT?

# Thread: FPIES
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fpies.13670/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### rlee314 — 2015-04-06T20:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fpies.13670/post-160433>

is anyone familiar w/dis-ease that is seeming to affect more and more children and infants?

[http://fpiesfoundation.org/about-fpies-3/](http://fpiesfoundation.org/about-fpies-3/)

**Food Protein-Induced Enterocolitis Syndrome (FPIES)** is a type of food allergy affecting the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Classic symptoms of FPIES include profound vomiting, diarrhea, and dehydration. These symptoms can lead to severe lethargy, change in body temperature and blood pressure. Unlike typical food allergies, symptoms may not be immediate and do not show up on standard allergy tests. Furthermore, the negative allergy evaluation may delay the diagnosis and take the focus off the causative food. Nonetheless, FPIES can present with severe symptoms following ingestion of a food trigger.

**FPIES Common Symptoms:**

There are two ways that infants or children with FPIES might come to medical attention.

1.  The classic pattern of an FPIES reaction is when a healthy infant or child develops symptoms shortly after eating a food. There is a characteristic delay of 2-3 hours before onset of severe and repetitive vomiting and eventually diarrhea. The child may appear very ill and sleepy (lethargic), and may become pale or blue. When evaluated by a doctor, he/she may be found to have low blood pressure, seem dehydrated, and have blood tests that mimic infection (sepsis); which in some cases can lead to sepsis-like shock. Many infants who are eventually diagnosed with FPIES are initially suspected to have a severe infection or sepsis.
2.  The second common pattern of FPIES reaction symptoms occurs when infants who are ingesting a problem food (usually milk or soy-based formula or proteins in breast milk) as a consistent part of their diet might experience increasingly severe vomiting, diarrhea, and poor growth, possibly progressing to an illness mimicking a severe total-body infection. Please note that each child is unique and your child may experience their own range and intensity of these symptoms.

**FPIES Common Triggers:**

In the first months of life, FPIES reactions are most often caused by cow’s milk protein formula, and sometimes by soy. Proteins in breast milk may also cause symptoms in some infants.

For infants experiencing FPIES with solid foods, rice and oats are the most common triggers. Current research reports other common triggers that include, but are not limited to, milk, soy, barley, poultry, peas, green beans, sweet potatoes, and squash. However, any food protein can be a trigger and some infants may be sensitive to other foods as well. In addition, some children may react to one or two foods whereas others may experience reactions to multiple foods.

**FPIES Diagnosis and Testing:**

FPIES is a non-IgE food allergy, which unlike classic food allergy, cannot be diagnosed with readily available food allergy tests such as skin prick test (SPT) or blood test that measure food IgE antibodies (RAST). These tests are helpful to identify triggers for typical food allergies that result in immediate hives, wheezing, and swelling and are characteristically _negative_ in FPIES. An FPIES diagnosis is usually made by considering the history of the characteristic symptoms and exclusion of various alternative illnesses. A medical doctor, often an allergist and/or gastroenterologist, should be involved in making the diagnosis. Although Atopy Patch testing (APT) is often used for FPIES patients, it is not considered a validated test for FPIES diagnosis. Blood tests performed during a reaction may be helpful since the results often mimic the body’s response to infection. The most definitive test is a medically supervised oral food challenge (OFC)-where the suspect food is given to the child in a controlled clinical environment. An oral food challenge, however, is not often needed initially if the doctor has excluded alternative illnesses and the medical history is consistent with FPIES.

**FPIES Treatment and Course:**

FPIES reactions can be severe. It is important to get to prompt medical attention where treatment, such as fluids given into the vein to help stabilize blood pressure and treat dehydration, can be given in order to avoid sepsis-like shock. Although some doctors prescribe epinephrine to stabilize blood pressure before medical treatment, the main therapy is to get intravenous fluids; also steroids can be used to quell the immune reaction. Preparing a [letter](http://fpiesfoundation.org/emergency-care/) for potential trips to the ER, containing both FPIES information and a list of your child’s triggers, may be helpful.

Unfortunately, there are currently no simple tests for FPIES. The primary test, as mentioned above, is a medically supervised oral food challenge with the trigger food. The good news is that FPIES usually resolves with time. Your child will need to be closely followed by his/her doctor to discuss what foods are safe to eat and when it may be time to determine if FPIES has resolved. With proper medical attention and a personalized dietary plan to ensure proper nutrition, children with FPIES can grow and thrive.

how would one help those who are dealing w/this?
what would you suggest?

Yew,
I have the greenwave meter and filters. I bought the greewave meter first to test. The meter measures in millivots. Before filters installed, my home wires were reading between 700 to over 1900. I could listen to a radio station through one power plug. After installing filters, most power plugs went to below 50mV and one or two at just above 70mV. They do work and make a huge difference in terms of cutting excessive microsurges. However, I don't have a gauss or RF meters to check before and after.

# Thread: Year abroad in Bosnia and Herzegovina!!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-04-08T05:01:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160610>

After essays, interviews, a lot of eliminations and waiting, I have been offered a full scholarship to Bosnia and Herzegovina (this is where they see me as a best fit, I had no choice) for my junior year in high school, 2015-2016! This is through the Department of State's AFS Kennedy-Lugar Youth Exchange and Study Abroad (YES abroad) scholarship program, for US-Muslim relations basically.

**Does anyone have any advice?** I will do EVERYTHING possible for optimal without making my host family think I'm crazy. Strict epi-paleo may be difficult, but PLENTY of CT, loads of grounding, along with avoiding fake light and using my many special glasses for protection. Avoiding EMF will be a challenge, but I will strongly consider bringing my canopy, schumann generator, and magnetico (all of which I hope to purchase very soon).

**Any experience traveling, living with a host family, being in that part of the world, or any general wisdom or ideas, is GREATLY appreciated.**

Thanks a million!

\- Matt

### lioness7 — 2015-04-09T03:06:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160685>

Matt, my family is from there...been there many times....ask away!

### Penny — 2015-04-09T03:27:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160686>

Congrats Matt
Consider rooms might be smaller in Europe than in the U.S.A. and you might not be able to fit all that stuff into your room... the magnetico in particular is quite heavy and I would not begin to know how you would transport it
Plus, you might be spending time living in a bedroom with another occupant - who might not feature any of it either... I would take the blue blockers and seeing if there is a body of water you could jump into - failing that - maybe a cold shower will have to suffice - ice cubes are probably easily available - truly, travel light IMHO - mabye get a picture of the place you will be staying prior to going - so blue blockers and hot water bottle which you could freeze - I'm sure you can get plenty of seafood there as well -
Remember, you are a guest... and an ambassador for us

### lioness7 — 2015-04-09T04:08:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160687>

Matt, do you know where you will be staying yet?

### Matty_M — 2015-04-09T04:24:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160688>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=160686)
>
> Congrats Matt
> Consider rooms might be smaller in Europe than in the U.S.A. and you might not be able to fit all that stuff into your room... the magnetico in particular is quite heavy and I would not begin to know how you would transport it
> Plus, you might be spending time living in a bedroom with another occupant - who might not feature any of it either... I would take the blue blockers and seeing if there is a body of water you could jump into - failing that - maybe a cold shower will have to suffice - ice cubes are probably easily available - truly, travel light IMHO - mabye get a picture of the place you will be staying prior to going - so blue blockers and hot water bottle which you could freeze - I'm sure you can get plenty of seafood there as well -
> Remember, you are a guest... and an ambassador for us

Thanks! I will keep all of that in mind. I definitely want to pack lightly, and that will be easy in the clothes department because I'm interested in unifying my wardrobe (like steve jobs and mark zuckerberg). I just dislike having to pick something different every day. Besides that, it's my phone, maybe a laptop, and some "gadgets" to help me get close to optimal in the modern world.

I will probably have no option but to eat things like bread and what not, but I will likely be leptin sensitive and have my gut and hypercortisolism healed up a good bit by when I leave (end of summer), so it's not the worst thing. Benefit>negatives, for sure! I appreciate the advice.

\- Matt

### Matty_M — 2015-04-09T04:33:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160689>

> [lioness7 said:](/goto/post?id=160685)
>
> Matt, my family is from there...been there many times....ask away!

> [lioness7 said:](/goto/post?id=160687)
>
> Matt, do you know where you will be staying yet?

Thanks a lot! I was hoping someone here might be a native! How lucky am I.

I am still pretty much in the dark on where I will be staying still. All I know is that I have been offered the year abroad somewhere in Bosnia, _most likely_ in or near Banja Luka or Sarajevo, because that is where the current American students are.

I could probably ask a million questions but I don't want to know _too_ much before going, otherwise it might steal from the "awe" of being in a new place for the first time. However:

**How conducive is BiH to the "optimal" lifestyle? What things are totally acceptable, and what aren't, in terms of grounding, fancy EMF equipment like bed canopies or things like blue-blocker glasses, etc.? What is the food like? How technologically advanced is it in comparison to the U.S.?** Any of that good stuff that you might think of.

Also,

**What are the things you would like me to know before going over as an American public diplomat?
**
I will definitely keep in touch here if I have more questions. Thanks so much!

\- Matt

### Nittygrittydanny — 2015-04-09T04:46:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160692>

Magnetico might not be wired for euro power plug. If your N=1 is good go and do your best. If poor health say no thanks and stay home!

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-04-09T06:15:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160698>

> [Nittygrittydanny said:](/goto/post?id=160692)
>
> Magnetico might not be wired for euro power plug. If your N=1 is good go and do your best. If poor health say no thanks and stay home!

Magnetico does not need a plug as it is just a 'fixed magnet' no power involved. Maybe you mean a grounding sheet (which can be 'grounded' into a wall socket or the ground itself )or something like that just wanted to clear up possible confusion on that.

### Penny — 2015-04-09T21:42:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160751>

I think you should focus more on how cool an experience it will be living in another country, learning some words in the new language and maybe checking our the book: Dictionary of Cultural Literacy...

A grounding plug for your laptop - along with the European plug converter might be a light/healthy idea ([www.lessemf.com](http://www.lessemf.com)) - make sure any American "gadgets" have a European adapter - I would also do any "metering" in private - plus, who knows what the airport authorities might think of any "meters" they find in your bag... and would it change anything anyway? You will still be living where they tell you to

In fact, you can start here
[http://www.101languages.net/bosnian/](http://www.101languages.net/bosnian/)

[http://wikitravel.org/en/Bosnia\_and\_Herzegovina](http://wikitravel.org/en/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina)

The wikitravel site paints an idealic picture - I predict there will be much fun to be had...

Go forth and explore Matt And send us lots of pictures

Having said all that, you might take some probiotics with you, just in case... people who travel also swear by oregano oil and Paradex...

### Hope — 2015-04-10T03:37:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160765>

Awesome!! It's a sort of poorer part of Europe - or used to be - plus of course there was that terrible civil war in the 90s and I think you'll have a great/easy time finding non-processed foods there. Especially in smaller towns people might farm etc. which will also or may also mean alcohol...even the bread while not optimal is MOST likely whole grain and very different than the US air tosser that is horrible. Also I would think they use much less pesticides than here - I know Poland supposedly uses less - not sure about B&H. You'll meet some amazing people I am sure who will have been through very different things.

You should not have trouble finding fermented foods of all kinds.

Certainly your life will be very very different after and during this experience.......it'll be amazing.

For your host family - you will likely find them very hospitable, trying to feed you and offer you what they have....you might think about a little present to give them, something typical of the US - even if it's a baseball cap of your home team or whatever for the child/dren in that family. They'll be so grateful, I bet. Or even a US sweater or something.

Will you get any cross cultural training before you leave? I'd be surprised if not. Here is a tiny sliver: [http://www.expatsblog.com/contests/799/top-10-crazy-things-about-living-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina](http://www.expatsblog.com/contests/799/top-10-crazy-things-about-living-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina)

You'll experience much beauty and you won't be too far from the Sea either........which will be gorgeous come summer!

Have a blast!!

### Matty_M — 2015-04-10T04:04:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160767>

They'll be so grateful, I bet. Or even a US sweater or something.

>

>

You're getting me so much more excited to go!!!!!!

Thanks so much for the advice. I wasn't planning on getting my own training, but I do believe the program does so at the pre-departure orientation in late june in DC. If not, I may look into that myself.

I bet the food will be great over all. I'm hoping there are fewer cell towers and what not, but it's not a huge deal. When I get home I will be able to do whatever is necessary to fix anything up.

I'm not sure if it's customary, but if the family (like I've heard about Italy) regulary allows the kids to drink alcohol with dinner and stuff, would that be wise to avoid, for the most part or entirely?

Thanks so much,

\- Matt

### Matty_M — 2015-04-10T04:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160768>

> [lioness7 said:](/goto/post?id=160685)
>
> Matt, my family is from there...been there many times....ask away!

Another thing to ask: I am reading that there are still around half a million land mines undetected in the country. How might I be certain to avoid these, and if I have to fear walking off of the beaten path (some articles suggest not even going off the roads), how should I get grounded? I imagine the host family's property will have a yard or something, but I can't be sure. What are your thoughts/experiences with this?

\- Matt

### Penny — 2015-04-10T05:58:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160772>

Matt - kids have been having a little (4 oz or less) wine with dinner for eons... remember, when in Rome...

The land mines would be scary admittedly - I would stay on the roads, personally - there must be some place/park/lake/body of water people go to relax - you might also look into putting a copper tack or whatever those things are that make shoes able to ground - or those attachments for your tennis shoes - I think lessemf has them and they're cheap -

You are going to have a blast

### Hope — 2015-04-10T17:29:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/year-abroad-in-bosnia-and-herzegovina.13701/post-160833>

> [Matty\_M said:](/goto/post?id=160767)
>
> You're getting me so much more excited to go!!!!!!
>
> Thanks so much for the advice. I wasn't planning on getting my own training, but I do believe the program does so at the pre-departure orientation in late june in DC. If not, I may look into that myself.
>
> I bet the food will be great over all. I'm hoping there are fewer cell towers and what not, but it's not a huge deal. When I get home I will be able to do whatever is necessary to fix anything up.
>
> I'm not sure if it's customary, but if the family (like I've heard about Italy) regulary allows the kids to drink alcohol with dinner and stuff, would that be wise to avoid, for the most part or entirely?
>
> Thanks so much,
>
> \- Matt

No idea about the alcohol - I'd express what you'd like. I am sure there will not be as many cellphone towers there as even Italy or Austria to the North/West. It is still a poor country, and even when I grew up in Germany, MANY people I knew had emigrated to Germany to make a better life there and send money back......

Do read a little about culture shock - it will be there....it is very normal and eventually it will help to truly deeply broaden your internal horizons in a way not much else can. I used to work for Berlitz Cross Cultural who prepared expats to go anywhere to work and they had lots of super helpful material. I am sure the course you'll get in DC will cover some of this.

Above all, remember to have fun....and don't worry too much. It truly will be amazing. And you will make friends for life.

# Thread: hypercortisolism and DHEA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercortisolism-and-dhea.13723/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-04-08T23:34:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercortisolism-and-dhea.13723/post-160677>

I'd like to hear anyone's advice on these numbers. Any suggestions are appreciated. The stuff below this line is a background.

I'm a 15 year old male, 5' 7" and 145 lbs. I was a vegetarian in poor health from age 7 to 14, then I began the dive through paleo, GAPS, autoimmune paleo, binge eating/dieting cycle, and eventually came upon the Leptin Rx as the best way to help me recover, which I have been working with since around February, and have now made the most progress in the last two weeks (aka no major setbacks- any of which have tended to be food-related issues, specifically with carb craving issues). I had been previously following epi-paleo since december 2014, and paleo since last january (2014), with a good amount of binging on carbs and sugar that summer, which almost stopped completely when the school year began, but occasionally occurred with less bad foods such as dark chocolate, nuts, dried fruit, starches, or other things with added sugar or carbs- typically not grain/legume/veggie oil containing products, though. Paleo beat those out of me.

This january I began seeing a functional medicine doc who got a stool test and we found a parasite (blastocystis hominis), for which we used an antibiotic...but I did feel a bit better after, and my stomach issues significantly improved. We are improving my gut health with a variety of supplements while I follow the leptin rx and epi-paleo.

Just yesterday I saw her again for the results of a saliva test, which showed what I have pictured. She prescribed a supplement called "adaptocrine" and adrenacalm, both by apex energetics, as well as to continue what I'm doing. She also gave me a chiropractic adjustment- which I don't really know much about...

My current symptoms are **waking up like a zombie**, having **much less energy than I'd like** and getting **very tired in the middle of the day** (while sitting down in school- I had been not eating lunch and just doing the big-ass breakfast and early dinner but am beginning to have a big snack in the middle of the day to avoid the drop in blood sugar and resultant spike in cortisol, only while I am unable to access the PPP. I have a chronic low-level **stuffy nose** which becomes worse at times, and I believe the main triggers to be certain auto-immune restricted foods, which I will keep out. I am constantly **anxious and often stressed without obvious cause,** and **sometimes extremely irritable.** I have **memory issues,** for example forgetting appointments and similar things completely, my phone calendar usually saving me. Most annoyingly is that I am obsessive, which goes along with the anxious part, specifically about improving my health to an optimal state.

Don't get me wrong, I don't feel horrible all the time, in fact I'm pretty well. But the sleep and anxiety especially really suck. I am following most (almost all) of the points of Dr. Kruse's work- ketogenic epi-paleo, leptin Rx, leaky gut Rx, grounding, CT introduction, sunlight, light exercise, avoiding EMF (I'm working on my sleep area first), getting lots of sleep, etc.

Thanks,

\- Matt

The first thing that comes to mind is that I should be trying to implement meditation, but then I start thinking about where I will be taking the time from...I just have to put the slightly less important things like homework/school on the back burner, where I can still get good grades just with slightly less focus/stress and more perspective on what's really important.

Dr. Rooks has me using those two supplements that I mentioned and I'd like to see how they work. Also, I believe that as I become more and more leptin sensitive, the better I will feel, and the better I will be able to heal this up.

**Do you think there's anything really big that I'm overlooking?** I'm trying to find happiness in every day, and I'm having great success recently. I try to sleep a lot, although waking up feels nearly impossible. I get grounded as often as possible, am working on lowering EMF exposure, eating very, very well and trying to incorporate more of the leaky gut rx principles, and trying to avoid stress, as well as to see sunrise/lots of sunlight and the sunset/darkness at night, and all that good stuff. It definitely is a lot but I believe it's the best way, and that I have a brain good enough to handle it as long as I try to keep my cortisol lower and my dopamine higher.

Thanks for the help,

\- Matt

**[David Limacher](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/david-limacher.11045/)**
posted nice video on light
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...pathways-and-seasons.13531/page-5#post-161705](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/page-5#post-161705)

There is a statement there, that before invention of electric light most people slept close to 12 hrs/day,
in that context sleeping 10hrs/night may sound more normal.

Sleep have cycles. One may naturally wake-up during night at the end of each cycle.
When one uses alarm clock cycle gets interrupted.
Assuming that you would sleep the same amount of time,
you should end up rested more when you awake naturally,
comparing to other time
where you would be awaken up by alarm clock in the middle of your sleep cycle.

Consider arranging your sleep time in such a way that you would wake-up naturally, that would be sometime before the 5:50AM you mention. Say 1/2 to one hour before the 5:50AM. Plan for that, have some morning activities ready and pre-planned so you do not have to waste this morning time.

..
Listen to that video, (I did but was being interrupted by my grandchildren).
There is number of light therapies mentioned there.

I am thinking if possibly having red and infrared (heat) lights operating during part of your sleeping time may be beneficial.

Hopefully others may opine.

/

[www.orgoneffectsaustralia.com](http://www.orgoneffectsaustralia.com)

Quote:
"Most people who are EMF aware seem to believe that the radiation being emitted from their electrical system is the most harmful form of EMF in a building. Closely followed by radio frequency from WIFI and Smart Meters. After that earth radiation being the least harmful.

**Not so! The most dangerous forms of EMF in a building come from the various forms of earth radiation in the form of grid lines, large Very Low Frequency fields and imprints such as Personal, Death and Black Lines.

If we were to list all the various forms of EMF that could exist within a given space, the list below would most accurately explain the most dangerous to the least dangerous. However even the least harmful is still a potential health hazard.

Very Low Frequency(VLF) earth radiation
Photon Line
400 Metre Grid earth magnetic grid line
Black Line
Water Vein over 1000mm wide (ground floor only)
Imprints from Death or Personal energy
EMF from electrical systems
Radio Frequency and Microwave fields from WIFI/ Smart Meters and appliances\*
Digital TV beams
Benker Grid earth magnetic grid line
Mirrors holding negative thought forms
Cursed land (negative thought forms)

So having a building assesed for MF from electrical appliances and systems and radio frequency from potential outside hazards such as mobile phone towers, and not including all forms of earth radiation can be misleading."
**

Hi, i wanted to upadte important improvements. Three weeks ago i started with 2 important changes at the same time. They gave me amazing results. The bad thing is that i dont know which of both was the most effective. I started the Fournier Effect CT and Morning Sunbathing. I did the CT by filling the bath tub up to its half size, sitting and adding a table on top of it and using a piece of ice as a pillow. The table was for being able to read while on the tub, and did this every day and stayed 30-40 minutes. The water covered all my legs and my lower body up to 5 cm above my navel. I added a lot of ice to the water. This CT didnt make me shiver at all... And the sunbathing, i went to read outside from 8 to 9, amazing morning sun. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### dantothep — 2015-04-13T17:29:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concussioncte-prescription.13825/post-161084>

Head injuries - I had a minor parkour related occipital head injury yesterday. Didn't lose consciousness, and was able to continue training, jumping etc. Felt a little mentally slow yesterday (trouble remembering words etc) , trouble finding words. Did supplement with 500mg Magnesium post injury (+ 500mg before bed) then ate roast lamb dinner with a few glasses of red about 1600 , in the evening 2100 felt a little nausea. Have CT this morning and today feel a bit mentally foggy and a little more tired than unusual but neck isn't too stiff, slight restriction on rotating head left.

However read this [http://www.jackkruse.com/concussioncte-prescription/](http://www.jackkruse.com/concussioncte-prescription/) and it freaked me out a bit, as I have had many head injuries including fractured skull @ 18 months old, and many head lacerations.

I generally rely on my inbuilt diagnostics tool that lets me know if i'm doing ok, by how I feel, which is pretty good considering, which is why I haven't gone to hospital.

FWIW - before training I had some CO and Oysters, and some more Oysters this AM.

Anything else I should do to mitigate risk apart from more CT tonight?

Dan
