### Nittygrittydanny — 2015-05-06T21:09:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mother-of-the-year.14352/post-163460>

**Local**
**Woman Cuts Family Off From WiFi Over Health Concerns**
May 5, 2015 11:17 PM
[Share on email62](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2015/05/05/woman-cuts-family-off-from-wifi-over-health-concerns/#)

[View Comments](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2015/05/05/woman-cuts-family-off-from-wifi-over-health-concerns/#comments)

**Related Tags:**
[Anura Lawson](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/anura-lawson/), [CBSLA](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/cbsla/), [Cellphones](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/cellphones/), [Health](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/health/), [Internet](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/internet/), [IPads](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/ipads/), [IPods](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/ipods/), [Lisa Sigell](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/lisa-sigell/), [Radiation](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/radiation/), [Wifi](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/wifi/), [WiFi Dangers](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/wifi-dangers/), [Wireless](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/tag/wireless/)
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

**Lisa Sigell**
Lisa Sigell is a reporter for CBS 2 and KCAL 9 where she has worked...
[Read More](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/personality/lisa-sigell/)

**CBS Los Angeles (con't)**

Affordable Care Act Updates:**[CBSLA.com/ACA](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/category/affordable-care-act/)**

Health News & Information:**[CBSLA.com/Health](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/content-vertical/health/)**

**Links & Numbers**[Information & Resources On Dangers Of Marijuana Use](https://sites.google.com/a/state.co.us/marijuana/)[Covered California Enrollment Methods](https://www.coveredca.com/?utm_source=google&utm_medium=cpc&gclid=CPu_wf_-qL0CFcuSfgodJX0AoQ)** **[Hire LA Youth](http://hirelayouth.com/Welcome.html)** **[Hospital Ratings](http://www.consumerreports.org/health/doctors-hospitals/hospital-ratings/ratings/search-results.htm?state=CA)[Stradivarius Fest](http://laco.org/series/41to)** **[Tell Us Who's Hiring!](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2011/05/23/contact-cbs2-news-department/)
You've Earned Points for Reading!**Claim points in our Reward Center, and earn more tomorrow.[health](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2015/05/05/woman-cuts-family-off-from-wifi-over-health-concerns/#)  concerns prompted her to cut her family off from wireless and wants more research conducted into the safety of WiFi.

In the Lawson household, cassette tapes are still in use, as are landline telephones.

Not in use, however, are cellphones, iPads, iPods nor absolutely no wireless connections to the outside world.

“You’re just thinking, ‘I want to live,’ ” said Anura Lawson, a mother and [teacher](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2015/05/05/woman-cuts-family-off-from-wifi-over-health-concerns/#)  .

Lawson says she started feeling sick in 2012 soon after the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power installed a wireless smart meter on her home.

There are 52,000 of these smart meters being tested in L.A., and the DWP says they’re safe.

But some people claim the radio waves they emit trigger health problems.

“I began to feel dizzy at first,” Lawson said. “Migraines. Heart palpitations.”

Lawson’s daughter, Amira, 22, also experienced trouble.

“My brain was running slower, and I was like, ‘I don’t know what’s going on,’ ” she said.

“There is a syndrome called electromagnetic hypersensitivity,” said Robert Nagourney, an oncologist in Long Beach and professor at UC Irvine.

“There are two kinds of radiation we speak about. One is ionizing, and one is nonionizing,” Nagourney said. “In ionizing radiation, clearly, there’s a great risk of DNA damage, mutation and cancer.”

This danger has not been proven with nonionizing radiation, which is the type emitted by cellphones, smart meters and WiFi.

But with our 21st century explosion of wireless, Nagourney says “we’re bathed in this type of radiation.”

“Does it cause [medical](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2015/05/05/woman-cuts-family-off-from-wifi-over-health-concerns/#)  illnesses? Great question. Difficult to answer,” the doctor said.

For the Lawson [family](http://losangeles.cbslocal.com/2015/05/05/woman-cuts-family-off-from-wifi-over-health-concerns/#)  , who consider themselves sensitive to electromagnetic fields, the smart meter had to go.

Once they got their analogue meter back, Lawson said they felt better healthwise.

But a 1 ½ years later, Lawson says her symptoms returned.

“I began to have migraines. I began to have the heart palpitations,” she said.

It coincided with a change at work as Lawson is an eighth-grade English teacher at Johnnie Cochran Middle School.

In March 2014 to facilitate its iPad program, Lawson says, the “LAUSD decided to install WiFi within my school site.”

With her health deteriorating, Lawson testified before the Board of Education.

“I’m the parent of six children. I want to be around to see them grow up,” she had said. “When I am in the WiFi classroom, I don’t feel good at all.”

“There were a lot of people that were concerned about putting WiFi in the classroom,” said Bill Piazzi, the LAUSD’s environmental assessment coordinator, whose job it is to make sure classrooms are safe.

Where there are WiFi access points, Piazza says, they use a very sophisticated piece of equipment, which measures radio-frequency output.

“We looked very hard at the potential exposures in the classroom,” he said. “No matter where we went, it’s all low.”

Piazza showed CBS2 an emissions report from a classroom that had WiFi.

“These are 30 kids streaming in a classroom, and these are the exposures that they’re getting,” he said “Look how low that is.”

Still, the board in September agreed to turn off the WiFi in Lawson’s classroom.

Now, she is believed to be the first public school teacher in the U.S. granted a health accommodation for electromagnetic hypersensitivity.

“I think the district did a very good job in making a reasonable accommodation for her,” Piazza said.

Lawson’s accommodation applies to her classroom only. To keep her children away from WiFi exposure, her four youngest are being educated at home.

The Lawson’s have Internet at home, but it’s only through a wired connection.

Over the past three years, Lawson says, she has encountered plenty of doubters, which Nagourney says is too bad.

“People are of different sensitivities. We know that one person can get a bee sting and nothing happens. Another person goes into anaphylactic shock. It’s the same bee sting. Different reaction,” he said.

Nagourney says the likelihood of WiFi emissions causing illness is small, “but the chance is not zero.”

Lawson says she would like to see the issue thoroughly researched and believes the newness of wireless makes it impossible to fully understand now how it might one day affect one’s health.

Thus, she’s keeping her kids away from it for as long as she can.

“Teaching doesn’t have to involve a device,” she said. “I think that our students unfortunately are the Guinea pigs, and I don’t think that’s right.”

The LAUSD says it is continuing to study all safety data with respect to WiFi. To view the most recent reports, click **[here](http://achieve.lausd.net/Page/3954)**.

Lawson has started an online petition to get WiFi out of California classrooms. For more information, click **[here](http://petitions.moveon.org/sign/stop-microwave-radiation-1)**. To reach Nagourney, click **[here](http://www.rational-t.com/)**.
**

### fitness@home — 2015-05-08T22:13:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-after-cilantro.14377/post-163635>

Love or Hate Cilantro

> We put the cilantro taste question to about 50,000 23andMe customers, asking whether they liked the taste of cilantro and whether they thought cilantro had a soapy taste. When we compared the DNA of the cilantro haters to the DNA of cilantro lovers, we found a SNP (or genetic variation) called rs72921001 to be associated with the trait in a subset of about 25,000 people with European ancestry. (About 13 percent of 23andMe customers with European ancestry answered that cilantro tastes soapy, and 26 percent dislike it.)
> Read more at [http://blog.23andme.com/23andme-research/cilantro-love-hate-genetic-trait/#hcK3jqmgEIIXTrFQ.99](http://blog.23andme.com/23andme-research/cilantro-love-hate-genetic-trait/#hcK3jqmgEIIXTrFQ.99)
>
> People with the less common version of rs72921001 had lower odds of perceiving a soapy taste and of disliking the herb. This SNP is not directly tested by 23andMe’s DNA chip (it was inferred from nearby SNPs), so 23andMe customers can instead look at their results for rs7107418, which is highly correlated with rs72921001 in people of European descent.
> It shouldn’t be surprising that this SNP is located near eight genes that code for olfactory receptors, biological sensors that detect chemicals in the air and in food. Humans have hundreds of these receptors, which send signals to our brains to produce what we recognize as aromas and flavors. But exactly how this works is complex and differs from person to person. The same chemical can be found in both appealing and unappealing places — [cheese and body odor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butyric_acid), for example. Conversely, the same ingredient — such as cilantro — can contain both pleasant and unpleasant chemicals. Whether stinky cheese and cilantro are delicious or disgusting depends on your particular perception of many different chemicals.
>
> Read more at [http://blog.23andme.com/23andme-research/cilantro-love-hate-genetic-trait/#hcK3jqmgEIIXTrFQ.99](http://blog.23andme.com/23andme-research/cilantro-love-hate-genetic-trait/#hcK3jqmgEIIXTrFQ.99)

### Peertje80 — 2015-05-08T22:47:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-after-cilantro.14377/post-163638>

I guess it does tastes soapy

# Thread: Pollen allergies getting pretty bad
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pollen-allergies-getting-pretty-bad.14424/>

### Matty_M — 2015-05-09T16:00:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pollen-allergies-getting-pretty-bad.14424/post-163700>

Hi Everyone,

I'm not sure if this is the right section for this, but I think it works.

I've been feeling pretty good lately, as my overall health is definitely improving. I'm doing CT nightly (around 30 min near 16 degrees C), grounding, sleeping 8 or more hours, waking up more refreshed, avoiding fake light when its dark (and using my red glasses at night and gamma rays while under fluorescent lights in school), and drinking over a just over a gallon (about 4 liters) of ice cold water daily. I'm eating autoimmune protocol (toying around to see what's an issue- now I've gone all-out besides ghee and egg yolk- no eggs, nuts, seeds, dairy, nightshades) epi-paleo and have been taking a lot of the supplements for leaky gut (coconut fat, probiotics, prebiotics, l-glutamine, Mg, Zn, CoQ10, bone broth, and betain hydrochloric acid because it seems my stomach acid is still low) and those for cortisol issues (B complex, ubiquinol, Mg, L-carnitine, D-ribose) because my cortisol index showed it very high. I am working on increasing the fish bone broth, probiotics & prebiotics, and incorporating meditation/mindfulness.

However, I am having issues and constantly feeling uncertain.

One of my number one goals is to not have a stuffy nose (sign of autoimmune stuff I think?) by the time I leave for Bosnia and Herzegovina. I think most of optimal will come with this, but I'm not sure.

The pollen in my area recently has been pretty bad, and it's definitely affecting me a lot (stuffy, stuffy nose, itchy throat eyes ears sometimes), especially as I've been sleeping in my backyard to try to be further from the EMF in my house and grounded. When I wake up my symptoms are horrible, and progressively get better through the day. I feel as though the antibiotic from a few months ago definitely contributed to this (leaky gut?..). Would I be better to sleep outside and deal with the allergies, or in my room on the third floor. I would sleep in the laundry room, but I am pretty sure it is moldy, and it also causes me a stuffy nose.

Also, now that it's getting towards summer, would it slow my progress to include some carbs (sweet potatoes, plantains, berries, etc.)? I believe I have done pretty well with the Leptin Rx, as my carb cravings have almost vanished entirely, but I don't want to slow my progress. I saw in some thread that nutritional winter until one is optimal can be very good.

I'm also strongly considering getting a radio/microwave shielding tent from here [http://www.radmeters.com/HF-Canopy-pyramidal-for-single-beds-from-NEW-DAYLITE-fr.html](http://www.radmeters.com/HF-Canopy-pyramidal-for-single-beds-from-NEW-DAYLITE-fr.html) . Do you think this would provide benefit worth the money? I am willing to spend the money if it could truly benefit my health.

So thanks a lot for your help. Any advice/direction would be appreciated very much.

\- Matt

# Thread: Brain fog update
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-fog-update.14428/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Erik — 2015-05-09T18:28:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-fog-update.14428/post-163716>

When i sleep in my grandma\`s house, the next day i dont have brain fog, or have it but super mild. (strange because my grandmas house is surrounded by way more EMF than my house)

When i get a lot of sunlight in the morning, (8, 9 or 10 AM), my brain fog gets super strong and persistent an hour later, it may last till the evening. Why might this be?? Can it be a die-off??? Maybe the morning sun starts to combat this infection? (is it an infection??)

Thanks

### Da-mo — 2015-05-10T01:04:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-fog-update.14428/post-163746>

Is there something different in the construction of the house? (tin roof? metal siding?)
Is there something different in the construction of the bed? (box springs? wooden slats?)
Ground floor or basement versus upper stories?
Geological differences?

Noticing the difference gives you a great opportunity to find out what is working for you there.

### Erik — 2015-05-10T14:42:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-fog-update.14428/post-163759>

In a health forum, somebody posted that a blue LED array device, used every day for 20-30 minutes in the morning, vanished his braing fog after one week, and improved his mood. Might this be true?? I think i have read that blue light its anti-bacterial, maybe it worked that way and killed the infection. What do you think about it?

### Erik — 2015-05-10T14:44:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-fog-update.14428/post-163760>

In my house there is a heating system of underground plastci pipes conducting warm water. My grandmas house may be grounded be cause its an old house. Anyway i start to believe that it is not where i sleep, but when i take the morning sun. When i stay home, in the morning i do sunbathing, and when i go to my grandmas, i do not go out in the morning....

It looks like I need to increase my EPA levels more than anything. Maybe increase AA?

### Lahelada — 2015-05-13T02:56:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/0-1-ml-l-fluoride-water-okay.14465/post-164031>

Yeti,drink it if you have to it is a good,low level as far as fluorisation goes but it is essentially dead water or better to say unstructured water. What you are looking for is structured water or in other words reverse osmosis water or bottled spring water.
This is just my gut feeling but the naturally occurring fluor is handled better by your body than the manmade stuff they use for fluoridation

# Thread: What are the 5 most impactful changes you've made so far?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-5-most-impactful-changes-youve-made-so-far.14466/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Yeti — 2015-05-13T01:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-5-most-impactful-changes-youve-made-so-far.14466/post-164023>

For me (and I'm new here) the 5 most impactful changes I've made for my health and well being are:

1\. Limiting blue light after sunset and using an eye mask when sleeping
2\. Mineral supplementation (inc. Epsom salt baths)
3\. Limiting any caffeine to early morning so that I sleep better
4\. Not using sunscreen (always hated the stuff)
5\. Learning and practising Buddhist mindfulness + philosophy helps me stress less and be happier

# Thread: Which blue blocking glasses are most optimal?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-blue-blocking-glasses-are-most-optimal.14467/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColinGorham — 2015-05-13T05:30:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-blue-blocking-glasses-are-most-optimal.14467/post-164047>

Which blue blocking glasses are most optimal? I recently replaced most of the bulbs in my house with amber ones but am looking for a solution for going out at night and other routine blue light exposures at inappropriate night times.

### fitness@home — 2015-05-13T12:31:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-blue-blocking-glasses-are-most-optimal.14467/post-164073>

Do a search here for blue - blocker threads. There have been many mentions lately.

I wear glasses, so my night time pair are fit overs that I purchased from the Noir website and block 96%. I recently ordered some clip ons from them also that I use at work. [http://www.noir-medical.com/filters/index.html](http://www.noir-medical.com/filters/index.html)

Got this tint for fit overs [http://www.noir-medical.com/filters/63.html](http://www.noir-medical.com/filters/63.html)

Last week I was waiting to meet a friend for dinner (who was late). Went into the optometrist office next door to look at other options for work. Ended up ordering another pair of glasses for work.

Used my old Silhouette frames (saved $$), Crizal lenses with additional BB tint added, and tri-focal my prescription. Paid with my FSA.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-05-13T16:01:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164106>

BTW my estrogen is not high/ I do not have estrogen dominance. I'm sure most people assume that I have that issue and that's why they gave me pg, but that's not the case. I also have no trouble with sleep whatsoever, and no anxiety issues. I get PMS but who doesn't?

When they tested my estradiol (the only estrogen test I've ever been given) it was 140 and I was in the luteal phase, so I was perfectly in range ( "Luteal Phase 58-157" ). No dominance issues.

Had my progesterone done the same day (17 hydroxyprogesterone is what he ordered) and it also was perfectly in range (before ever starting on progesterone lozenges) - my result was 202 (Normal range: Luteal Phase: < or = 285 ng/dL ).

### Lahelada — 2015-05-13T17:22:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164132>

Hope,tanning is actually a wrong word to use. Sunning yourself even with cloudy sky would be more apt. You are not looking for max wattage per square foot but exposing yourself to the correct UV frequencies ,i.e between 8-11 am. Thought I should clarify.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-05-13T17:27:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164134>

oh I see what you mean. I didn't think those frequencies would get through the cloud layer. But that time of day doesn't fit into my n=1 so I'll keep plugging away with supplements of various sorts to see what works.

### JanSz — 2015-05-14T01:00:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164174>

Is There a Correlation Between Androgens and Sexual Desire in Women?
Article first published online: 5 DEC 2014
[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jsm.12774/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jsm.12774/abstract)

/////////
**Results**
Sexual desire correlated overall with FT and androstenedione in the total cohort of women.

In a subgroup of women aged 25–44 years with no use of systemic hormonal contraception,
sexual desire correlated with TT, FT, androstenedione, and DHEAS.

In women aged 45–65 years, androstenedione correlated with sexual desire.

No correlations between ADT-G and sexual desire were identified.

......

### Hope2Learn — 2015-05-21T14:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164915>

I got my DHEA cream today. It says there is 15mg of natural DHEA per pump. When I was playing around with DHEA pills each pill was 25mg I think, and I used to take half per day but got bad acne so then took a quarter per day but stopped taking it because of mood/acne. So I definitely don't want more than one pump per day but not sure if I should do less than that. I know @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) always has so many wonderful charts, but for some reason I don't have very good comprehension ability (translation ability) with charts, which is hard to admit. ### Hope2Learn — 2015-05-21T14:24:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164917>

PS - I had my DHEA done in January to get a baseline - my results:

Dhea (Dehydroepiandrosterone)

(standard range: 102 - 1185 NG/DL )

**MINE: 325 ng/dL**

\-----------
edited to add: I just found this chart of normal levels for DHEA-S for women by age range on the NIH site -ont sure if that's the same as DHEA or not though, range-wise...

BUT I just noticed they're using different values. My test is ng/dL and this chart is ug/dL, so I found an online conversion calculator here -

[http://www.endmemo.com/sconvert/ug\_dlng\_dl.php](http://www.endmemo.com/sconvert/ug_dlng_dl.php)

and it says that my result of **325 ng/dL = 0.325 ug/dL.**

Typical normal ranges for females (for DHEA-S) are:

-   Ages 18 - 19: 145 - 395 micrograms per deciliter (ug/dL)
-   Ages 20 - 29: 65 - 380 ug/dL
-   Ages 30 - 39: 45 - 270 ug/dL
-   **Ages 40 - 49: 32 - 240 ug/dL**
-   Ages 50 - 59: 26 - 200 ug/dL
-   Ages 60 - 69: 13 - 130 ug/dL
-   Ages 69 and older: 17 - 90 ug/dL

So now I guess I have to know if the chart is the same for DHEA and DHEAS because it would appear that I have like 100x less than normal.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-05-21T15:33:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164922>

Mayo clinic says my levels should be:

2-5 years: <2.3 ng/mL
6-10 years: .4 ng/mL
11-14 years: <5.0 ng/mL
15-18 years: <6.6 ng/mL
19-30 years: <13 ng/mL
**31-40 years: <10 ng/mL
41-50 years: <8.0 ng/mL**
51-60 years: <6.0 ng/mL
\> or =61 years: <5.0 ng/mL

and an online conversion calculator says **my level is 3.25 ng/mL**... I'm 42 so so I guess I'm in the bottom third (or half) of my range..? Seems I've got as much DHEA as a child between the age of 6 and 10, which might explain the lack of sex drive..

"**Serum DHEA concentrations exhibit a circadian rhythm that reflects the secretion of corticotropin (ACTH); they also vary during the menstrual cycle, being higher during the luteal phase.** In contrast, serum DHEAS concentrations do not exhibit a circadian rhythm because the plasma half-life of DHEAS is much longer."

oh. Mine \*was\* done during the luteal phase. So in reality my levels are generally even lower than THAT. yikes.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-05-21T16:00:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-164924>

Okay, answering one of my own questions here - DHEA and DHEAS results are not the same even though an article I read on the NIH site said the tests are used interchangeably.

"Results for DHEA are given in nanograms per milliliter (ng/mL). Normal value ranges for DHEA are:

-   1.0 to 9.5 ng/mL for adult men

-   0.4 to 3.7 ng/mL for adult women

Results for DHEA-S are given in micrograms per deciliter (mcg/dL). Normal value ranges for DHEA-S are:

-   10 to 619 mcg/dL for adult men

-   12 to 535 mcg/dL for premenopausal women

-   30 to 260 mcg/dL for postmenopausal women"

So. mine tested DHEA and not the S form. So normal for me would be 0.4 to 3.7 ng/mL, and mine is 3.25 ng/mL. So now if I go by \*this\* page, my levels are right up at the TOP of the range.

Yeah, clearly I need some help here!

### JanSz — 2015-05-27T00:21:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-165485>

Test DHEAs,serum
Consider using guidelines from lef.org
Women 275-400 μg/dL
Eat as many pills as need to get there.
/

### Jill1 — 2015-05-27T06:15:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-165499>

.
a more accurate way to see what the real levels are - use a saliva test. LABRIX is the top rated lab. Saliva is testing free not bound to protein like serum tests. Unlike serum tests, saliva testing represents only hormones actively delivered to receptors in the body. Clinically, it is far more relevant to test these bioavailable hormones and provide an accurate reflection of the body’s active hormone levels.

[https://labrix.secure.force.com/Home](https://labrix.secure.force.com/Home)

### lilly antic — 2015-06-02T19:57:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-166290>

If you test in serum, definitely test for DHEA-S,
DHEA is not stabile and has fluctuations during the day.
25 mg orally can be too much for women, depending how good an absorber you are and how your converting enzymes work, which convert DHEA into androstendione, testosterone, dht and estrogens.
So if you consider to supplement dhea, you should test all these hormones best 3-5 hours after taking it in the morning( after a few weeks on the supplementation). Then you have the highest post dose level. In this way you can monitor the conversion and levels pretty well.
Your symptoms like bad skin are a clear indication that the dosage was definitely too high (-high androgens).
Did at least your libido change in that time?
Maybe 5-10 mg might have been sufficient.
If you try the cream remember that the absorption via skin is different. So 25mg topically is not the same as 25mg orally.

### LisaLearning — 2015-06-02T21:44:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-166306>

Periodically I see people claim that estrogens and progesterone affect sex drive in women, but I think that is wrong and it is all testosterone (and other non-hormonal factors as mentioned early on). Perhaps you should look at testosterone levels (free and and SHBG) directly?

Are you now using DHEA cream, or DHEA sulfate cream? Just a warning that DHEA cream could increase hair growth anywhere you put it that has hair follicles (so that's everywhere except your palms or feet bottoms). Jack says cream that contains DHEA sulfate will not cause the hair growth.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-16T02:17:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-for-libido-increase.14490/post-172078>

> re: testosterone, the only way I've ever foudn to increase that in myself is over the counter DHEA which I presume turns into testosterone in me because I get cystic acne immediately and a raging bad attitude that includes a sense of not caring what happens/being right despite being wrong/being willing to get into physical confrontations.

I already am going to have to go the over the counter, internet ordering route to tend to my own thyroid though so if I have to do it with testosterone I will. I know it's legal to get ampules and needles of it or else my husband wouldn't have done so for so many years, I guess I just have to find out how where how much to take etc at this point.

# Thread: Mercury, Amalgams..Root Canals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-amalgams-root-canals.14525/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Luz — 2015-05-15T12:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-amalgams-root-canals.14525/post-164348>

Hello,

I need some advice from the community because I’m a bit confused on what to do.

3 months ago I removed all the amalgams (6) which I had in my mouth for the past 20 years.

I have been eating 100% kitogenic epi-paleo for the last 10 months and I feel great and I have put lots of muscle on lost some fat.

Now my questions are:

Should I do some mercury test? If yes what kind of test?

Should I do some kind of detox program? If yes could you please point me to right information source?

I have read so many bad things on root canals, and I have one should I remove it?

Thanks for any advices/inputs or for any of your own experience on this subject.

Claudio

### JenRN — 2015-06-27T05:25:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-amalgams-root-canals.14525/post-169702>

Hi Claudio,
I had a hair test once for heavy metals and it was very interesting. You can try this for mercury but make sure the lab will interpret the results for you because mercury can be deceptively low if certain other toxic metals are present. You can read Andrew Cutler for more info. Good luck!

### Gagnrad — 2015-06-27T10:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-amalgams-root-canals.14525/post-169729>

> [Luz said:](/goto/post?id=164348)
>
> Hello,
>
> I need some advice from the community because I’m a bit confused on what to do.
>
> 3 months ago I removed all the amalgams (6) which I had in my mouth for the past 20 years.
>
> I have been eating 100% kitogenic epi-paleo for the last 10 months and I feel great and I have put lots of muscle on lost some fat.
>
> Now my questions are:
>
> Should I do some mercury test? If yes what kind of test?
>
> Should I do some kind of detox program? If yes could you please point me to right information source?

I've heard Jack say in an interview that he thinks it actually better not to disturb existing Hg-Ag amalgam fillings. That's something I've heard before, and is indicative of the fact that removing them, however carefully done, is going to release mercury. And my understanding has been that when people have it done some kind of chelation therapy is usually part of the process.

AFAIK, near infra-red saunas should help you sweat out mercury - and a lot of other things. It might be worth looking into.

# Thread: The Dorito Effect
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-dorito-effect.14568/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2015-05-18T22:06:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-dorito-effect.14568/post-164636>

Has anyone read the book of this name from Mark Schatzger?

[http://www.amazon.com/Dorito-Effect-Surprising-Truth-Flavor/dp/1476724210/](http://www.amazon.com/Dorito-Effect-Surprising-Truth-Flavor/dp/1476724210/)

I ordered a copy a few days ago—though it's only in hardback and rather expensive for me—and am expecting it any day now.

I think the first I heard of this was on Sean Croxton's podcast.

[http://undergroundwellness.com/329/](http://undergroundwellness.com/329/)

I think Sean is very sharp, and one of the best podcasters in Ancestral Health. He asked all the right questions and brought out some interesting material from Schatzger. (He also did a really good interview with Dr. Perlmutter recently, possibly better than Robb Wolf's though Robb is no slouch.) I found a couple of other interviews that Schatzger has done and there were a few different details that came out, but overall they were nowhere near as good as Sean's

Anyway, the book. You look at the cover and the word "Doritos" and you might think is the Stefan G hyperpalatability stuff. But it's much deeper than that.

Schatzger is very interested in the manipulation of flavour, but he is equally interested in the _loss_ of flavour in traditional foods. (And not for him the SG notion that what we all need is bland food.)

I'd recommend anyone who hasn't heard about Schatzger's book to try the podcast. What he said is that most psychologists believe that humans are "wired for calories". The strange disconnect is that if you go and ask animal scientists you'll find that they believe that animals are wired for _taste_. There are many experiments that do seem to show this. And, if that's so, why would humans be any different? There's your million dollar question. Actually, I think you only have to ask yourself: "Why do we have taste?" (Interestingly, there's a couple of comments along that line in _Nourishing Traditions_—smart lady, Sally Fallon.)

I'll give an example.

One scientist made sheep phosphorous-deficient. He then put out feed that was flavoured with coconut and just before they ate it fed a phosphorous solution into their stomachs. On other occasions he put out food flavoured with something else and just before they ate it pumped pure water in through the tube. Eventually, he just put out both feeds ... and the sheep always went to the coconut-flavoured food. Why? Because they associated the coconut flavour with the phosphorous they needed. It's not that they knew what phosphorous was, of course, but they knew how they FELT and associated the feeling of wellness with the flavour. Now you might think that it was just that sheep like coconut. He thought of that. He reversed the procedure with a second set of sheep, and that flock went for the other flavour, because he'd associated the other flavour with phosphorous. Several other experiments suggest the same.

Even more interesting, one scientist did experiments with self-feeding toddlers. Now, AFAICT, she only put out really good wholefoods, so there's a level of knowledge about the world—where to get this stuff, how to prepare it, etc.—going into there, but the toddlers just took whatever they wanted:

> Our first \[guess\] would be … that the toddlers stuffed themselves every day on the foods they found to be the sweetest and avoided the other food items. However, the reverse was quite true – they tended to be drawn to the foods that best nourished them. During growth spurts, they gravitated towards more protein. During periods of high activity, they ate foods that were more dense in carbs and fat.
>
> What is even more astonishing about this study is that when the toddlers were experiencing health challenges they responded by making very specific food choices. ...

[http://www.grassfedexchange.com/article/58/](http://www.grassfedexchange.com/article/58/)

I think Schatzger has it be right. "Wired for calories"? Nonsense! And now I think about it Jack has always talked of the value of intuition. Well, there you go. Goats have it, toddlers have it (or did in the 30s, at any rate). The interesting question becomes: "Why don't we?" The answer seems to be that real foods have lost their flavour because of intensive farming, and artificially-flavoured, nutrient-denuded foods just confuse the hell out of our brains. Schatzger even suggests that government-inspired initiatives to insist on "fortification" of nutrient-poor foods may be mis-firing, in that people may unconsciously associate the nutrition (hence effects on their system) they're getting from an added nutrient with the flavour of a processed food they happen to meet it in.

I wonder if the disruption of circadian rhythms might also have some kind of effect on our response to flavour and disrupt all this too. AFAIK Schatzger has said nothing about that, and I wondered what Jack—supposing he agreed that we are wired for flavour—would say.

Finally, this is slightly off at a tangent, but I was interested to see that Professor Michael Crawford was mentioned in a review of Schatzger's book. I'll quote in full, because I think almost anything Crawford says is probably worth thinking about:

> In 2009, a seminal figure in British nutrition named Michael Crawford, of London’s Institute of Brain Chemistry and Human Nutrition, compared chickens of today with chickens of times past. Humans aren’t the only ones who are way fatter than they used to be. Back in 1870, a 100-gram (31/2-ounce) piece of chicken contained just under 4 grams of fat. By 1970, that number had risen to 8.6 grams, and by 2004 chicken was packing more than 20 grams. As Crawford dryly noted, “While chicken was at one time a lean, low-fat food, it is no longer.” Less dryly, he asked, “Does eating obesity cause obesity in the consumer?”

[http://www.theglobeandmail.com/life...or-undoing-the-dorito-effect/article24151856/](http://www.theglobeandmail.com/life/food-and-wine/food-trends/the-flavour-factor-undoing-the-dorito-effect/article24151856/)

The more I think about this one, the more I think it's an interesting question. I don't mean that there's "too much fat" on a serving of these chickens (although the chickens themselves are certainly too fat: they've been bred to be obese.) I don't even mean the omega-3 : omega-6 ratio will be off. I simply mean: an obese chicken is a sick chicken, and should we be eating sick animals? I'm sure Palaeolithic Man never did. Schatzger says in one of these interviews that a chicken by the time it's slaughtered is like a human baby that would have gone from average human birthweight to weighing 600lbs in just a few months. Folks, This Ain't Normal, as Joel would say.

### nicld — 2015-05-18T23:06:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-dorito-effect.14568/post-164639>

Interesting. I have noticed a difference in tomatoes. The ones that I grow myself taste so much better then any I can buy in the store. Just have to wait 2 months now before I can eat my grown ones again.

### caroline — 2015-05-19T02:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-dorito-effect.14568/post-164647>

You are back Mike! Yay

sounds like an very interesting book .....I am so far behind in my reading

### Jude — 2015-05-19T03:17:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-dorito-effect.14568/post-164649>

Where have you been? Welcome back!

### Gagnrad — 2015-05-19T20:51:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-dorito-effect.14568/post-164732>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=164639)
>
> Interesting. I have noticed a difference in tomatoes. The ones that I grow myself taste so much better then any I can buy in the store. Just have to wait 2 months now before I can eat my grown ones again.

Yeah. The book arrived this morning, and I'm not far into it yet. But I've already encountered a section on tomatoes.

Schatzker—I misspelt him above—seems to want to erect the flavour principle into a complete explanation of obesity, and there's some fluff in the book around people "blaming" different food elements: carbs, fat, sugar, etc. and he says that none of this fits. Oh, yeah, nothing wrong with sugar … just calories and no nutrition even if there weren't questions around its effect on insulin, on the liver and so on. But he's inconsistent, so he does say that modern chickens are fed on nutrient-denuded but very high-carb diets to fatten them (with enough vitamins and minerals added to the feed to just keep them alive.) Chickens that U.S. industry doesn't want, by the way—infant males of specialist egg-laying breeds—are sometimes fed live into mincing machines.

But if you ignore that element of obesity over-theorising, there are absolute nuggets of gold in the book. As I say, once you begin to think about some of this it becomes very difficult to believe that we are "wired for calories" rather than taste.

Anyway, on the tomatoes. See pp. 27–33. He says an engineer at Monsanto was aware that supermarket tomatoes taste of nothing much and thought the problem might be that they're picked when still unripe for ease of transport over long distances and storage. (I've often heard this mooted.) Monsanto spent $10 million putting alien genes in the tomato in order to develop one that would ripen really slowly—so that it could be picked when near ripe and still not go off in transit. This didn't solve the problem: they still tasted like cardboard.

The real problem seems to be that tomatoes, like virtually everything else, have been massively overbred. They've gone for yield, appearance, colour, a thicker skin (so they don't damage easily in transit) … everything other than flavour. Colour is an interesting one: Schatzker says that tomatoes were originally striped with green, but by the 1950s that had been bred out purely on grounds of appearance! (I have seen tomatoes sold as "Russian tomatoes" that were striped like this, and actually they were quite good, though not spectacular.)

Yield is rather like the chicken problem. Just as chicken used to be a fraction of the size but tasted of something, tomato plants are now higher yielders but blander. The plant used to be 12′ tall and carry 4 to 5 ripe tomatoes at a time. Now they're an easier-to-handle 6′ but carry around 10 at once. The plant just can't cope with shoving enough nutrition/taste into each one. There's just more water there.

The solution, I guess, is not only to grow them yourself if you can practising organic gardening, using really good compost. I suppose that the only real solution is to get hold of heirloom varieties. AFAICT, the oldest form of whatever plant you can get is probably the best bet if you want it to actually f---ing taste of something.

I was reminded of these people who did reconstruction archaeology by rebuilding a Stuart-era farm in Wales—buildings and farmland:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/372/dp/1905223137/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/372/dp/1905223137/)

What Stuart Peachy did, when planting the orchard, was to get _as many_ varieties of fruit trees from the period that were still obtainable as he could. That, he said, was what real farmers of the period did. Apart from anything else, you didn't want everything ripening at once: if you had half a dozen varieties of apple-tree instead of one, the crops would likely be staggered, as they'd ripen at different rates, so the farm could have a supply of fresh fruit for a longer period. Some, of course, would be sold, but much of what was grown would be to serve the people living on the farm themselves.

Against this background one can see why Sally Fallon said that one of the books that was most influential on her was this:

[http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb\_sb\_n...=stripbooks&field-keywords=small+is+beautiful](http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb_sb_noss_1?url=search-alias%3Dstripbooks&field-keywords=small+is+beautiful)

It's that problem of _scale_ that Joel Salatin has pointed to so often, isn't it? Stuff is picked unripe and bred to be uniform, because it's grown by an over-sized outfit that's shipping it hundreds of miles to the outlets of an oversized grocery chain. They would want everything to ripen at once, because they're not feeding themselves and a local community: at the scale they're doing it on, it would pay them to use a single variety so that everything comes at once, thus allowing them to hire labour or machinery for a shorter time. One type of crop, as well as one variety, is probably all they're growing: mono-cropping. And this brings pest problems, and therefore necessitates agrochemicals. And the whole thing, as Joel points out, is based on cheap fuel. If fuel were just a little more expensive, small-scale and local would be cheaper than driving stuff all over the country.

# Thread: Maybe we'll morph so we'll have 4 copies of DNA like this bacteria:

### Penny — 2015-05-19T06:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maybe-well-morph-so-well-have-4-copies-of-dna-like-this-bacteria.14571/post-164663>

"There are even bacteria living in the radioactive waste of nuclear power plants. The bacteria, Deinococcus radiodurans can stand extremely high amounts of radioactivity. For a cell, the greatest threat from radiation is that it can break the DNA. As a defense mechanism, the d. radiodurans has 4 copies of its DNA in each cell, and the ability to use the other copies to repair a broken chromosome."

I'm in a biofilm class on coursera.org and the opening lecture started talking about some bacteria that could handle radiation... if I were a scientist, I would studying this little darlin'...

The course just started and goes for 6 weeks - fantastic so far - easy to understand too -
[https://class.coursera.org/bacteria-001/lecture/11](https://class.coursera.org/bacteria-001/lecture/11)

# Thread: how significant is altitude in mito function

**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Aerose91 — 2015-05-19T07:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-significant-is-altitude-in-mito-function.14572/post-164665>

I'm moving from Connecticut to the desert to do a mold avoidance stint before starting antibiotics for bartonella encephalopathy. I also have M.E.
As soon as I hit 3200 in the mountains feet I started having exponentially more mental problems that only increased as the altitude did. By 4500 i couldn't open doorknobs and by 6800 i couldn't speak and had partial paralysis. By the time I got below 3200 i started to clear up a bit but was still hospitalized with "high altitude cerebral edema"
My guess is that since i have so much existing brain inflammation, the slightest bit of pressure decrease allowed it to swell even further all the way to the point of HACE at merely 4500 feet.
My question is, since I felt a significant improvement when I crossed 3200 feet does that mean I'm out of the woods anywhere under 3200? Or is it just sensible that the lower the elevation the better. I'm obviously trying to reduce inflammation here so is the lower the elevation the better for your mitochindria?

### seanb4 — 2015-05-19T14:01:25+0300

NUTRITIONAL ELEMENTS:
Calcium 49 (22-97)

Sodium 3 (4-36) LOW
Potassium 2 (2-24)
Copper 1.2 (0.9-3.9)

Iron 1.1 (0.5-1.6)

Selenium 0.05 (0.03-0.18)
Boron 0.02 (0.02-0.91)
Cobalt .001 (.001-.003)
Molybdenum .003 (.003-.008)
Sulfur 4288 (3546-5336)

TOXIC METALS HIGH:

Mecury 0.25

SIGNIFICANT RATIOS:

Na/K 1.5 (1.4-3.4)

Zn/Cu 14.17 (4-12) HIGH

Fe/Cu .92 (.2-1.6)

What seems to stand out to me is my calcium ratio being way too high compared to other electrolytes. Also my minerals all seem to be at the bottom of the reference range.

I think the calcium ratio could be playing a big part in my heart problems. Does this have something to do with calcium efflux/EMFs or is there something else at play?

The arsenic and mercury levels could be from canned mackerel (been eating it daily for ~a year)

The report says my mineral profile indicates lack of stomach acid which I have long suspected but I am unsure how they came to this conclusion...

Any suggestions/thoughts appreciated!

### Sue-UK — 2015-05-20T10:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair-mineral-analysis-help.14600/post-164774>

Hi Sean

I think of hair as a secondary excretion route. No mercury or arsenic in the hair could mean low levels in the body, or it could mean there is any amount, but the body can't use the hair to help shift it. So having mercury and arsenic in the hair sample could be a good sign.  As for the other results, what minerals were excreted to help bring out the metals? Is the excess of some a good sign (body getting rid of what it doesn't want), is the low readings on others also a good sign (body keeping what it needs).

Thinking of hair as an antenna, is your body trying to optimise the structure of the antenna to improve its function or to protect the organism as a whole, within the environment you are in? N=1.

I.e. is a hair sample simply a reflection of the body's best response to what the cells are sensing?

### seanb4 — 2015-05-20T11:47:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair-mineral-analysis-help.14600/post-164777>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=164774)
>
> Hi Sean
>
> I think of hair as a secondary excretion route. No mercury or arsenic in the hair could mean low levels in the body, or it could mean there is any amount, but the body can't use the hair to help shift it. So having mercury and arsenic in the hair sample could be a good sign.  As for the other results, what minerals were excreted to help bring out the metals? Is the excess of some a good sign (body getting rid of what it doesn't want), is the low readings on others also a good sign (body keeping what it needs).
>
> Thinking of hair as an antenna, is your body trying to optimise the structure of the antenna to improve its function or to protect the organism as a whole, within the environment you are in? N=1.
>
> I.e. is a hair sample simply a reflection of the body's best response to what the cells are sensing?
>
>

Hi Sue,

So are you saying that hair is used as a detox pathway? If this is the case then the results obtained wouldn't be that useful as you say because certain things could be in high amounts because they are getting excreted, etc.

I was under the impression that the hair would be like a snapshot of whats in your cells currently...

It would make sense if the body excreted excess minerals through the hair though.

### Sue-UK — 2015-05-20T12:18:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair-mineral-analysis-help.14600/post-164780>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=164777)
>
> Hi Sue,
>
> So are you saying that hair is used as a detox pathway? If this is the case then the results obtained wouldn't be that useful as you say because certain things could be in high amounts because they are getting excreted, etc.
>
> I was under the impression that the hair would be like a snapshot of whats in your cells currently...
>
> It would make sense if the body excreted excess minerals through the hair though.

I think it is a detox pathway, and what is excreted may be related to light.

Apparently different frequencies of light stimulate different enzymes, which may account for what is being excreted. We may excrete more of different things at different seasons, in line with the light cycle. Or it could be a way of getting metals away from the blood flow in the scalp, or a metal that is in a particular form. Faster growing hair might also encourage the body to throw more off. Or it may just be a back up system if one of the major elimination routes needs help.

I don't see it as a snapshot of what's in the cells, it could just as easily be what the cells are managing to keep out .... Having the benefit of the seafood, but the body having the right nutrients in the seafood, to be able to move what it doesn't need out maybe ..? All sorts of possibilities .......

Bilirubin Negative
Urobilinogen, Semi Qn 0.2
Nitrite, urine Negative
Leukocyte Screen Negative
Hemoglobin Screen Negative
Urine Creatinine 71.0 mg dL
ProteinCreatinine .04
Urine Glucose Negative

Thank you!

### Danny — 2015-05-25T03:05:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-to-back-blood-work-any-thoughts.14605/post-165254>

### Butters — 2015-05-20T12:30:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-with-bioimpedance-analysis.14623/post-164782>

Hey I am working in the sports departement of my university and have access to a INBODY 720 device for body composition measureing.
The devices showes intra- and extracellular water content. Are these usefull facts to determine if im well hydrated?
Latest measurement shows I am well above the norm for both. Well of course it would be interesting what the norm is like.

But I think for no money it is a pretty good test. Of course I can check bodyfat and musclemass to.

### nonchalant — 2015-05-20T18:01:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-with-bioimpedance-analysis.14623/post-164808>

Sounds good, Butters. But the norm might be for other dehydrated folks living in this microwaved world.
How do the results compare to typical tests, like BUN/creatinine ratio?

### Butters — 2015-05-20T23:35:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-with-bioimpedance-analysis.14623/post-164848>

# Thread: Daylight vs. Actual Sunshine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-20T16:18:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/post-164791>

So I guess this is somewhat of a biohack! I posted before that we are planning to move south. Even in the north I know I do better over summer, but I've been struggling with knowing whether that's from getting real, legit sunshine or from longer daylight hours. I keep thinking that moving south really isn't going to give me any more actual daylight than what I'm already getting. One of my main problems I have is with temperature regulation. I tend to get really hot - especially my neck and upper body - in the evenings. I've finally realized this almost always happens when the sun goes down, which is why winter is so much worse for me. It starts so much earlier - around 5 PM and lasts all evening. In the summer, especially now that it's staying light out til 8:30 or so, it doesn't start until much later.

So, I spent the last few days mostly inside - not getting a lot of sun at all and I found that my body temp issues were still much improved over the winter. Normally over the summer I am earthing and getting sun 6-8 hours at least each day, but the last few days I wasn't doing that and I still was better. This makes me think it's the longer daylight that is really the catalyst to my improvements and not so much the sunshine.

Which of course begs the question once again - will going south to Florida really be beneficial for me? I'm only going to get an extra 30 minutes of daylight versus what I'm getting here - not really anything significant.

ANy thoughts on this?

### nonchalant — 2015-05-20T17:51:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/post-164802>

I forget where you are moving from, Looking, but it isn't just 30 more minutes of sunlight. The intensity of the sunshine is much stronger.

You seem to be seeing that your internal temperature regulation is a function of energy, thus the evening problems for you. Do you currently CT? That helps you regulate your temp, whether it is summer or winter.

I pay close attention to my perception of body heat/cold. It is often the first sign that I need to do something to raise my energy levels.

### nonchalant — 2015-05-20T17:54:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/post-164803>

As to your question, it's got to be both, duration and intensity of sunlight. Both will affect you in different ways. And since everyone is different, we all see the effects differently.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-20T18:32:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/post-164815>

Thanks for the input nonchalant. Yes, I have been CTing as much as I can tolerate. With my body temp issues it is pretty difficult for me, but I take 1-2 cold baths per day with the water temp being around 55 degrees. I generally only last 15-20 minutes though! I'm going to keep trying for longer duration.

### shah78 — 2015-05-20T18:38:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/post-164817>

I've been comparing New England to Florida for twenty years. Slam dunk. Florida. When we drive down to Florida each fall, I feel better as soon as I hit New Jersey. Placebo? Really? New Jersey is no placebo!

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-20T21:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daylight-vs-actual-sunshine.14630/post-164839>

Interesting @[shah78](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1152/) ! We used to visit FL 1-2 times a year in the past before I got sick and I always called it "my happy place!" I ALWAYS felt better there, but the last couple of trips (after getting ill) haven't had quite the positive boost for me that I was hoping to experience. I know it's not going to happen overnight though . . . my expectations were just a bit too high


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: I HAD THIS!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-had-this.14632/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nittygrittydanny — 2015-05-20T18:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-had-this.14632/post-164812>

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/concussion.14631/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/concussion.14631/)

I got hit from behind in a football game. went down, hands had pins and needles, went away. Had chiropractor adjustments to relieve pain, worked for awhile. got hit in the mask with a foul ball in a fast pitch softball game a couple years later, saw stars, shooting pain in left arm. Got an MRI that my chiropractor viewed and said "GO GET SURGERY ASAP".

Had to remove C 5&6 disc.

my symptoms were classic concussion, never knew, but looking back I wish i'd known!

> k\_\_Bacteria;
>
> p\_\_Bacteroidetes;c\_\_Bacteroidia;o\_\_Bacteroidales;f\_\_S24-7;g\_\_ 0.000065

Received my British Gut results and the data seems lacking and I'm not sure how to decipher it.

From what I have gathered my Bacteroidetes outnumber my Firmicutes by quite a lot which, on the one hand, appears to be a good sign as the flip is associated with obesity, on the other, it is associated with c**eliac disease**.

My Actinobacteria and Verrucomicrobia are a lot lower than average whereas Fusobacteria is higher.

Not sure what else to really take from the test, it only measures at the genus level so it's impossible to tell if the strain is a pathogen or not.

My own, separate from this test, thoughts as to what could be causing my gut problems is 02 entering my gut thanks to a shitty lower esophagus. I know it's bad because I can feel water/food reflux back up after I eat eat / drink / bend over. I also think I have very low stomach acidity. I think this because the reflux never burns, I have more issues with protein, I can take boat loads of betaine hcl without burning, and my hair analysis indicates a low salt level.

Any thoughts?

# Thread: Anyone had success w/ DGL=deglycyrrhizinated licorice?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Danny — 2015-05-25T03:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165253>

I know Kruse suggests this in his leaky gut Rx. I was wondering if anyone has used this as a temporary hand up sort of thing. Thanks!

### Peertje80 — 2015-05-25T12:01:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165277>

I don't know what dgl is. However my granny always told me to eat some bay leaf licorice or liquorice licorice (difficult translation from Dutch, zoethoutdrop) when having a tummy ache or sore throat. So must be something to it.

We call it drop. Foreigners don't really seem to know it or like it for that matter. At average Dutch people eat about [2 kilo's per person](http://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drop) a year of this stuff. We are always warned not to eat to much because it can raise blood pressure.

### Danny — 2015-05-25T14:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165283>

Interesting! I will look into this bay leaf licorice. It's supposed to help produce protective mucus secretions for the GI tract. Thanks!

### Lahelada — 2015-05-25T15:29:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165289>

German Grandmas are quite gezellig with Dutch grandmas....ugh ...Lakritze . I read up a bit on it though . Apparently in Europe it is widely used from Arab countries right up to Finland. Medicinal uses include curing peptic ulcers etc . As the degly...etc taks away the component that is questionable for some states of health I would say try it. I give you this cultural excursionat as 10 people may tell you it does nothing and you might be No 11 who it really helps. N =1 and all that .

### Peertje80 — 2015-05-25T17:54:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165299>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=165289)
>
> German Grandmas are quite gezellig with Dutch grandmas....ugh ...Lakritze . I read up a bit on it though . Apparently in Europe it is widely used from Arab countries right up to Finland. Medicinal uses include curing peptic ulcers etc . As the degly...etc taks away the component that is questionable for some states of health I would say try it. I give you this cultural excursionat as 10 people may tell you it does nothing and you might be No 11 who it really helps. N =1 and all that .

Sehr gut das gezelligheid. Lakritze... aber nur das schwarze Gold der Süßholzwurzel. Too bad today liquorice has to come in multi colors/flavors. We eat it like candy nowadays. Doesn't have anything to do with the traditional thing that turned your mouth straight black.

> [Danny said:](/goto/post?id=165283)
>
> Interesting! I will look into this bay leaf licorice. It's supposed to help produce protective mucus secretions for the GI tract. Thanks!

Bay leaf licorice I really love it. It is quite bitter tasting though. Never knew that about it. But thinking about it... hmm yes it does. But don't eat too much of it, you get constipated.

### Lahelada — 2015-05-25T18:12:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165301>

Trust me Lakritze comes black and bitter....Come to think of it it maybe why I am here. I nibbled off the colourful sugary bits and spat out the black stuff...lol.
Sorry Danny for hijacking this post. I willbe good now.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-26T06:53:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165378>

I mentioned the topic of DGL to @[Matty\_M](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/11546/) in his journal -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ould-use-some-advice.12816/page-9#post-164863](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/15-year-old-who-could-use-some-advice.12816/page-9#post-164863)

I have also used it before, to go good effect (even with >4g of DHA a day)

DGL works through Glycyrrhetinic acid, which prolongs the activity of prostaglandins PGE-2 and PGF-2α -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enoxolone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enoxolone)

Firstly, Prostaglandins need a bunch of factors to work -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostaglandin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostaglandin) .

(a) They need to be synthesized (usually not a problem)

(b) then they need to be transported out of the site of synthesis by the prostaglandin transporter protein (PGT). Anytime we hear "transport" and "protein" we need to think of the electromagnetic force, which is highly altered in a bad gut.

(c1) PGE-2 needs to be synthesized by Prostaglandin E Synthase. The "-ase" suffix means this is an enzyme, ie: needs favourable EMF environment for proton tunnelling to work (not a good assumption in a bad gut) -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostaglandin\_E\_synthase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostaglandin_E_synthase)

(c2) PGF-2a also needs to be synthesized by enzymes, and needs S-proline specifically as a catalyst -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proline](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proline) . While S-proline is the more naturally occurring form, the molecule is highly polar, and is likely prone to oxidation (not good if the gut is full of oxygen)

(d) Then these prostaglandins need to bind to receptors, which are G-coupled protein receptors -- example: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostaglandin\_F\_receptor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostaglandin_F_receptor) . G-coupled protein receptors receive lots of inputs from other G-coupled protein receptors (including the ones found in your eyes, activated by blue-light sensitive Melanopsin). Changes in receptor activity can be up- or down-regulated according to the overall circadian sensing of all G-coupled protein receptors.

(e) Furthermore, the mechanisms of DGL do nothing to reduce the excess oxygen found in a bad gut, though it may aid the process provided the rest of the circadian sensing is right.

That's just a long and specific way of saying that this is complex coupled shit  which can be affected by many different pathways, and can therefore break in many different ways.

So is it going to be effective? Plausibly, but you're running up against a lot of factors.

Is it going to be harmful? Plausibly, though I think the chances are low.

....

### Matty_M — 2015-05-26T17:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-w-dgl-deglycyrrhizinated-licorice.14698/post-165430>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=165378)
>

G-coupled protein receptors receive lots of inputs from other G-coupled protein receptors (including the ones found in your eyes, activated by blue-light sensitive Melanopsin). # Thread: Polluted River CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/polluted-river-ct.14703/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-05-26T04:33:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/polluted-river-ct.14703/post-165373>

I went to the shore yesterday and today and spent a good bit of time in the water, and felt phenomenal. It is amazing. It was the Jersey shore and the water was too cold for most people (~55 F) but I LOVED IT! Also, my propensity to sunburn is decreasing for sure in my venture to optimal.

I am thinking about taking a train (high EMF- over 50 milligauss for 1.5 hours each way) to the jersey shore from philly each weekend going forward because it is only $10. Would the benefit of some hours in the ocean be "worth" the negatives from the train?

Also, I do have the schuykill river, as well as a few creeks near me. The issue is that the creeks all have s good bit of street runoff, such that there is little life in them. The schukill probably only has mutant life, if any, due to the pollution from industry.

Given these options, what seems the best for CT (for someone not optimal)? For the shore, I would probably take a bus actually because the EMF is a good bit lower, and it is less of a faraday cage I believe, and has fewer passengers with fewer antennas on them.

Any help is greatly appreciated.

Thanks,

\- Matt

### Lahelada — 2015-05-26T16:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/polluted-river-ct.14703/post-165425>

I would steer clear off the polluted river for a while. My avatar was taken with my legs dangling in the polluted arm of the River Plate,think heavy metals and sewer water. It is fairly still water and I felt sluggish for a good while after. I did the same in the middle of the river, but with no ill effect, on the contrary it felt good.
I would try the trip to the ocean. Live as optimal as you can the days before, note how you feel and see if you feel as good , better or worse on Monday.
I recommend the ocean as you get more out of the visit than CT and sun.
[http://www.newscientist.com/article...incredible-diversity--and-human-likeness.html](http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn27578-oceans-microbiome-has-incredible-diversity--and-human-likeness.html)

### Matty_M — 2015-05-26T17:18:27+0300

It is fairly still water and I felt sluggish for a good while after. I did the same in the middle of the river, but with no ill effect, on the contrary it felt good.

# Thread: Movement with CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/movement-with-ct.14729/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-26T15:05:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/movement-with-ct.14729/post-165413>

Is CT more effective with movement or does it not matter? Just wondering as right now I could sit still in a 56-degree tub (but only last 20 minutes) or swim in a 69-degree saltwater pool and last at least twice as long and getting sunshine at the same time. I thought I read somewhere that movement makes CT more effective, but I can't remember where and can't find it now.

### seanb4 — 2015-05-26T17:25:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/movement-with-ct.14729/post-165428>

when you are moving around you are exposing yourself to cooler water (if you stay still the water around you warms up) however you are generating heat via movement (so not thermogenesis) so that might counter the effects somewhat and make it more difficult to reduce skin temp.

I would say do the pool, the sun is a big benefit and I'm guessing it will be more fun (tolerable mentally) swimming around.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-27T18:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/movement-with-ct.14729/post-165565>

I hadn't thought about the movement warming me up, because honestly I don't feel any warmer when doing that! I actually feel the cold air swirling more around me, but I'm an oddball when it comes to all these things, so that doesn't surprise me!

### Sue-UK — 2015-05-27T19:35:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/movement-with-ct.14729/post-165569>

Movement can make you lose heat, because of things like exposing the femoral triangle, armpits etc. If the water is icy cold I need to hunch up to avoid hypothermia, if its warmer I move more to feel the cold.

Just wondering as right now I could sit still in a 56-degree tub (but only last 20 minutes) or swim in a 69-degree saltwater pool and last at least twice as long and getting sunshine at the same time. I thought I read somewhere that movement makes CT more effective, but I can't remember where and can't find it now.

I have also wondered the same thing as I can also handle CT longer if I swim. Less boring that way too.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-06-21T06:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/movement-with-ct.14729/post-169116>

> [lookingforanswers said:](/goto/post?id=165565)
>
> I hadn't thought about the movement warming me up, because honestly I don't feel any warmer when doing that! I actually feel the cold air swirling more around me, but I'm an oddball when it comes to all these things, so that doesn't surprise me!

# Thread: Best way to ask questions (?)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-30T21:49:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-165968>

I am just absolutely fascinated with this stuff. But of course the more you learn the more questions pop up. I have so many questions but I want to make sure that I'm asking them using the proper "etiquette". I don't want to drive Jack nuts asking questions that he has already answered a gazillion times.
Any ideas on the best way to have 'starfish' answer questions? Is it 'okay' to start a new thread for every question I have or is that kind of bad practice?

### nonchalant — 2015-05-30T22:28:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-165970>

Sure, it's ok to start new threads for every question, Captain.
Getting questions answered is kinda hit-or-miss here. You have to get the attention of others. One thing I often do is to start my post with an attention-grabbing sentence. Nothing outlandish (usually) but I try to put the most important part of my post first. Then when others scan "Recent Activity" of the forum, they will see that sentence, and might choose to read more of your post.

But also you have to get used to not getting an answer. I think less than half of my questions have been answered, or even responded to. Also people have asked Jack some good questions, and not gotten a response, probably because he wasn't ready to dig into that area of knowledge with us yet. But a newbie will ask it months/years later, and Jack quickly responds with something brilliant, often using a topic that he has recently posted on. Timing is important. Or someone will ask Jack a question, and he does answer, using knowledge we have no background on. So his reply just goes over our heads.

I think Jack is trying to teach us that the most important part is asking the question, not necessarily getting the answer.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-31T01:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-165989>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=165970)
>
> Sure, it's ok to start new threads for every question, Captain.
> Getting questions answered is kinda hit-or-miss here. You have to get the attention of others. One thing I often do is to start my post with an attention-grabbing sentence. Nothing outlandish (usually) but I try to put the most important part of my post first. Then when others scan "Recent Activity" of the forum, they will see that sentence, and might choose to read more of your post.
>
> But also you have to get used to not getting an answer. I think less than half of my questions have been answered, or even responded to. Also people have asked Jack some good questions, and not gotten a response, probably because he wasn't ready to dig into that area of knowledge with us yet. But a newbie will ask it months/years later, and Jack quickly responds with something brilliant, often using a topic that he has recently posted on. Timing is important. Or someone will ask Jack a question, and he does answer, using knowledge we have no background on. So his reply just goes over our heads.
>
> I think Jack is trying to teach us that the most important part is asking the question, not necessarily getting the answer.

Thanks for your reply,
Yeah that's kind of what I've been doing..what you said about starting a thread with a title that grabs people's attention. I just wasn't sure if that was the right way of doing it.
I noticed that they would usually get answered much faster that way.
I know a lot of the info is in the blogs. I try to read them as much as I can but I find I can get overwhelmed by the info very fast. Also it seems that Jack can make some simple mistakes in his English that can confuse me because I'm not sure if he has made a mistake or not. It seems to be quite frequent, like almost every paragraph! I worry that people may not take his blogs seriously because of the 'lack of editing'.

### DavidL — 2015-05-31T02:04:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-165990>

Don't get hung up on editing....have you ever typed something really fast? Not sure exactly how he works but you must train yourself to look for answers, not grammar.

Maybe the errors are there to teach us....to not get stuck on what doesn't matter in favor of sticking to what matters.
My 2 cents.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-31T02:10:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-165993>

> [David Limacher said:](/goto/post?id=165990)
>
> Don't get hung up on editing....have you ever typed something really fast? Not sure exactly how he works but you must train yourself to look for answers, not grammar.
>
> Maybe the errors are there to teach us....to not get stuck on what doesn't matter in favor of sticking to what matters.
> My 2 cents.

Yeah maybe it's better to just read and absorb what we can and not worry about 'getting it' right away. I know I do that with the webinars. Eventually I start to get it

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-31T02:13:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-165995>

It's best to let Jack just do what he is doing so he can stay in his comfort zone so his ideas flow out better.

### Peertje80 — 2015-05-31T09:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-ask-questions.14910/post-166013>

Consider: "The best teachers are those who show you were to look, but don't tell you what to see" (Trenfor).
