# Thread: No food or water for 60 years?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-food-or-water-for-60-years.16367/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jenc288 — 2015-07-12T06:06:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-food-or-water-for-60-years.16367/post-171731>

Is he tapping into photosynthesis?

### Gagnrad — 2015-08-03T21:32:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-food-or-water-for-60-years.16367/post-173872>

Something I came across just a couple of days ago that relates to this.

This practice is mentioned by the sociologist, Kyriacos Markides -

[http://umaine.edu/sociology/faculty-and-staff/kyriacos-markides/](http://umaine.edu/sociology/faculty-and-staff/kyriacos-markides/)

\- as being claimed to have been done for a year by one of the healers on Cyprus whom he studied, on and off, for a decade or so.

This man, Kostas, said he ate virtually nothing for a year - occasionally some watermelon, but nothing else.

Markides, who was himself brought up on Cyprus before emigrating to the U.S. obviously has the linguistic and cultural ability to do this work. In another sense, however, he was like a fish out of water, because his academic education in sociology in the U.S. had taught him to accept only materialist explanations. He says now that he has seen too many unusual phenomena over the years for him to be able to take materialist (or, as some philosophers would prefer to say "physicalist") accounts of the world too seriously. At the same time, his background in sociology gives him a certain distance. There's a sense of suspended judgment about what he sees, which makes him in some ways an ideal person to write about these matters.

I imagine people like Isaac Asimov, Carl Sagan and the Amazing Randi would be appalled by his even entertaining the possibility that this kind of thing is possible. I suppose that some people, reading what he wrote, would even be purple with anger by now. But Markides just reports in this dispassionate manner, not eager to believe in the unusual phenomena that he hears about (and sees) but finding himself unable honestly to categorically reject them either. His books make interesting reading.

Here's the book in which this account appears:

[http://www.amazon.com/Fire-Heart-Arkana-Kyriacos-Markides/dp/0140192859/](http://www.amazon.com/Fire-Heart-Arkana-Kyriacos-Markides/dp/0140192859/)

What Kostas _said_ to Markides was that he was using energy from light. He said it took the practice of a special meditation for him to able to do this.

We're, of course, left with the question of whether Kostas - or the Indian man mentioned above - were telling the truth, or were themselves deluded.

Be that as it may, there's an interesting cultural difference here. The Cypriot healers were Christians, albeit very unconventional ones, and consequently tended to have a different attitude towards psychic abilities. Kostas by his own account (the reader must interpret this as he or she chooses) came to believe that psychic abilities were _only_ to be used for healing - which fits that cultural background - and not to be used for any other purpose (and most certainly not for money or fame or power). There are different attitudes in India. Kostas also felt that it was inadmissible to "scandalise" people. He was certainly scandalising his father-in-law. His father-in-law, who was a doctor (and who, therefore, "knew" that what he was doing - or was supposed to be doing (take your pick) - was impossible) was disturbed by it and assumed Kostas was eating secretly.

If such a phenomenon _can_ happen, then we're left with the statement by someone who claims to be able to do it, that it takes a meditative technique to do it. (Notice that the Iceman says the same about some of his more demanding techniques, which he seems to think go beyond mere adaptation.)

For strict materialists we're in uncomfortable territory here. _Mind_ makes a disconcerting entrance. (Not that it isn't always lurking in the background, anyway, because if you make a subject-object distinction (as opposed, perhaps to a polarity) and push everything you're uncomfortable with into the subject … well, the subject's still _there_, isn't it?)

People must make their own minds up about this. In any event, Markides' books make interesting reading.

It is deceptively simple and yet has such far reaching implications....

It has been such a long time since we had the "oxy" thread ..... we aren't getting boring are we????

It is so liberating and freeing ..... and it changes everything - and I do mean everything

I used to spend so much time trying to figure out what Jack was really saying ....all the things he is teaching us.

Now, sometimes, I have to just say WTF .....I get the overall message ... all be it in very simple terms and it is time to apply, apply, apply!

What very simple and yet very profound things have changed your life here at jackkruse.com?

# Thread: Mad Bear
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mad-bear.16492/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2015-07-18T16:39:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mad-bear.16492/post-172255>

Does anyone here know this book - Doug Boyd's _Mad Bear_ ?

[http://www.amazon.com/Mad-Bear-Healing-American-Medicine/dp/0671759450/](http://www.amazon.com/Mad-Bear-Healing-American-Medicine/dp/0671759450/)

Mad Bear was a Iroquois (specifically Tuscarora) medicine man. Here he is meeting the Dalai Lama:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I don't know how to take all of this. I like to read almost everything I read with a kind of "willing suspension of disbelief". (How would even know how, say, a nineteenth-century Frenchman saw things unless you made some effort to try to see things how he did?)

Anyway, there are many phenomena in here that would faze a lot of people and that they just wouldn't accept - mind-reading, seeing things at a distance, and so on. But, IMO, it's often more difficult _not_ to accept such things, when one reads accounts of them in context, than not - unless one's arbitrarily decided in advance not to do so. But there's stuff in there - wider concepts about the nature of things and our destiny - that I think tend to raise more scepticism.

Interesting cross-cultural stuff in here, too. For example, Boyd tells of a Japanese man - a shinto priest perhaps: not clear - meeting Mad Bear and telling him he had diabetes and to lay off the soda. Apparently, he just "saw". And curious that Mad Bear himself, while - apparently - being in tune with so much could not, it seems, see his own diabetes or its cause.

But I said too much already. I talk too much: don't listen enough.

What I really want to know is if anyone else here has read the book and, if so, what they made of it.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Metformin, PCOS, and hair loss

### AnnieW — 2015-07-23T16:46:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-pcos-and-hair-loss.16573/post-172764>

I am going back to square one. Had my bloodwork done with my RE, but he only tested for glucose and TSH. I just put in a call to see if we could have the hormones tested, as those haven't been tested since 2012.

Glucose is 108
A1C is 6.2
TSH is 1.326

I had taken metformin in the past and remember feeling fatigued along with the GI issues (although the diarrhea improved greatly when I started eating low carb).

I am very concerned about my hair loss. It is really bad at this point and I am wondering what effect the metformin will have? Will it improve my situation and slow the hair loss or will it make my hair fall out even more due to the lack of B vitamins?

### Shijin13 — 2015-07-23T16:54:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-pcos-and-hair-loss.16573/post-172766>

context is everything... Metformin in the right context is helpful for more than lowering glucose.

Following circadian cycles, and eating seasonaly, if you're in summer time, its normal for BG to be higher than it would be in the winter.... Jack has commented upon this recently in the UV thread here.

If you're loosing hair while taking met - I'd be looking at your fully thyroid function (TSH, Free T4, Free T3, Reverse T3 and Antibodies) as well as a full lipid panel, and hormone panel (Pg, E2, testosterone, LH, FSH, pregnenolone, D3, prolactin) Sex Hormone binding Globulin, IGF-1, Mag RBC, Chem7 and CBC, and a ASI.

2nd what does your environment look like, and what are you doing to address it?

How long have you been following the Leptin RX, CT, protocols?

### AnnieW — 2015-07-23T17:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-pcos-and-hair-loss.16573/post-172772>

I don't remember losing hair with met before, but it was a few years ago. I think the hair loss is really bad right now because my weight has skyrocketed and my hormones are probably all screwed up which is why I need another panel done. I have no problem with my circadian cycle. I sleep very well but I am surrounded by EMF due to a certain family member (who insisted on putting a "smart" TV in our room a couple of months ago).

I am re-starting the protocols and trying to change things where I can. In a few months there might be some major changes taking place that will allow me to improve my situation even more. I can't really expand on that right now as I'm still unsure and afraid. It basically comes down to whether or not I have the courage to put my own health and well being over a situation I know isn't going anywhere (and is making me very unhealthy). Maybe I will start a journal and chronicle all that if and when the time comes. But it is not easy for me right now which is why the weight is really bad.

The RE's major goal with the met is to get the glucose down and prevent me from developing diabetes. I just didn't want to go bald before I hit 35. I used to have really nice hair but now I am losing it and my facial hair could rival the Duck Dynasty guys.

# Thread: Stored Carbs in Winter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stored-carbs-in-winter.16580/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-07-24T04:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stored-carbs-in-winter.16580/post-172853>

I couldn't find any threads on here relating to this.

What is the deal with stored carbs? For example, an agricultural society that stores carbs that they have grown to eat in the winter. Is this where humans began to go wrong, 10,000 or so years ago?

I am coming to understand the science about how carbs in winter are very sub-optimal- losing energy to env't requiring more electrons, also not enough sun photons to make up for the lack of "energy" in carbs.

\- Matt

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-24T19:59:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stored-carbs-in-winter.16580/post-172904>

Shan't touch the biochemistry or biophysics but ...

> [Matty\_M said:](/goto/post?id=172853)
>
> What is the deal with stored carbs? For example, an agricultural society that stores carbs that they have grown to eat in the winter. Is this where humans began to go wrong, 10,000 or so years ago?

But one thing I will say is that from an anthropological/historical view to some extent this is Paleo Fairy Tales. By this I mean that while there's some truth in this picture of things it's too schematic. The Neolithic Revolution took a **lot** longer to reach some parts than this. You could, for example, probably measure the move to agriculturalism in Ireland and Highland Scotland in _hundreds_ of years rather than thousands - these places were essentially pastoralist till historical times.

And on the other hand, people were playing around with cultivating the foods the Paleo Diet says they weren't interested in a lot longer ago. There's been a fair amount of evidence around for a long time on that, but just this week:

> The Origin of Cultivation and Proto-Weeds, Long Before Neolithic Farming

[http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131422](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131422)

Also very new and interesting to note - but not, I suggest, surprising:

> A Milk and Ochre Paint Mixture Used 49,000 Years Ago at Sibudu, South Africa

[http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131273](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131273)

I can't count the number of times I've heard Professor Cordain say "You try milking a wild animal". He's got egg on his face now though: evidently people did. And if we know they used it in pigments, can we sure they didn't drink it? There are, in any case, accounts of hunter-gatherers killing suckling animals and eating the curds from their stomachs.

Moreover, before people even began to play around with cultivating cereals, they were gathering wild grasses. We know the Australian Aborigines - who were, in effect, the last Palaeolithic people on earth - made hearth-cakes from grass seeds and other suitable plants. See here:

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bush\_bread](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bush_bread)

(Important to bear in mind, however, that this was not _refined_ carbohydrate, and that it was only a part of the diet - meat was probably eaten in pretty large quantities.)

And the other point to bear in mind is that people's health didn't immediately go into the toilet as soon as they began farming. The health of people on the eastern seaboard of what's now the United States of America at first contact seems to have been superb. Many of these societies were farming people, although they also supplemented their diet with hunted, fished and gathered food from the wild.

And we can go a lot more recent than this. In the 1930s Weston Price chronicled the health and diets of fourteen societies of healthy people around the globe. Some of these societies were hunter-gatherers, but some were pastoralists, some fisher-folk - and some were agriculturalists:

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/pricetoc.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/pricetoc.html)

It's clear that societies certainly _can_ come unstuck when they switch to agriculture. But it's not a given, as Price's studies show. I think the problems come not because people begin to eat Paleo-forbidden foods, such as cereals, but because, in some cases, they don't really eat much else at all. What's going to happen to, for example, your zinc and iron intake, if that's what you do? And then there are further problems that can arise like soil-exhaustion and sudden population-increase, whereby food supplies - or supplies of more nutritious foods - can be outrun.

### Matty_M — 2015-07-27T21:51:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stored-carbs-in-winter.16580/post-173252>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=172904)
>
> Shan't touch the biochemistry or biophysics but ...
>
>
>
> But one thing I will say is that from an anthropological/historical view to some extent this is Paleo Fairy Tales. By this I mean that while there's some truth in this picture of things it's too schematic. The Neolithic Revolution took a **lot** longer to reach some parts than this. You could, for example, probably measure the move to agriculturalism in Ireland and Highland Scotland in _hundreds_ of years rather than thousands - these places were essentially pastoralist till historical times.
>
> And on the other hand, people were playing around with cultivating the foods the Paleo Diet says they weren't interested in a lot longer ago. There's been a fair amount of evidence around for a long time on that, but just this week:
>
>
>
> [http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131422](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131422)
>
> Also very new and interesting to note - but not, I suggest, surprising:
>
>
>
> [http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131273](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0131273)
>
> I can't count the number of times I've heard Professor Cordain say "You try milking a wild animal". He's got egg on his face now though: evidently people did. And if we know they used it in pigments, can we sure they didn't drink it? There are, in any case, accounts of hunter-gatherers killing suckling animals and eating the curds from their stomachs.
>
> Moreover, before people even began to play around with cultivating cereals, they were gathering wild grasses. We know the Australian Aborigines - who were, in effect, the last Palaeolithic people on earth - made hearth-cakes from grass seeds and other suitable plants. See here:
>
> [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bush\_bread](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bush_bread)
>
> (Important to bear in mind, however, that this was not _refined_ carbohydrate, and that it was only a part of the diet - meat was probably eaten in pretty large quantities.)
>
> And the other point to bear in mind is that people's health didn't immediately go into the toilet as soon as they began farming. The health of people on the eastern seaboard of what's now the United States of America at first contact seems to have been superb. Many of these societies were farming people, although they also supplemented their diet with hunted, fished and gathered food from the wild.
>
> And we can go a lot more recent than this. In the 1930s Weston Price chronicled the health and diets of fourteen societies of healthy people around the globe. Some of these societies were hunter-gatherers, but some were pastoralists, some fisher-folk - and some were agriculturalists:
>
> [http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/pricetoc.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/pricetoc.html)
>
> It's clear that societies certainly _can_ come unstuck when they switch to agriculture. But it's not a given, as Price's studies show. I think the problems come not because people begin to eat Paleo-forbidden foods, such as cereals, but because, in some cases, they don't really eat much else at all. What's going to happen to, for example, your zinc and iron intake, if that's what you do? And then there are further problems that can arise like soil-exhaustion and sudden population-increase, whereby food supplies - or supplies of more nutritious foods - can be outrun.

Very interesting! Thanks. It's amazing how (especially with LR) one is more than willing to subsist on a refined sugar and starch (cereals) diet.

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-27T22:31:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stored-carbs-in-winter.16580/post-173255>

> [Matty\_M said:](/goto/post?id=173252)
>
> Very interesting! Thanks. It's amazing how (especially with LR) one is more than willing to subsist on a refined sugar and starch (cereals) diet.

Yeah, plenty of people do. Sugar is new, of course, but you could find people subsisting on little more than cereals in the past. IIRC, the Maya would be an example - and also an example of eventual civilisational collapse.

Lots of interesting issues around your original thoughts there. Another one, I think, would be the storing of food, which you originally raised. I see where you're coming from, and think we probably would think this … and then, thinking again, I'm not sure we'd be right to. Are we unconsciously affected by outdated views of hunter-gatherers as feckless "primitives" (in some derogatory sense) when we do?

Some do store food when it makes sense. Some Inuit stockpiled frozen fish:

[http://www.amazon.com/Kabloona-Gontran-Poncins/dp/B0007DPPL4/](http://www.amazon.com/Kabloona-Gontran-Poncins/dp/B0007DPPL4/)

And Plains Indians made pemmican for the winter. During the wars on the Plains, there were occasions when the U.S. Army surprised a village and having killed and chased off tribespeople burnt their tepees and their _parfleches_ of pemmican. That was their food for the winter: hence a means for the Army to cut off their possibility of surviving through that season and make them either starve or "come in":

[http://www.amazon.com/Death-Prairie-Thirty-Struggle-Western/dp/0803297211/](http://www.amazon.com/Death-Prairie-Thirty-Struggle-Western/dp/0803297211/)

So I think hunter-gatherers are perfectly capable of thinking ahead and storing food when it makes sense to do so. They just wouldn't bother if it was easily available without doing so.

Now I have read that "wild rice" was so plentiful in the region of the Great Lakes that stands of it would last all year and still be there when the new crop came through in the following year. And this despite the use made of it by humans (Winnebago, etc.), animals and birds. To collect it you just paddled a canoe into a stand and whacked the plants, so that the seeds dropped in the canoe. Here, there's no storage necessary: the stand itself is natural storage.

I don't know if stands of cereals in the Fertile Crescent were so prolific. I doubt anyone does. It's an interesting question, isn't it? But there certainly can be a lot of food there when stands of wild wheat ripen. A few years ago some archaeologists reaped wild wheat - which is, apparently, still around in places in the Near East - with flint sickles as a "reconstruction" exercise and got a surprisingly large haul in a fairly short time.

I think all this is a caution against taking the Paleo view of the past too seriously. It may be a good a good way to eat for many reasons - not least that the foods it tends to eliminate seem generally harder to deal with for those with disturbed gut-flora (read many, perhaps most, of any modern population). But as for some of the looser claims about what people did and didn't eat in the past. I think that's more debatable.

There just are lots of unknowns. And doubtless plenty of "non-Paleo" foods were around in at any rate some environments. But, again, I'm sure people preferred to eat plenty of meat and fish when they could get it, and doubtless those were often abundantly available.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-07-24T07:02:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hs-crp-testing-avoid-during-periods-of-high-inflammation.16583/post-172865>

I just got my HS-CRP test back and it was 12.6 mg/L (holy sh\*&!).

The day of the test I had a gut ache from something..

My instinct is to do the test again when I have no real inflammatory symptoms and my naturopath agreed.
So just a heads up for anyone who does this test..I wouldn't do it when your symptoms are worse than normal because you will not really get a good baseline reading.

Please post if this is inaccurate.

# Thread: Photograph of a Navajo mother and child
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photograph-of-a-navajo-mother-and-child.16615/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2015-07-25T11:04:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photograph-of-a-navajo-mother-and-child.16615/post-172962>

Beautiful image of a Navajo mother and child:

[https://twitter.com/NativAmProverbs/status/624704932533411846](https://twitter.com/NativAmProverbs/status/624704932533411846)

Apparently, the date is unknown. I wonder if it's actually newer than it might at first seem: the figure of the mother seems calm and still to me but not "frozen" having had to stand still for the long exposure-time of really old photography. And would such a small child stay still for very long? The child's eyes seem to have been caught in a moment, too, but that might be down to re-touching.

I like the obvious health and strength of the figures. It's also noticeable that the faces are broad and the middle-third of the woman's face well-developed. Weston Price mentions this as a feature of all healthy populations.

### thomas — 2015-07-25T20:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photograph-of-a-navajo-mother-and-child.16615/post-172997>

Pretty nice picture indeed.
Gagnrad you really love anthropology

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-26T13:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photograph-of-a-navajo-mother-and-child.16615/post-173038>

> [thomas said:](/goto/post?id=172997)
>
> Gagnrad you really love anthropology

Depends what you mean by anthropology, Thomas.

I really don't know that much, though it does interest me. But I wonder sometimes about the dead hand of the theorist. I think I really prefer ethnography - writings about peoples, simple accounts of small-scale societies - rather than academic analyses of same.

These accounts can be naive and they mightn't always be truthful but at least they're first-hand. Sure, they do come laden with the baggage of the person who tells the tale. But that's unavoidable anyway: there can be no detached observer of anything.

And is that any worse than academic baggage? I think it might be less insidious. Look at the simplified and totally erroneous model of heart disease that's regnant in the medical sphere - the diet-heart hypothesis. Is it likely to be any different in any other academic discipline?

I tend to think that the naive observer might record quite significant information just by-the-by without even noticing it or thinking about its significance. I quite like this kind of material:

[http://www.amazon.com/Captured-Indians-Firsthand-Accounts-1750-1870/dp/0486249018/](http://www.amazon.com/Captured-Indians-Firsthand-Accounts-1750-1870/dp/0486249018/)

# Thread: Help with my "Master Challenge" starting Aug 3, 2015

### BobR — 2015-07-25T21:33:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-my-master-challenge-starting-aug-3-2015.16622/post-172999>

I've been a follower (off/on) of Dr Jack for a few years and I'm back ready and motivated to move forward!

I've set a date of Monday Aug 3 to fully implement three major areas and I'd like some feedback on best ways to get started.

1.  Epi-Paleo eating (I'm pretty good here, I eat Paleo now and only drink Tequila if I have alcohol)
2.  Cold Thermogenisis (I'm not sure if cold packs and the bath tub are good enough or should I buy a tank... see below)
3.  EMF blocking (I've spent the past 20+ year career behind a computer screen so I'm looking into Blue blocker glasses etc..)

There is SOOO much awesome info and I have the "getting started guides" from JackKruse.com BUT are there other resources here in the forum I should be aware of?

**More about me:**
I've lost over 30lbs the past few years and attribute it to Tim Ferriss 4 Hour Body which lead to Paleo eating and now Dr Jack.

I'm very healthy, never get sick, nor do I take any medications prescribed or over the counter.

I've had 20-30lbs of "Belly Fat" I can't seem to get rid of for over 10 years. I'm one of those guys with virtually no fat except my mid section.

I've wondered is it Visceral fat, Colon build up, enlarged kidneys or ???
I drink a gallon of water a day and walk/bike/jog a few times a week.

**My questions I need help with:**
Is there other areas I should be looking at than the big 3 listed above?

Has anyone built a cold tub at home? I'm looking at the Rubbermaid 70Gal tank for my garage to better regulate the temperature and use a snorkeling tube for air?

EMF seems the most "out there" and I can wear blue blockers but ???

**My major focus is using/following Dr Kruse guides on the website but wondering if there are other hidden tips you can send me to in the forum... not sure how much time I have each day to dig into every conversation.
**
Thanks in advance and looking forward to the challenge!

### Lahelada — 2015-07-26T00:40:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-my-master-challenge-starting-aug-3-2015.16622/post-173011>

Sleeping in total darkness with no electronics electrics
Sleeping in cold
Emf = blue light I.e your fridge creates emf but/ no blue light
Plan to spend time outside between the hours of 8-11 and/or 3-5 pm

### fitness@home — 2015-07-26T03:25:28+0300

# Thread: Cockles??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cockles.16623/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2015-07-25T22:17:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cockles.16623/post-173004>

Where is Molly Malone when you need her...

So I bought frozen cockles . Reputable supermarket,the labels says frozen raw cockles.
My problems is that they are all open to a degree which is what they should be after cooking. Do they open on freezing ? Common sense would say yes as speed freezing would kill them. I just cooked one as atrial which was slightly open and it did indeed open as predicted for fresh cockles. I would imagine that they do not get tapped individually before freezing hence they are slightly open and that if they open further on cooking they are OK to eat.
Has anybody got experience with this?

### Peertje80 — 2015-07-25T22:37:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cockles.16623/post-173006>

You would definitely smell if it was rotten. But I think the rule is it should be closed before cooking (room temp)and it should be open after cooking, if not... toss them. If you have doubt, but they are open after cooking but smell like &^%$ they are rotten for sure. Others wise try 1, if it is very chewing it could be on the edge too.

I used to cook spaghetti vongole with the frozen ones. They always were a tiny bit open. I figured this would be logical considering what happens to stuff when you freeze it.

### Lahelada — 2015-07-26T00:04:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cockles.16623/post-173010>

Thanks Peertje ! I am glad you confirmed what I was thinking while meandering around Walmart.Flash freezing shortens the closing muscle but it retains tensegrity. When you cook it,tensegrity is lost and the shell pops open.if it does not open it was bad before freezing. Complete novice to cockles can you tell....

# Thread: Food and medicine from the wild
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-and-medicine-from-the-wild.16624/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2015-07-25T23:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-and-medicine-from-the-wild.16624/post-173008>

Here's a documentary made in 1986 about the work of Major Les Hiddens of the Australian Army.

At the time he was going around the north of Australia cataloguing the plants that had medicinal uses or that could be used as food - "bush tucker", in Australian parlance. In some cases he's working alone, but whenever he can he gets local Aboriginal people to show him the uses of these plants.

There's also some footage of Aboriginal women catching a small lizard. Interesting to note in this context that macronutrient ratios in hunter-gatherer diets that people have calculated in the past have sometimes been based on looking at tables in encyclopaedias of anthropology that give weights of food provided on the one hand by the men and on the other by the women then putting them through algorithms that assume that any food foraged by women is some kind of notional plant. Bad assumption.

There's a surprising amount of food available in some of these places. There have been occasions in the past when white men stranded in the Outback have starved amid plenty, since they just did not know what was around them.

The information gleaned by Major Hiddens was used to draw up guides for soldiers in case they needed to fight over this region and live off the land.

There's a telling vignette filmed in a store at the beginning of the film. Hiddens shows store food, remarking that processed food doesn't even look like anything it's made out of and is now so prevalent that many people no longer know what real food looks like. He says a soldier would recognise a packet of dried tomato soup, but would he even know what a tomato plant was? Yet his grandmother would have been bemused by the packet: soup was something she would have made from real raw ingredients. And this was filmed nearly 30 years ago! Things have not got better since then.

Very interesting footage.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lactobacillus-fermentum-can-raise-glutathione-plus-contains-sod.16631/post-173244>

So, Dr. K - can we use this probiotic to spike super oxide? And if so, is there an applicabel "season" to do it?

[http://www.google.com/patents/US20040151708](http://www.google.com/patents/US20040151708)

What is interesting to me is that used goat's milk and fermented it using d-ribose+galactose - galactose BTW seems to be another way of spiking superoxide - kinda pricey though -

The strain of micro-organism _Lactobacillus fermentum_ ME-3 is a novel anti-microbial and anti-oxidative probiotic. It has a high anti-microbial effect on_Escherichia coli, Shigella sonnei, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella typhimurium,_ and moderate activity against _Helicobacter pylori_ strains. The strain of micro-organism possesses Mn-superoxide dismutase and both its lysates and intact cells have high anti-oxidative activity, increasing the glutathione red-ox ratio in blood sera and able to capture toxic hydroxyl radicals. The strain of micro-organism could be used as a probiotic for the production of functional food (yoghurt, cheese) and non-comestibles (tablets, capsules) for the prophylaxis of intestinal and uroinfections, both for the prevention and treatment of chronic diseases, caused by prolonged oxidative stress.

# Thread: Gas...

### Matty_M — 2015-07-29T07:07:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gas.16638/post-173383>

I never would've tried to change my health 2 years ago and ended up stumbling here if it weren't for horrible, chronic, stinky gas that forced me to look for answers.

Recently, this issue has been seeming to make a small comeback, despite soooo many things I'm doing. It is very annoying and I would do anything to fix it. It is primarily after meals. I find myself having to stand up and walk away for "no reason".... otherwise hold it in. I am fairly sure it is high in sulfur, or at least that is what I believe causes the strong smell. Digestive enzymes/betaine HCl seem to help.

I have done almost everything on this site that seems to apply to me, and I'm often told it is too much.

\- I see the sunrise every morning. I get lots of sunlight (and barely burn anymore- maybe a little bit on my face and neck after many many hours in powerful sun). I avoid fake light and wear blue blockers consistently after dark, if in a lit area.
\- I do CT daily, with some exceptions. Around 20-60 min at between 45-60 F
\- I dont drink alcohol
\- I sleep outside grounded (when I'm at home, currently on vacation sleeping in basement..)
\- I drink over a gallon of ice cold spring water daily
\- I follow the Epi-Paleo Rx to a tee and have for many months (as well as the leptin rx and I'm pretty sure I'm leptin sensitive- few/no carb cravings, more energy, better sleep)
\- I completely avoid halides (pools, public water, etc.)
\- I use meditation while doing ct
\- I sleep a lot (although I'm lower in energy than I should be, I believe)
\- I do a good amount of non-exercise activity thermogenesis, not HIIT because I'm not 100% sure if I'm leptin sensitive

I'm guessing this gas is a gut flora/gut functionality issue... I'm not sure what else it could be (although with all the truth bombs I get here I wouldn't be surprised if at its core it is a light issue or something).

I really just want answers. I could really use a short term band-aid, as well as a long term solution.

I have been working on EPCOTx for the last week, but I obviously haven't noticed any immediate improvements...

Any suggestions for immediate relief would be GREATLY appreciated.. as well as a path I might consider following for a long-term solution (If something besides EPCOTx)

Thanks a lot.

\- Matt

### Penny — 2015-07-29T21:56:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gas.16638/post-173441>

You probably have a "sulfur reducing bacteria" i.e. - one that eats sulfur and generates hydrogen sulfide - this is the "did something die in your stomach" stench you are smelling...

**Sulfur-reducing bacteria** (SRB) get their energy by [reducing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Redox_reaction) elemental [sulfur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sulfur) to [hydrogen sulfide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide). They couple this reaction with the oxidation of [acetate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acetate), [succinate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Succinate) or other [organic compounds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organic_compounds).

Several types of [bacteria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bacteria) and many non-[methanogenic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methanogen) [archaea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archaea) can reduce sulfur.
Some bacteria – such as _[Proteus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteus_\(bacterium\))_, _[Campylobacter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Campylobacter)_, _[Pseudomonas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pseudomonas)_ and _[Salmonella](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salmonella)_ – have the ability to reduce sulfur, but can also use oxygen and other terminal electron acceptors. Others, such as _Desulfuromonas_, use only sulfur.
These bacteria can be used in industrial processes to generate [hydrogen sulfide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide) for the precipitation of metals.
Some bacteria can use both elemental sulfur and sulfate as electron acceptors"

I would get the american gut ([www.americangut.org](http://www.americangut.org)) test and see which of the little darlings you have... it's $99.00 with quite detailed taxa...
Some great articles on which probiotics for which bug can be found here:

[http://www.nature.com/ijos/journal/v6/n4/full/ijos201452a.html](http://www.nature.com/ijos/journal/v6/n4/full/ijos201452a.html)

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2481460/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2481460/)

Try to take a probiotic with bacillus subtillus - I like the Garden Of Life Ultra as it contains both b. subtillus and s. boulardi (for fungi) - if you have campylobacter, this will reduce your stomach acid, thus making food undigested - it's just like h. pylori - a cheap test is this stuff:

[http://www.amazon.com/NOW-Foods-Ulc...pebp=1438195329751&perid=03QZHMF4TPR3VR7QEQWM](http://www.amazon.com/NOW-Foods-Ulcetrol-60-Tablets/dp/B0013OXM0M/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1438195322&sr=1-1&keywords=ulcetrol&pebp=1438195329751&perid=03QZHMF4TPR3VR7QEQWM)

You can also try cat's claw or myrhh gum - if you get any sort of improvement, I would lean toward thinking it's a urease producer - or get the urease breath test from your doctor - IMHO, the blood test is useless -
[http://www.amazon.com/Natures-Way-Myrrh-Gum-Capsules/dp/B000S86T5E/ref=sr\_1\_1?&ie=UTF8&qid=1438195451&sr=8-1&keywords=myrrh gum&pebp=1438195460371&perid=0TRZR49QVVX2VXJW54GT](http://www.amazon.com/Natures-Way-Myrrh-Gum-Capsules/dp/B000S86T5E/ref=sr_1_1?&ie=UTF8&qid=1438195451&sr=8-1&keywords=myrrh+gum&pebp=1438195460371&perid=0TRZR49QVVX2VXJW54GT)

the myrrh gum or mastic gum will inhibit the production of urease in the bugs living in your stomach, thus giving you an improvement - if you have GERD, this is also a huge tell IMHO...

Or, like my kid, you could have both pseudomonas and campy...

This is the stuff I use to kill urease producers:
[http://www.amazon.com/Biotics-Research-Bio-HPF-180C/dp/B000UQPI7Y/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1438195958&sr=1-1&keywords=bio hpf&pebp=1438195964372&perid=1CBR0SQWK2E7GYN2NW4R](http://www.amazon.com/Biotics-Research-Bio-HPF-180C/dp/B000UQPI7Y/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1438195958&sr=1-1&keywords=bio+hpf&pebp=1438195964372&perid=1CBR0SQWK2E7GYN2NW4R)

It is said for every year that you live, your odds increase 1% of having one of these babies...

Digestive enzymes are fantastic IMHO - I take these because they have ox bile (fat digestion), betaine HCL, plus the normal enzymes:
[http://www.amazon.com/NOW-Foods-Super-Enzymes-Capsules/dp/B0013OXKHC/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1438196113&sr=8-1&keywords=now enzymes&pebp=1438196116665&perid=0HNRD7JZW83FH5DM4B67](http://www.amazon.com/NOW-Foods-Super-Enzymes-Capsules/dp/B0013OXKHC/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1438196113&sr=8-1&keywords=now+enzymes&pebp=1438196116665&perid=0HNRD7JZW83FH5DM4B67)

Naturally, it also means your environment is still wrecking you life - you might find when you move that all these troubles go away - if not, it means IMHO your new environment is just as crappy as your old one...

Keep us posted

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-08-02T01:40:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-measurements-and-meters-suggestions-needed.16654/post-173751>

### Scompy — 2015-08-02T04:28:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-measurements-and-meters-suggestions-needed.16654/post-173766>

Awesome, very cool... I like the fact that there are places allowing us to rent it for a day. There are a few friends I've made aware of EMF and have explained it to them in enough depth that they mentioned wanting to check too. Maybe some sort of sharing could be in-order amongst us

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-08-02T06:18:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-measurements-and-meters-suggestions-needed.16654/post-173773>

> [Scompy said:](/goto/post?id=173766)
>
> Awesome, very cool... I like the fact that there are places allowing us to rent it for a day. There are a few friends I've made aware of EMF and have explained it to them in enough depth that they mentioned wanting to check too. Maybe some sort of sharing could be in-order amongst us

The thing is that if you rent you don't really get a chance to get used to the meter and the way it reads. For instance, my meter will read very high if it is moving at all. It will also give you odd readings if your hands or body is anywhere near it. Certain angles, etc. Different times of day. If somebody moves in and out of next door. Some of the EMF is actually from the meter itself!
Just letting you know that there are many factors that can change readings. That's why I decided to buy my meters.
Good luck!

### Martin — 2015-08-02T15:07:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-measurements-and-meters-suggestions-needed.16654/post-173782>

After reading up on various mag field meters for months, I decided on this one. Karl Riley, an EMF radiation specialist in USA uses this same one. He also has a DVD and book on correcting problems. Learned a lot, and now I have a meter that compares to ones costing thousands.
It has a separate probe (blue boxy thingy in the photo of the case) that you plug into the meter and then hold the probe near an object to test it's field. You rotate the probe slowly to find the highest reading, and you can use it to zero in on a source versus a meter with a three-axis configuration (three probes built into the meter itself that read in three different directions at once, averaging the total score).
There are bennies to a single-axis like this one, and different advantages to the three-axis meters. I like fine tuning my measurements and exploring so I chose this one. It seems like the best for the money and has a great accuracy history.

[http://www.magneticsciences.com/MSI95Gaussmeter.html](http://www.magneticsciences.com/MSI95Gaussmeter.html)

### Jason Coates — 2018-05-25T16:22:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bdnf-without-exercise.16670/post-242039>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28959190?dopt=Abstract](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28959190?dopt=Abstract)
Homeostatic regulation of REM sleep drive, as measured by an increase in the number of REM sleep transitions, plays a key role in neuronal and behavioral plasticity (i.e., learning and memory). Deficits in REM sleep homeostatic drive (RSHD) are implicated in the development of many neuropsychiatric disorders. Yet, the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying this RSHD remain to be incomplete. To further our understanding of this mechanism, the current study was performed on freely moving rats to test a hypothesis that a positive interaction between extracellular-signal-regulated kinase 1 and 2 (ERK1/2) activity and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) signaling in the pedunculopontine tegmentum (PPT) is a causal factor for the development of RSHD. Behavioral results of this study demonstrated that a short period (<90 min) of selective REM sleep restriction (RSR) exhibited a strong RSHD. Molecular analyses revealed that this increased RSHD increased phosphorylation and activation of ERK1/2 and **BDNF** expression in the PPT. Additionally, pharmacological results demonstrated that the application of the ERK1/2 activation inhibitor U0126 into the PPT prevented RSHD and suppressed BDNF expression in the PPT. T**hese results, for the first time, suggest that the positive interaction between ERK1/2 and BDNF in the PPT is a casual factor for the development of RSHD. These findings provide a novel direction in understanding how RSHD-associated specific molecular changes can facilitate neuronal plasticity and memory processing.**

### Gagnrad — 2015-08-06T21:46:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spirituality-ultra-style.16671/post-174134>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=174099)
>
> I mean the Norwegians are a bit satiric
> and yeah they do stuff a little bit their own way too......
> they still are a bit wild a free  at least some of them

Best not to "politicise" things too much, but I one guess could point out that the figure of fun in the video is a "liberal". In Scandinavia people of "progressive" temperament perhaps tend more towards to something rather more like "social democracy" than liberalism.

I'm not judging here - more thinking aloud. I guess all these attitudes - liberal, conservative, social-democratic - probably have their strengths and weaknesses.

The most salient characteristic that the character in the video is given - I recall seeing him in a "how to go gluten-free" spoof - is narcissism.

But one could go one level deeper and take apart the assumptions that the sketch writer himself has and unwittingly reveals. Here's an example. There's the dig at people of a certain sort over-using the term "intuition". But I don't think the writer understands what's meant (and has been meant) by the term. He's confusing it with premonition - or, perhaps better, precognition. And I don't think he's suggesting the character is confused as to the meaning: I think _he himself_ is.

Very interesting definitions given here:

[http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/intuition](http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/intuition)

Keep scrolling down and notice how the meaning has changed from the Middle English usage to current usage. _Neither_ has anything to do with precognition, but there's a massive difference between:

> The ability to understand something instinctively without the need for conscious reasoning ...

And:

> spiritual insight or immediate spiritual communication …

"Instinct" - there's another word that's changed it's meaning - currently tends to be applied to animals. It's something that's thought of as sub-rational.

But I think "spiritual insight" implies something totally rational but above, not below, a process of reasoning. It's simply seeing. Think of a mathematical proof. There you have a chain of reasoning, but it always goes back to something that is axiomatic - that you simply _see_.

Maybe there _is_ such a thing as intuition, and maybe we need it, and maybe it's not easy at all to develop. And doubtless the character in the video would not have much of it - all he has is the ghost of an idea that it is important (or perhaps "impressive" would be a better word for him). But I think the sketch writer assumes that _there is no such thing_. Really? Are we sure about that?

Of course, the video is about getting laughs out of us: it's not meant seriously. But perhaps it's also good to remember not to take it too seriously.

### Optimalbound — 2015-08-07T20:57:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spirituality-ultra-style.16671/post-174208>

Nice comments, Gagnrad.

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=174034)
>
> LOL
>
> I don't think there's a political _purpose_ there: it's just played for laughs. And it's more social than political. But it's part of the joke that you know how he would vote, isn't it? And there's a kind of edge when he asks for money near the end, because the thought "Who's paying, and what's the justice in that?" has a familiar resonance with Americans who self-identify as "conservative".

This sort of thing is a bit of a minefield to discuss across the Atlantic. Our political and socio-economic landscapes are not quite the same, trends are rarely in sync, and the terminology might seem similar, yet have somewhat different connotations and nuances.

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=174034)

# Thread: GI Effects + 23andMe Results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gi-effects-23andme-results.16674/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2015-08-05T13:02:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gi-effects-23andme-results.16674/post-174003>

Hey guys,

GI Effects Results (My interpretation):

> \-Slightly low PH (Don't think it's overly concerning)
> \-Very high beta glucuronidase (Allows toxins to recirculate. Why is this so high?)
> \-No lactobacillus (Lower levels correlate with symptom severity in IBS)
> \-Bacillus Spicies in potentially pathogenic amounts (Don't think this is the problem)
> \-No mycology (No yeast, don't think this is anything to worry about)
>
> All this seems to confirm my suspicions that it is in fact my stomach / duodenum that's the cause of my issues.

> MTHFR Mutations
> No homozygous, 3 hetrozygous (MTHFR C677T, MTHFR 03 P39P, MTHFR A1298C)
> MTHFR C677T:
> One function of MTHFR is to help convert homocysteine to methionine. A C677T mutation means the MTHFR enzyme may be less functional leading to high levels of homocysteine. Impaired function of the enzyme can cause/contribute to Autism, Chronic Fatigue Syndrome, Fibromyalgia, Miscarriages, IBS, many birth defects, Multiple Sclerosis, Alzheimer's, Bipolar Disorder, blood clots, Stroke, Chemical Sensitivity, and more.
> C677T can lead to high homocysteine. While heterozygous (+/-) mutations indicates reduced activity, it does not necessarily mean there will be high homocysteine levels in a clinical setting.
> As S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH) accumulates, the COMT enzyme may become impaired. Since I also have COMT V158M (+/+), the high level of SAH can lead to behavior problems and mood swings according to Dr. Amy Yasko.
> MTHFR A1298C:
> A1298C is involved in converting 5-methylfolate (5MTHF) to tetrahydrofolate (THF). Unlike C677T, the A1298C mutation doesn't lead to elevated homocysteine levels. This reaction helps generate BH4. BH4 is important for the detoxification of ammonia. The gene is compromised about 30% in people with a heterozygous (+/-) mutation.
> BH4 acts as a rate limiting factor for production of neurotransmitters and catecholamines including serotonin, melatonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. A1298C + status may cause a decrease in any of these neurotransmitters or catecholamines. BH4 is also a cofactor in the production of nitric oxide. A dysfunctional BH4 enzyme may lead to mental/emotional and/or physical symptoms. Mercury, lead, and aluminum may act as a drain on BH4.
> MTHFR 03 P39P:
> There is currently not enough research or data to draw conclusions from this SNP.
>
> Other Mutations
> Homozygous ( COMT V158M, COMT H62H, MAO-A R297R, BHMT-08 )
> Hetrozygous ( VDR Bsm, VDR Taq, MTRR A66G, BHMT-02, AHCY-01, AHCY-19, CBS C699T, CBS A360A )
> CBS Mutations (C699T +/-, A360A +/-):
> CBS (cystathionine beta synthase) catalyzes the first step of the transsulfuration pathway, from homocysteine to cystathionine. CBS defects are actually an upregulation of the CBS enzyme. This means the enzyme works too fast. In these patients, it's common to see low levels of cystathionine and homocysteine since there is a rapid conversion to taurine. This leads to high levels of taurine and ammonia. CBS upregulation can result in sulfur intolerance. BH4 can also become depleted with a CBS upregulation. BH4 helps regulate neurotransmitters and mood. Other mutations, such as MTHFR A1298C, Chronic bacterial infections, and aluminum can also lead to low BH4 levels. Lack of BH4 can lead to mast cell degranulation and possibly mast cell activation disorder (MCAD).
> MTR/MTRR Mutations (MTRR A66G +/-):
> MTRR (Methionine synthase reductase) helps recycle B12. The combination of MTR and MTRR mutations can deplete methyl B12. MTR A2756G, MTRR A66G, MTRR H595Y, MTRR K350A, MTRR R415T, MTRR S257T, and MTRR A664A all work together to convert homocysteine to methionine.
> MTR (5-methyltetrahydrofolate-homocysteine methyltransferase) provides instructions for making the enzyme methionine synthase. Methionine synthase helps convert the amino acid homocysteine to methionine. To work properly, methionine synthase requires B12 (specifically in the form of methylcobalamin). An MTR A2756G mutation increases the activity of the MTR gene causing a greater need for B12 since the enzyme causes B12 to deplete since it is using it up at a faster rate. Mutations in MTR have been identified as the underlying cause of methylcobalamin deficiency. Megaloblastic anemia can occur as a consequence of reduce methionine synthase activity.
> A homozygous mutation of MTR A2756G is not very common (<1% of CEU population). Some studies have demonstrated that people with a combination of MTHFR C677T and MTR A2756G have persistently high homocysteine levels unless they are treated with both B12 and folate.
> MAO-A (R297R +/+):
> MAO-A (Monoamine oxidase A) is a critical enzyme involved in breaking down important neurotransmitters such as serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine. Males only have one allele since the gene is inherited through from their mother since it is located on the X chromosome. Only females can be heterozygous (+/-) for this mutation. When a (+/+) MAO-A mutation is combined with my (+/+) COMT V158M mutation, imbalances in neurotransmitters may be more severe. These imbalances can potentially lead to neuropsychiatric conditions and symptoms such as Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD), mood swings, and aggressive and/or violent behavior.
> COMT Mutations (V158M +/+, H62H +/+):
> COMT (catechol-O-methyltransferase) helps break down certain neurotransmitters and catecholamines. These include dopamine, epinephrine, and norepinephrine. Catechol-O-methyltransferase is important to the areas of the pre-frontal cortex. This area of the brain is involved with personality, inhibition of behaviors, short-term memory, planning, abstract thinking, and emotion. COMT is also involved with metabolizing estrogens.
> COMT (-/-) individuals can usually break down these neurotransmitters efficiently, but COMT (+/+) individuals may have trouble breaking these chemicals down from impaired function of the enzyme. With a COMT + status, it has been clinically observed by physicians that people may have trouble with methyl donors. This can lead to irritability, hyperactivity, or abnormal behavior. They may also be more sensitive to pain.
> VDR Mutations (Bsm +/-, Taq +/-):
> VDR (Vitamin D Receptor) encodes the nuclear hormone receptor for vitamin D3. Low or low normal vitamin D values are often seen in those with chronic illness and even the general population. Low vitamin D is related to a lot of neurological and immunological conditions. Vitamin D stimulates enzymes that create dopamine.
> VDR Tak and VDR Bsm are usually inverse from eachother. So if there is a (+/+) VDR Tak, there would be a (-/-) VDR Bsm. However, this is not always the case.
> It has been clinically observed that the body may have trouble tolerating methyl donors with my COMT V158M + and a VDR Taq + status. VDR Taq (-/-) individuals may already have higher levels of dopamine, and combinations of variations COMT and VDR Taq can lead to a wide range of dopamine levels. Those that are VDR Taq (+/+) and COMT (-/-) may have lowest dopamine levels.
> BHMT mutations (08 +/+, 02 +/-):
> BHMT (betaine homocysteine methyltransferase) acts as a shortcut through the methylation cycle helping convert homocysteine to methionine. The activity of the enzyme can be negatively influenced by stress. The Information on this enzyme related to methylation is mostly based on Dr. Amy Yasko's clinical experience and research.
> According to Dr. Yasko, a homozygous mutation of BHMT 01, BHMT 02, BHMT 04, can produce results similar to one with a CBS upregulation even if you don't have a CBS upregulation. In her book, Autism: Pathways to Recovery, She also states that a BHMT 08 mutation may "increase MHPG levels relative to dopamine breakdown (HVA)". This can result in attention type symptoms. It is common to see elevated glycine in someone with a homozygous BHMT 08 mutation.
> AHCY Mutations (01 +/-, 19 +/-):
> AHCY (S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase) is involved in breaking down the amino acid methionine. It controls the step that converts S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase to adenosine and homocysteine. Adenosine plays an important role in energy transfer as ATP and ADP. It helps promote sleep and suppress arousal. Dysfunction of this enzyme can affect levels of homocysteine and ammonia. Some physicians claim AHCY mutations may actually take the strain off the CBS enzyme and may even prevent taurine from becoming very elevated.
>
> My Notes
> Homocysteine: low (high, low, low, high, low)
> S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH): high
> Adenosine: low
> Methionine: low
> BH4: very low (low, low)
> Neurotransmitters: low, servere trouble breaking down
> Catecholamines: low, trouble breaking down
> Nitric Oxide: low
> Amonia: high (high, low, high)
> Taurine: high (high, low, high)
> Cystathionine: very low (low, low)
> MHPG: high
> Glycine: high
> Methyl Donors: double trouble tolerating
> B12: low
> Sulphor: intolerance

So my Large intestine and at least most of small intestine seems to be okay, indicating a stomach/duodenum nerve/mastcell/something else problem.

My genetic mutations may have come into effect since may energy has been so low for years so I have started supplementing to assist methylation.
Within the last week I have started various supplements with interesting effects:

Molybdenum: 1200mcg/ day. Started 8 days ago. On the first day massive fatigue and heart palpitations. At first I attributed this to dark chocolate but when the symptoms persisted I realised the moly was causing some sort of detox. Strangely I have taken this before without these effects, I think it is because I am higher energy now the moly can actually be put to work. Taking because mercury levels are slightly high and in hopes it will help fat tollerance.

Yucca Root: 6g/day. 5 days ago. Taking to reduce ammonia. Seem to be tolerating large food amounts better but am hesitant to atribute that for this as a lot of changes have happened recently.

L-methylfolate: 3000mcg/day. 6 days ago. Noticed a slight increase in fatigue first 2 days or so. Taking to increase BH4 (to see if mast cells are a problem) and to increase methylation.

Lo Salt (potassium chloride): 3 Days ago. Taking to help with dry mouth and supposedly increased methylation causes increased need for potassium. I think it's helping...

Hydroxy B12: 6000mcg/day sublingual. 2 Days ago. VERY interesting reaction with this. The first night I took it I woke up with quite strong stomach pains that were unusual to me around the area of where I had ulcer pains all those years ago. Now it could be I had a lot of butter that day which caused the reaction however the optimist in me says it is a healing reaction caused by increased methylation / cells renewing / nerves coming back to life. Sadly this reaction did not happen again last night however I have noticed reduced bloating and increased stomach tolerance... I definitely need to pursue this further and slowly up b12 and mfolate until I get another reaction. Again strangely I have taken methylb12 (not hydrox) without this reaction, it must be due to the other supplements being in place (mfolate) and my energy being increased, that is unless it wasn't a healing reaction after all.

Anyways it is looking promising...

# Thread: About to vomit/pass out
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-to-vomit-pass-out.16677/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-08-05T19:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-to-vomit-pass-out.16677/post-174032>

Hi,

I assumed that I am leptin sensitive after the protocol and some signs (more energy, less carb cravings, building lean mass without workouts, not skinny anymore). I decided to try some sprint to see how I feel, as it has been a year since I did any HIIT.

I warmed up with a quick light jog, and then did 4 100m sprints barefoot on grass, with about 30-60 seconds rest in between, enough to catch my breath and cool off. I felt pretty good, though very tired.

Immediately afterwards, I felt like shit, like I was going to vomit, and to pass out if I sat down. I recovered by drinking water, squatting, and breating deeply. I got a minor minor pressure headache.

Are these signs of LR, or just trying too much too fast? I really want to work out going forward, pending leptin sensitivity, to become even more strong and toned than I am just from CT and the protocols. Also, I want to see if I can secrete more HGH.

Any input appreciated.

\- Matt

### shah78 — 2015-08-05T23:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-to-vomit-pass-out.16677/post-174044>

Try two intervals, instead of four next time. You probably overdid it..... Signed, Shah 78, "King of Overdoing it"

### Matty_M — 2015-08-06T08:56:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-to-vomit-pass-out.16677/post-174084>

> [shah78 said:](/goto/post?id=174044)
>
> Try two intervals, instead of four next time. You probably overdid it..... Signed, Shah 78, "King of Overdoing it"

Yup. I'll build up. It doesnt feel like that much but sprinting barefoot in grass is a great workout.

### thomas — 2015-08-06T21:10:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-to-vomit-pass-out.16677/post-174126>

way too much if you're not used to do it regularly. Also, did you have at least 2-3 hours between your last meal and exercise?
I did vomit after sprinting too, but it was because I ate about 1 hour before. Your biology wants you to chill/nap after a meal!!

### Matty_M — 2015-08-07T00:21:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-to-vomit-pass-out.16677/post-174142>

> [thomas said:](/goto/post?id=174126)
>
> way too much if you're not used to do it regularly. Also, did you have at least 2-3 hours between your last meal and exercise?
> I did vomit after sprinting too, but it was because I ate about 1 hour before. Your biology wants you to chill/nap after a meal!!

So right. I did eat before. Next time I will be cautious of all this. Also, I will stick to 40 m sprints.

\-Matt

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1

### 493403g494f99539 — 2015-08-05T23:00:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-cold-contrast-shower-for-cardiovascular-health.16681/post-174043>

I've had a problem of getting dizzy, fatigued and start yawning when working out with heavy weights for years. I feel like CT has improved it, but then again I've learned to not push myself a lot during workouts to avoid feeling like garbage. (Also I've never been able to handle a sauna very well, guessing it's related)

Apparently doing a "hot-cold contrast shower" should be good for cardiovascular health? From what I've gathered it's a shower of 2 min with cold water, then like a minute of hot water and then cold again...

My question is whether or not this is true or should I just stick with good old cold only?

I did try it out today and boy does warm water feel weird after showering exclusively with cold water for over a month now. The effect I felt was mild dizziness I guess, but really mild... also I don't know how hot the hot water should be... just warm or _hot_ hot?

# Thread: Compression + Thermal; and a thought experiment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-thermal-and-a-thought-experiment.16682/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-08-05T23:42:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-thermal-and-a-thought-experiment.16682/post-174045>

I just found these when screwing around on Ben Greenfield's site:

[http://110playharder.com/](http://110playharder.com/) compression clothing items with slots inserted for re-usable ice packs.

Here's a [video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uCAE5aJAglA)

I believe compression clothing items were mentioned in the Redox Rx and also the Jet Lag Rx (for mitigating the damage induced by air travel). It was, of course, also mentioned for use with deep CT.

**Is there any reason _not to_ use compression garments more frequently, say, daily, if living in and/or working in a less than optimal environment?** Especially when combined at the same time with CT, I don't see why it couldn't help a lot for various issues.

**Thought experiments:**

**1.** Say we have been using the info in blogs - and, if a member, in the EMF boot camp. We have made our home a sanctuary - fixed faulty wiring and poorly done outlets, grounded our electronics such as lamps, have low EMF home phones, no microwaves, etc. - yet we work in a moderately poor environment (e.g. not something intense like shift work at a hospital or working on power lines or some 9-5 IT job, but a job where we may not be grounded all the time, many people will have cell phones, we may be required to occasionally use pc's, etc.). Wouldn't something such as the 110% Play Harder garments, when used with ice, mitigate some or most of the damage (again, from a moderately harmful environment)? The shorts made by the company have ice pack slots located near the upper thigh/groin, abdomen, etc. This strategic use reminds me of a post on this forum about using **spot CT on the femoral artery:** [](https://www.urmc.rochester.edu/Encyclopedia/GetImage.aspx?ImageID=125515)

**2.** Use these compression+thermal garments _prior to_ eating a seafood/egg based meal, or prior to apply some sort of substance containing sulfur, to **signal the necessity to incorporate more DHA/sulfur/ChS/**etc. to that location.

**3.** Using compression+thermal garments as a means to achieve the fournier effect when not near a body of water.

### Dennis Clark — 2015-09-07T22:34:47+0300

Have you by any chance begun a biohack experiment with the combo of CT and compression clothing yet? I am looking for a way to enhance my CT effects beyond a compression shirt and the CoolFatBurner and CoolGutBuster (with added ice pack on my upper back). My technique gives me spotty results with skin temps. I know that a cold bath would be optimal, although I haven't got one yet. Thanks!

Afterwards, I plan on experimenting with a lot of the PEMF products Dr. William Pawluk has reviewed on his site, as I think that will offer a much bigger bang for my buck than the CT+Compression. I still do think this is a viable route for overall enhancement and "protection" in a sense, but I need very significant improvements in my neurological well-being ASAP, which PEMF, ICES, and rTMS would be much more suitable for.

# Thread: Dr. Kruse, broad-stroke visualizations and low-level details
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-broad-stroke-visualizations-and-low-level-details.16686/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-08-06T20:58:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-broad-stroke-visualizations-and-low-level-details.16686/post-174122>

In a podcast, I heard Dr. K say, "I tend to be a detailed-oriented person." One of the greatest understatements of the century  I too am about the details and get complaints from people 24/7 that I write walls of text as a perfect example of this post.

I hold a premise that we are continually facing a time when there are even more people cannot make connections and therefore understand the ramifications about why those connections are meaningful. (And possibly the irony is that the environmental impacts reduce one's ability to consider the connections due to more brain-fog, upregulated glucose, etc.)

Dr. Kruse has a great, down-to-earth approach when it comes to communicating the vast knowledge-base to the layman. I heard a broad-stroke analogy about the 'Flower Pot'. This was in-reference to the DC electric current and how doctors often pump in Vitamin D to patients and it doesn't help those patients. The analogy was a flower-pot with a ton of holes at the bottom, representing that the Vitamin D just falls right back out again because those patients don't have enough DHA. (Assuming I got that analogy correct). **This got me thinking about whipping up photoshop-representations of these types of analogies for the layman: having images to increase the relatedness about the ideas.** Adding comedy to the images helps lock-in the concept. An example would be the pictoral book I read with my son called, "Darth Vader and Son" that does humorous magic with imagery.

Random thoughts:

_I read posts all of the time from people that advocate misinformed or outdated science with only a subset of information as their knowledge base. It's an understandable experience among us all. It's also understandable that people come to a 'conclusion' about the knowledge base they possess, but I've been seeing frequently that has become harder and harder for people to revise their conclusions. At the root of this is if people hold the virtue of truth higher than their ego when new information is understood and they see better results with other methods. Moreover, those that do not have an understanding the beauty of the Scientific Method and the skepticism it promotes.

The extensive knowledge base of scientific details is why non-detailed thinkers throw up their hands and often label some scientists as nutjobs. They simply don't possess the knowledge-base of many of us that have two primary qualities: 1. keeping an open-minded skeptical sense, 2. continual pursuit of knowledge that better-fits the piece of the puzzle. It's really like a giant pyramid with base knowledge at the bottom and more knowledge leading up to the top--many of us have the base knowledge at the bottom on the pyramid, but only have a spackling of knowledge as you move up the pyramid toward the top, since the knowledge-base is so great.

I'd also argue that neuro-damaged people have a harder ability to visualize these concepts and retain them long-term. I say this because when I was in college in the early 90s, I had an extremely hard time then visualizing in my mind's eye, but that all changed when I fixed myself and my brain fog years ago being obese, going blind and close to death 20+ years ago. This is really the heart of the irony... the very person running on a low battery is the person that could benefit from this information the most._

_Personally, I have a 'belief' that **Fear-Itself** is the primary enemy to healthy skepticism: one's personal belief-system plays into why people hold onto old theories because it can be 'scary' to throw away one's current notions and try-out or implement new ones because it requires an adjustment of currently-held beliefs. Some belief-constructs can hold a person back far more than others, especially when a person cannot separate human-constructed belief from morality, or cannot decipher or compartmentalize one's belief system independently of another system: having mulitple mental lenses rather than filtering everything through a single lens.

A prime example would be the subset of vegans that hold a morality around the harming and treatment of animals. (Sorry if this brings up a can'o'worms with anyone, I actually think it's great we have better standards, practices and ethics across the food industries). But it illustrates how some vegans (not all) can use beliefs to trump everything they personally consume into their bodies, even if it results in the lack of nutrients (like omitting DHA because they omit the consumption of seafood and grassfed meats). This belief-trumping-everything mentality often leads to the refusal to embrace beneficial knowledge and then the outright dismissal of it; in turn, this can take people down a path of pursuit of Justifying-Belief (pride and ego) instead of Pursuing-Truth._


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Is it worth avoiding grains while abroad?

### Matty_M — 2015-08-07T00:29:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-worth-avoiding-grains-while-abroad.16689/post-174143>

Do you guys think it is more important to fit in with the culture and deal with grains (slightly sub optimal), or to attempt to remain epi-paleo?

If I were to eat them (I think I will), am I best off to mitigate them with more H2O, DHA, CT, and sunlight?

Also, I will be living high up in an apartment. 5th or 6th floor. How might I best mitigate this?

Is maintaining use of blue blockers a good idea? I got the red lenses in real glasses, so it is less flashy than my dewalts.

What other advice comes to mind for travelling? I am leaving in 6 days and will be gone for 10 months.

Any specific supplements? Issues in that part of the world? (Chernobyl- is there anything I can do to mitigate this?)
Things to be aware of?

As mostly adults, your wisdom is all appreciated.

\- Matt

### freesia — 2015-08-07T05:25:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-worth-avoiding-grains-while-abroad.16689/post-174169>

Mitigate every way you can that's not too socially isolating. Get your bare feet on the dewy grass and morning sun on your face every opportunity. Do you do cold? Croatia does awesome cold.  Adriatic sea is - refreshing!
Yes to blueblockers, and spread the word!!

Because you're a young guy on a cultural exchange I'd say enjoy the culture, and don't sweat the small stuff. You don't want stress undoing your ability to enjoy this opportunity.

Visit Dubrovnik if you can!  Lots of seafood places there for some excellent mitigation. One of my picks: [https://www.facebook.com/pages/KonobaRibar/280084748669723](https://www.facebook.com/pages/KonobaRibar/280084748669723)
Lots of lovely places just a short cycle away down the coast too.
