### Martha Ray — 2019-09-05T20:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-simple-little-shack-to-call-home.19036/post-277240>

I got even more interested in building materials when I viewed 2018 _The Toxic Home Transformation Summit_ produced by _**Health Talks Online**_ aka **_Health Means_**. Not sure if it can be viewed or even purchased now. But I did find this link It did not play for me but I only have a 'Free" _Health Means_ account.
[https://thetoxichometransformation.com/](https://thetoxichometransformation.com/) Ryan Sternagel was the Host [https://thesternmethod.com/our-story/](https://thesternmethod.com/our-story/)

Several speakers in that summit mentioned [**_Magnesium_** oxide wallboard](https://www.google.com/search?q=Magnesium+oxide+wallboard&spell=1&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjp1u30mLrkAhXRslkKHVa-AbQQkeECCC8oAA) for interior and exterior walls due to Mold proof as well as other benefits.
**Magnesium oxide wallboard .From** [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnesium\_oxide\_wallboard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnesium_oxide_wallboard) **."**[**Magnesium oxide**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnesium_oxide)****, more commonly called magnesia**, is a versatile mineral that when used as part of a cement mixture and cast into thin cement panels under proper curing procedures and practices can be used in residential and commercial** [**building construction**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Building_construction)****. Some versions are suitable for a wide range of general building uses and for applications that require fire resistance, mold and mildew control, as well as sound control applications and many other benefits. As an environmentally friendly building material, magnesia board** has strength and resistance due to very strong bonds between magnesium and oxygen atoms that form magnesium oxide crystals (with the chemical formula MgO)." ..."It is gaining popularity in the US, particularly near coastal regions." Sorry I know Wiki is not a very good source.
**
I believed they mention in the summit that up to 50% of the homes in the US have mold issues so may well be good for a lot more homes also less expensive and used a lot more in Europe.

FYI just in case anyone is moving and or building a healthier home. Two things I looking to do.

**Please let me know if you know any more about Magnesium Oxide walls.
_Thanks._**

# Thread: Light Therapy for low LH/FSH
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-therapy-for-low-lh-fsh.19040/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kris90 — 2017-02-05T19:59:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-therapy-for-low-lh-fsh.19040/post-208017>

I have secondary hypogonadism that I have been struggling with for years. I have treated it successfully with HCG over the years, and have attempted an HPTA restart using Clomid multiple times with no success.

Currently, I am respecting circadian cycles, wearing blue blockers at work, no artificial lighting after dinner other than dim red lights, etc. I also supplement red light and a 365 nm UV bulb while in the office, ground myself, and do CT 2x per day (10 min showers) and get out a few times a day in the sun (AM, and early PM).

I eat epi-paleo with some carbs on days I weight train and tan at a salon. Keto when not "biohacking" winter.

That being said, my most recent Clomid restart has failed: low LH, FSH, normal Total T, but Low Free T, and high SHBG. My understanding is that morning light (i.e. sunrise) is what stimulates the pituitary to release LH. My issue is that I work in the city at 6:30 AM. So I am awake at 5:00 AM when it is pitch black. The sun rise isn't until 7:30 AM, so I feel like my work schedule is a bit of a mismatch. Could this be why I am not getting a sufficient LH release in the morning? Should I adjust my working hours so I can wake up to the sunrise? Or should I consider a 10,000 lux light box for a 30 min session upon waking? Need some advice!

have you got into your situation using steroid?

it's estimated people are exposed to over 60,000 known toxins a day. You could be exposing yourself to BPA which shrivels up your testicles like prunes. You might be consuming soy products - again your testicles shrivel up. There are thousands of estrogen boosting chemicals and toxins out there. Just google it to see for yourself.

Next, I've only found one doctor who knew how to look at an ASI panel. The panel can reflect if you've had a concussion and/or TBI. The panel can show a number of hidden disorders but docs don't know how to look for it.

you could be infected with mold - that will destroy your test levels and again your testicles shrivel up to prunes. You can take a million supplements and steriods - it won't help. You have to get rid of the mold which can take years. It took me 5 years to kill all of the mold.

you could have a imbalance with metallothioneins. Consuming seafood and other foods can cause a toxic overload in people who have certain disorders. I have to be careful or I could be in the ER. Normally Metallothioneins are tied into KPU - a genetic disorder in which you pee out all of your zinc. Without zinc your test levels will be extremely low. You can supplement with zinc but you'll pee it all out until you address KPU.

medications maybe be a cause? Progestrone creams are created from mold. Prozac, prednisone, and Warferin are all drugs produced from mold. It's a long list of meds that can be an issue.

Water source? Tap water may have flouride, bromide or chlorine?

It's a long list of things that can damage your manhood.

Hi JanSz,

I did experiment a little with PEDs in my early 20s (now 26), but I have suspected issues even earlier due to symptoms that improved after using T injections. I have a history of chronically overtraining and starvation-type diets to get lean, so I had assumed I probably overstressed my body.

I did have an MRI of my pituitary, it came back clear. HCG at 300 IU/EOD got me a total T of 29 nmol/l (on a scale of 6-27) and free T of 738 (110-660) and I felt great. Unfortunately now, my LH/FSH is at 2, Total T 15.6, and Free T 190.

I did have an ASI done a few years ago when I was in a bad state. My cortisol levels were borderline low, but Insulin and DHEA were optimal. I would say I feel a lot better in terms of energy right now than I did when I had that ASI done, although I feel nowhere near how I did on HCG.

I train weights in my basement. I have 4 large windows letting light inside, so I do not use artificial lighting in my home gym.

Hmm not sure about BPA. I have a BPA free water bottle, and never use plastic Tupperware for food, only glass. I don't eat any soy whatsoever. Not sure how my ASI would be now (only had one 2 years ago) but in terms of waking up in the morning, it's easy for me. Going to bed same thing, fall asleep within minutes of hitting the pillow. No afternoon crashes, no reliance on coffee, etc. Right now it's moreso I feel blah in terms of emotions and mood. Takes effort to smile and feel pleasure.

I've never been checked for mold allergies. Is there a lab for that? As for zinc, I eat smoked oysters 5-6 times a week, so I think I'm getting my zinc.

Also, my testes work phenomenally, hence why HCG at such low doses put my T levels over the range. My problem is my pituitary never seems to output enough LH and FSH to get my levels where they need to be naturally. Something is suppressing my pituitary, and I thought maybe it's as simple as a circadian rhythm mismatch. I wake up in darkness, drive to work in darkness, and my first encounter with light is fluorescent lighting in my office. Maybe I need stronger light to shut off melatonin in the brain so my pituitary can release enough cortisol and sex hormones?

JanSz, you are misunderstanding me. I have secondary hypgonadism (low LH/FSH, low T, etc). When I am on HCG, my T levels are optimal. That's great and all, but this forum is all about reversing disease which is my long term goal. HCG is only treating my disorder, it's not actually reversing the problem.

When I am off HCG, I attempt a Clomid protocol to kickstart my pituitary to increase LH/FSH. It works O.K., but results not expected, something is suppressing LH/FSH. When tapering off the Clomid, my LH and FSH drop to 2 (on a scale of 1-20), and my T levels also drop to the low end of the range (especially Free T).

My goal is to obtain normal LH, FSH and T levels WITHOUT any meds. I am 26 years old, there is NO reason I should have permanent secondary hypogonadism when there is no apparent damage to my pituitary, and I am otherwise very healthy. Do you understand?

Regarding light therapy, because I work at 6:30 AM in the morning, and am up 2.5 hours before sunrise, I feel as though I am missing the stimulation of sunrise to release my pituitary hormones (as Jack mentioned in CT 7). [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12676335](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12676335)

### kris90 — 2017-02-07T14:03:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-therapy-for-low-lh-fsh.19040/post-208106>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=208077)
>

> what was the reason you did that?
> What was your status the day before you injected T?
> Was it good and you tried to improve?
> Was it bad and you tried to improve?

The reason was that I suspected low T and I was into bodybuilding. Whenever I wanted to bulk up and put on mass, I gained a lot of fat, and minimal muscle. When I tried to lose fat, I lost muscle as well, so I was running in circles. That's when I decided to take T. I felt incredible after only 1 shot, and my wife and I both noticed I became a new man. I went from being very cold, dull, unenthusiastic to bright, optimistic, confident and motivated man with a superman libido. She loved seeing me so happy, and said it was like a switch going off.

I had suspected I had low T, but the doctor's told me I was too young to get it checked, and said I probably have depression. So after my 12 weeks on T, I took Nolva/Clomid, and got bloodwork a few months later, and my total T was around 12 nmol/l and I felt back to my "normal" self (basically lost my superman drive).

Good point. I haven't had prolactin checked in a little while, but whenever I did, it was always just above midrange. Low dopamine makes perfect sense, and I think those who don't embrace winter (i.e. stay indoors, and bundle up) are probably suppressing their dopamine, and I would argue that being a big player in SAD (Seasonal Affective Disorder).

I admit, I definitely spend WAY less time outside in the winter than I do in the summer, so it only makes sense I don't feel as great in the winter. Although this year hasn't been too bad because I always make time to be outside for quick 15-30 mins a couple times a day. Not as good as the countless hours I spent shirtless in the sun last summer, but it does seem to make winter a little easier this year than previous years. Just need to get my LH/FSH fired up, and I'm hoping bright light in the early AM will help with that. I'm willing to give it a shot for a month or so, get more bloods, and if it fails, I guess I'll go back on HCG until spring and try again with the natural sun.

### JanSz — 2017-02-07T15:51:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-therapy-for-low-lh-fsh.19040/post-208114>

I haven't had prolactin checked in a little while, but whenever I did, it was always just above midrange.
\---------------
prolactin(4-15.2)ng/ml
average(4, 15.2)=9.6
so your prolactin was around 10 or more
\---------------
Normally normal prolactin is 6-7
When @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) mentioned Cycloset, many people started using it.
Some reported nausea. Push thru it was suggested by some (I doubt about quality of that suggestion).
When measured, on Cycloset I got my prolactin at 3.4 using 2 pills a day (3 were talked about in discussions)
Eventually I overdid Cycloset, got sick for a week. (overdid by taking pills but delaying food).
The idea of Cycloset is to use it right before breakfast.
I think (to curb raising glucose).

Prolactin correlates with blood glucose.
Higher prolactin, higher glucose.
Reduce prolactin, glucose goes down.

Very low, too low prolactin may cause hypoglycemia.
So, we need some prolactin. (I learned that the hard way).
But, low glucose over extended time, reduces insulin.
Low insulin allows body fat to be used for energy.
It is good to have very low insulin over prolonged time, when trying to loose body fat.
Some call it insulin sensitivity.
Others have issues with names (insulin sensitivity) or (insulin resistance).

Hair products are usually terrible, endocrine interrupters.
Minoxidil is suspected of causing prolactinomas and elevated prolactin levels

Prolactin affects testosterone levels.
Higher prolactin---> lower testosterone.
I think it likely because raising prolactin suppress LH.
That may be your additional chance, @[kris90](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19157/) .

Cycloset=(fast acting)(bromocriptine mesylate)=new, expensive drug

but
regular bromocriptine mesylate is likely better for your situation, much cheaper, either via script or over internet.
I would start with one pill/day and hope for the best.
Measure in blood (fasting 9hrs), early in the morning (7;30AM)
prolactin
insulin
glucose

adjust dose
\------------------------------------
Until you know your response to bromocriptine mesylate, keep 3-4 sugar cubes in your pocket.

TSH is pituitary hormone.
Your pituitary...., we talk about your pituitary a lot.
Observe
FreeT3
RT3

Desirable FT3 is in upper 1/4 of laboratory range
RT3 indicates how much there is of excess of T4.
Good place for RT3 is in mid range.

average(3, 6)=4.5
you want your FT3(4.5-6) so 5.1 is very good number

Check your
IGF-1
IGFBP3

If you are able, get IGF-1 done using RIA method (radioimmunoassay)
some say that chemoliminescence is crap method for this indicator

desirable
IGF-1(300-350) ug/l
IGFBP3(3000)ug/l (if over 4000, you want higher IGF-1)

### kris90 — 2017-02-08T14:00:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-therapy-for-low-lh-fsh.19040/post-208199>

I really think my issue is as simple as a lack of stimulus to my retinohypothalamatic tract because of my working hours: in winter, I wake at 5:00 AM in darkness, I drive to work in darkness, I get to work in darkness, I get up to my desk under fluorescent lighting (put on my blue tech glasses) and that's how I begin my day. I will go out for a walk around 9:30 AM or so, but I feel like I am missing that key stimulus to release my pituitary hormones and shut off melatonin.

This is probably why I get seasonal affective disorder in the winter. I think I need to biohack this: either work later hours so I can see the sunrise, or use a 10,000 lux light box upon waking. As much as I would love to use nature, my working hours are what allows me to make dinner for my family every night so we can eat before the sun goes down. So if I change my hours, we will then be eating dinner in darkness. I should get my light box tomorrow. I'll be sure to provide updates.

# Thread: Let me show you something awful
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/let-me-show-you-something-awful.19064/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matthias — 2017-02-10T20:03:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/let-me-show-you-something-awful.19064/post-208365>

Today, I'd like to show you the (metabolically) worst thing in my entire life by far. It's this:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

This an induction heater.

_**A 3kW induction heater**_

And I hate it.

We use it to heat up parts at the company I work for. We do a form of laser welding (use big lasers to apply powdered metal to a piece), and sometimes it requires getting the part hot ahead of depositing the additive material.

But this is what we used to do it. And I hate it. I can hear the frequency it operates at, too. And I know that it's wicked powerful, wicked toxic. Let me show you:

You ever known 70mG at 50Hz to be good? And that's just 12 inches away. It's literally off the readout right next to it.

Often I have to be right up next to this thing because that Dremel on the left side of the picture needs to be adjusted during the process.

It doesn't even get much better 12 feet away!

That 3mG is a low number; usually it hovers at 7-8 mG. Guy on the right is my coworker Oleg. Oleg is a real G, and soldiers on through this process. I'm trying to educate him about the problems with non-native EMFs. He answers his cell phone constantly, too. (I can't entirely blame him; he has a wife and two young kids).

\~~~~

Even with my Magnetico and with my (soon incoming) Quantlet, I don't know how much I can hold back the tide on this one.

I don't have to use the induction heater every day, but I take a hit on each day that I do:

The parts we use it on are ~50-60 lbs, and have a weird geometry that make it hard to hold. (Especially when some of it has just been welded and is wicked hot). Anyway, in lifting a bunch of them across the past two weeks, I've found that my forearms, on the ulna side, really really really hurt.

And they really hurt whenever I have to lift these parts. Even after ~a week of rest.

When I chipped my tooth, I remarked that Jack and you guys have all taught me that metabolic coincidences just don't happen. And I don't think that the failure of my arms to recover as a result of their relatively increased exposure are an exception.

I don't know if I want to continue working there if I keep having to do stuff like this.

# Thread: Biophotons redshifted from animals (bullfrog, mouse, chicken, pig, and monkey) to humans
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biophotons-redshifted-from-animals-bullfrog-mouse-chicken-pig-and-monkey-to-humans.19068/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2017-02-11T05:12:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biophotons-redshifted-from-animals-bullfrog-mouse-chicken-pig-and-monkey-to-humans.19068/post-208385>

This [article](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/biophotons-human-body-emits-communicates-and-made-light) was posted in the Quantum Health facebook page which got me searching pubmed for more recent papers on biophotons. I found this one super interesting "**[Human high intelligence is involved in spectral redshift of biophotonic activities in the brain](http://www.pnas.org/content/113/31/8753.full)"**
> Based on recent knowledge of the level of intelligence, which is related anatomically to the number of cortical neurons and physiologically to the speed of conductivity of neural pathways, the spectral redshift means that the human brain may use lower energy biophotons (longer spectra) to carry out neural signal communications between neurons to be more effective and economical.

I found it interesting because of what Jack has said in the past and in his Time series particularly [Time 2](https://www.jackkruse.com/time-2-how-is-time-built/) which talks about gravitational lensing and redshift.

> Jack said:
>
> Red light slows time. Cold slows time. Blue light speeds up time and your life is lived like a blue straggler star. Quick and bright. Over supplemented comes from being undereducated about light. Light has weight. As it falls to anything with gravity it gets heavier because light has momentum. Light moving away from mass allows light to "get lighter" because it loses its purple power and shifts to red. Gravitational lensing is a redshift effect.

> The 2016 Biophoton paper said:
>
> **Significance**
> It is still unclear why human beings hold higher intelligence than other animals on Earth and which brain properties might explain the differences. The recent studies have demonstrated that biophotons may play a key role in neural information processing and encoding and that biophotons may be involved in quantum brain mechanism; however, the importance of biophotons in relation to animal intelligence, including that of human beings, is not clear. Here, we have provided experimental evidence that glutamate-induced biophotonic activities and transmission in brain slices present a spectral redshift feature from animals (bullfrog, mouse, chicken, pig, and monkey) to humans, which may be a key biophysical basis for explaining why human beings hold higher intelligence than that of other animals.
> ****Abstract****
> Human beings hold higher intelligence than other animals on Earth; however, it is still unclear which brain properties might explain the underlying mechanisms. The brain is a major energy-consuming organ compared with other organs. Neural signal communications and information processing in neural circuits play an important role in the realization of various neural functions, whereas improvement in cognitive function is driven by the need for more effective communication that requires less energy. Combining the ultraweak biophoton imaging system (UBIS) with the biophoton spectral analysis device (BSAD), we found that glutamate-induced biophotonic activities and transmission in the brain, which has recently been demonstrated as a novel neural signal communication mechanism, present a spectral redshift from animals (in order of bullfrog, mouse, chicken, pig, and monkey) to humans, even up to a near-infrared wavelength (∼865 nm) in the human brain. This brain property may be a key biophysical basis for explaining high intelligence in humans because biophoton spectral redshift could be a more economical and effective measure of biophotonic signal communications and information processing in the human brain.

# Thread: Gassy Baby at Night
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gassy-baby-at-night.19078/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sam Richardson — 2017-02-12T19:38:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gassy-baby-at-night.19078/post-208466>

Our two week old baby has been having some rough cry sessions these past couple of nights. He's good throughout the day, actuallt sleeps through most of it, but once the night hits, oh boy. The only thing that really calms him down seems to be the sound of the dryer and the red light on his face that the dryer emits whike it is turned on (wonder if there is an explanation for this). I'm going to switch the lighting to amber bulbs in the house in hopes that they will help soothe him at night.

As for the bad gas, I assume that it is coming from an environmental mismatch since we live in Toronto, Canada and have not been getting out in the cold weather too much or getting sunlight. We aren't eating seasonally either, so I wonder if that it playing a role. How important is my wife's diet for the baby's breast milk? Our midwife told us that due to circadian biology, the mother's breast milk will become more fatty at night to facilitate sleep for the baby. Could it be the fattier milk that is causing the gas? Might my wife need to get out in the cold more, in particular, will it change the biochemistry of her milk.? How safe is it to expose a two week old baby to the cold as well?

### bionaut — 2017-02-12T20:06:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gassy-baby-at-night.19078/post-208473>

Your wife's diet can make the baby gassy. You'll have to widdle down what it may be. We used to live by a highway which would wake our twin babies often. So ambient noise is problematic, we did use a fan and had no issues but this can affect how children filter noise so use at your own risk.

Babies have a lot of brown fat, so they should be good in the cold, obviously use common sense and err on the side of caution. We would take them out for 5 min at a time to get some sun when it was the strongest in winter.

My newest one is 6 months at this point lights do affect his sleep. Change all the lights in your house for your own sake you will sleep better. Its typical for kids to cry at night, my advice to you would be to buy a pressure cooker, and make a shellfish broth, with beef bones, with cabbage, kelp, mushrooms. Add turmeric and pepper in yours, not hers, it can slow milk production. You want to beef up your glutathione production so you can deal better on less sleep. Also a 10 minute practice of mindfulness meditation each day goes a long way in keeping your cool when things get hard. Goodluck.

# Thread: Set f.lux to 900k (no blue or green light)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rubicon — 2017-02-14T05:15:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208551>

The current version of f.lux gives a user the ability to adjust the lowest color temperature of the screen to 1200K for the nighttime setting. At 1200k most blue is eliminated, but there is still a lot of green. It's possible to set the color temperature even lower, but that involves going into the registry and manually changing the values. [Here are some directions](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/flux-optimal-settings.18319/#post-200138) on how to do that.

The above method is difficult and time consuming to implement. I wanted to be able to quickly change the settings on several computers so I made a vbscript that does it all in one click. The script will change the f.lux settings in the registry for both the day/night to 900k, and then restart f.lux to apply the new settings. It should work on any Windows PC

**Download a VBscript to change f.lux to 900k**
Download the script here: [tiny.cc/FluxScript](http://tiny.cc/FluxScript)

**Directions**

1.  Install f.lux on a Windows PC
2.  Double-click the script after downloading and it will automatically run. The registry settings will be changed to 900k, and you will see f.lux will restart to apply the new settings.
    _\*\*Warning: When f.lux restarts, the screen will suddenly change to full 6000K and then slowly transition down to 900K. You may want to sheild your eyes.\*\*_

If you want to see the code in the script that makes it all happen, right click on the script and select "Open with" notepad.

**Testing blue/green blocking**
To test how well blue/green are being blocked, I open this image and visually check how much blue/green I can see: [RGB.png](http://i.imgur.com/rwemyMX.png)
At 1200K a bit of blue and a lot of green can be seen. At 900K, all blue and green disappears from the RGB image:
[F.lux at 1200K](http://i.imgur.com/9pocr9u.jpg?2)
[F.lux at 900K](http://i.imgur.com/K4xFUKJ.jpg?2)

I really like having the screen at 900K anytime after sunset. Please let me know if you try the script and run into any issues.

### Rubicon — 2017-02-14T05:16:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208552>

Reserved for future use. (directions on how to use Iris ([iristech.co](https://iristech.co/)) to eliminate all blue/green light)

### seanb4 — 2017-02-14T21:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208586>

Hi Philip, from what I'm understanding you mean that the script is opening in a program like notepad instead of running the script. If so, try right-clicking the file, select "Open with...", Microsoft Windows Based Script Host

### Rubicon — 2017-02-15T01:05:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208599>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=208586)
>
> wouldn't something like 2000k be better for day time, lots more red than blue but a bit of blue to signal day time?

My understanding is that blue has damaging side-effects in the eye that are typically mitigated & balanced by IR & red light. Since a computer monitor has no IR, I'm not sure even a low level of blue light from a monitor is "better for daytime".

Currently I use 1200K during the day to give a bit of color definition, and only go higher for brief periods if I have a reason to have true color. In the evenings, I wear blue blocking glasses and set the monitor to 900K.
I get some natural light through the window all day and take breaks outside as much as possible to get full spectrum light.

Perhaps 2000K wouldn't really matter if one was using @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) 's setup as described here: [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-4#post-201738](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-4#post-201738)

### Sue-UK — 2017-02-15T13:02:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208641>

> [Rubicon said:](/goto/post?id=208599)
>
> My understanding is that blue has damaging side-effects in the eye that are typically mitigated & balanced by IR & red light. Since a computer monitor has no IR, I'm not sure even a low level of blue light from a monitor is "better for daytime".

Having an eye injury, my worry is that green appears to be needed to balance the red ... [http://www.healingtheeye.com/Articles/AJO\_IOP\_ light.pdf](http://www.healingtheeye.com/Articles/AJO_IOP_%20light.pdf). I've been playing around with filters, and when I look at [http://i.imgur.com/rwemyMX.png](http://i.imgur.com/rwemyMX.png) through the Dinshah green filter, it seems to give a toned down balanced version of all the same colours, and takes out the brilliance of the white screen. Will make up some DIY glasses for a daytime biohack and report back ....

### philip malone — 2017-02-15T19:29:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208653>

Hello Rubicon, thanks for your answer. using Open With, I could not get to "Microsoft Windows Based Script Host" But if I go use Run to get to HKEY CURRENT USER>SOFTWARE>Michael Herf>Flux>preferences,
I get to "Outdoor REG-DWORD OxOOOOO a8c (2700)" If I click to Modify, only the a8c is presented. Not sure what to do......??
Thanks.

### Rubicon — 2017-02-15T20:02:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208655>

Philip, modifying the registry manually can get tricky. Here's another option: I compiled the .vbs file into a .exe file that should run on Windows without any issue. If you'd like to give it a try, here's a download link for the .exe file: [https://www.dropbox.com/s/32iejjev5xwgh03/Set f.lux to 900k and restart app.exe?dl=0](https://www.dropbox.com/s/32iejjev5xwgh03/Set%20f.lux%20to%20%20900k%20and%20restart%20app.exe?dl=0)

### philip malone — 2017-02-16T17:56:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208711>

Thank You Rubicon! One more question - if I run this, will I still be able to set f.lux brighter if I occasionally want to? Thanks again......

### Rubicon — 2017-02-16T18:34:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-208720>

yep, you can adjust the settings back the normal ranges anytime. If you do change the ranges and you want to get back to 900K, you'll have to re-run the script.

### philip malone — 2017-02-16T19:25:34+0200

Hi Rubicon,

Most computer monitors and TVs are polarized, but sunlight isn't.

I am surmising that polarized light may be just as bad for you in large doses as blue light.

Any thoughts?

### brent-next — 2017-03-13T12:31:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/set-f-lux-to-900k-no-blue-or-green-light.19081/post-210233>

> [Alper said:](/goto/post?id=209233)
>
> Any ideas for mac os x?

One work around for Macs is to re-set your time zone on your computer so that f.lux thinks it's your bedtime. This will allow you to set the color temperature as low as 1200 K.

If you have any timing applications that rely on your time zone being set correctly, this won't work, but for most people it should be just fine.

Can you teach me how to eliminate flicker on iris pro? not sure this is in the settings tab of Iris Pro. I think the flicker effect is built in the display itself, so unless you get a flicker free screen I don't think there is another workaround it.

Dark room mode go to settings - screen effects - simple inversion. and turn screen effects on.
Or you can go to color temperature settings tab and click invert colors.

In my experience using Twilight on Android, the app is terribly ineffective! The Night Shift mode I've seen activated on iPhones is even worse.

# Thread: Does Winter Swimming = Cold Thermogenesis?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### brent-next — 2017-02-14T09:20:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/post-208555>

Last year I discovered the Jack Kruse web site in early October and learned about Cold Thermogenesis (CT). Since I was in Ukraine where there is a long tradition of winter swimming, I thought I would try winter swimming and hopefully I would stay healthy in the winter.

Boy was I wrong! I swam in the river every day and tried to adapt myself to the cold as much as possible by walking around town lightly dressed with my big winter coat under my arm in case I really needed it. I did this from October until the middle of January, when I got the worst flue of my life. I also developed unexplained leg swelling which persisted until I was able to start sunbathing (in a house with less microwaves) in April.

When I got sick, I was eating lots of fish, drinking lots of home made bone broth, exercising regularly and using UV sun lamps regularly. On the down side, there was very little natural sunshine, my apartment had too much wi-fi in it (although I turned mine off) my apartment didn't have a tub for CT, and the only way to get my apartment cool was to open the window.

I swam like the locals did which was typically a 15 to 30 second swim in the river every day. The video below explains what it's like.

So why did I get so terribly sick in spite of doing many things right? Here are my guesses.

1\. It's very had to beat the averages. Millions of people in Ukraine got this flue and 350 people died from it. This was considered a fairly normal year for flue deaths. Note to self. Never spend another winter where people commonly die from the flue. This winter I've stayed healthy (but haven't made much progress on my road to optimal) in Cyprus.

2\. Perhaps the heat and microwaves in my apartment were a bigger detriment than the cold was a benefit? I did turn my Wi-Fi off completely and shut off my power at night and only used dim candles in my apartment after sunset.

3\. Sunlamps can't replace strong natural sunshine. In fact the ozone created by my uv lamps probably contributed to my lung irritation.

4\. I should have been very careful about warming the air I breathed with a scarf over my nose when I was on the street.

5\. Eating a high fat ketogenic diet resulted in more belching and this probably contributed to my lung irritation and sickness. First you belch up a little bit of acid -- then you inhale that acid and irritate your lungs. I should have reduced my fat intake slightly until my digestion improved.

My guess is that 15 to 30 seconds of winter swimming, European style, really doesn't help you that much compared to one hour of CT in a cold bathtub. The winter swimming does trigger the good feelings, but it wasn't enough to keep me healthy in the winter.

In my understanding of CT, we are trying to shrink the respiratory proteins in mitochondria to make them more efficient and make more ATP. Right?

It looks to me like sitting motionless in a 50 deg. F. tub of water for up to an hour would be much better than a few seconds of swimming in 35 deg. F water.

Comments? Clarifications? Suggestions?

### shah78 — 2017-02-14T10:39:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/post-208558>

Fine analysis of your Ukraine experience. NOW FIGURE OUT WHY YOU'VE WASTED YOUR TIME IN CYPRUS.  Why no "progress"?

### brent-next — 2017-02-14T18:22:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/post-208574>

> [shah78 said:](/goto/post?id=208558)
>
> NOW FIGURE OUT WHY YOU'VE WASTED YOUR TIME IN CYPRUS.  Why no "progress"?

It's difficult to weigh factors against each other without knowing if they are big effects or small effects. Sunshine is clearly a big effect. Was 35 degrees north far enough south to go? Perhaps not. Here in Cyprus, I have gotten enough sun to keep from getting worse, but not enough to get better.

On the other hand, I know that I do very poorly in hot climates, which is part of why I didn't go to a Red Sea resort in Egypt.

How much does living in a typical apartment building (which I have done here in Cyprus) hurt your mitochondria from too much wi-fi? I don't know.

Winter swimming seems to be something that will make you feel good for an hour, but not do any significant healing. Doing CT for an hour in your tub might help a lot, who knows.

My guess is that I have lousy mitochondria. I did 22 minutes of CT yesterday and seven minutes today. CT may help, but it will take time to show any effects.

I have methylene blue in my bag, perhaps thats the next thing I should try.

### brent-next — 2017-04-05T11:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/post-211795>

> [shah78 said:](/goto/post?id=208558)
>
> Fine analysis of your Ukraine experience. NOW FIGURE OUT WHY YOU'VE WASTED YOUR TIME IN CYPRUS.  Why no "progress"?

Hi Shaw78,

This is an insightful and helpful question. Thank you for asking it. Part of my answer is the parasite ascaris lumbricodes, which I write about in my optimal journal: [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/brents-optimal-journal.19063/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/brents-optimal-journal.19063/)

I tested positive for this naughty little worm again a couple of weeks ago after testing positive last year and then taking the prescribed medication and then re-testing negative. Doctors in parasite rich parts of the world (like Cyprus or Ukraine) say that a positive test for this worm means you have it, but a negative doesn't mean that you don't have it. Multiple re-tests from multiple different labs are recommended to establish a higher likelihood that you really don't have it. One worm is all that it takes to seriously mess you up and if you have one male worm, you will NEVER see eggs in your stool.

I did this re-test on the advice I had gotten from a sleep Dr. last October, but hadn't had a chance to poop and take it to a lab until now. Big mistake.

Two recent tests for my BUN / Creatine ratio were 15 and a few days later 35. Obviously I need more water and less microwaves. My vitamin D was low in spite of getting enough sun to leave me quite tanned. My Sun Friend UV meter watch was showing a 6 to 8 much of the time, my skin is tanning and I was taking about 2,000 IU of vitamin D per day. Even with all this my 25 OH Vitamin D levels were in the bottom 1/3 of this labs range. My current assumption is that my skin doesn't make a significant amount of vitamin D in my current environment.

The parasite ascaris lumbricodes (the worlds most common round worm parasite) is clearly a big effect.

> In my understanding of CT, we are trying to shrink the respiratory proteins in mitochondria to make them more efficient and make more ATP. Right?
>
> It looks to me like sitting motionless in a 50 deg. F. tub of water for up to an hour would be much better than a few seconds of swimming in 35 deg. F water.
>
> Comments? Clarifications? Suggestions?

Just keep evolving... Was I supposed to say swimming????

The first place I used CT was a 30 degree day with a river about 2 miles from my house. The water was so cold it shocked my whole body into a numbness, that within 10 minutes, was gone and replaced with boundless energy and a sense of well being. 30 minutes later I'm doing as many push ups as I humanly possible could at the time, maybe 50 straight? I was so cold I thought I was dying before I got home, that was the day I truly lived... Just keep evolving bud, you know what feels right, is the light.... Anytime you feel good remember light is sending that message...........

### CTforlife — 2017-04-05T20:38:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/post-211825>

> [brent-next said:](/goto/post?id=208555)
>
> Last year I discovered the Jack Kruse web site in early October and learned about Cold Thermogenesis (CT). Since I was in Ukraine where there is a long tradition of winter swimming, I thought I would try winter swimming and hopefully I would stay healthy in the winter.
>
> Boy was I wrong! I swam in the river every day and tried to adapt myself to the cold as much as possible by walking around town lightly dressed with my big winter coat under my arm in case I really needed it. I did this from October until the middle of January, when I got the worst flue of my life. I also developed unexplained leg swelling which persisted until I was able to start sunbathing (in a house with less microwaves) in April.
>
> When I got sick, I was eating lots of fish, drinking lots of home made bone broth, exercising regularly and using UV sun lamps regularly. On the down side, there was very little natural sunshine, my apartment had too much wi-fi in it (although I turned mine off) my apartment didn't have a tub for CT, and the only way to get my apartment cool was to open the window.
>
> I swam like the locals did which was typically a 15 to 30 second swim in the river every day. The video below explains what it's like.
>
>
>
> So why did I get so terribly sick in spite of doing many things right? Here are my guesses.
>
> 1\. It's very had to beat the averages. Millions of people in Ukraine got this flue and 350 people died from it. This was considered a fairly normal year for flue deaths. Note to self. Never spend another winter where people commonly die from the flue. This winter I've stayed healthy (but haven't made much progress on my road to optimal) in Cyprus.
>
> 2\. Perhaps the heat and microwaves in my apartment were a bigger detriment than the cold was a benefit? I did turn my Wi-Fi off completely and shut off my power at night and only used dim candles in my apartment after sunset.
>
> 3\. Sunlamps can't replace strong natural sunshine. In fact the ozone created by my uv lamps probably contributed to my lung irritation.
>
> 4\. I should have been very careful about warming the air I breathed with a scarf over my nose when I was on the street.
>
> 5\. Eating a high fat ketogenic diet resulted in more belching and this probably contributed to my lung irritation and sickness. First you belch up a little bit of acid -- then you inhale that acid and irritate your lungs. I should have reduced my fat intake slightly until my digestion improved.
>
> My guess is that 15 to 30 seconds of winter swimming, European style, really doesn't help you that much compared to one hour of CT in a cold bathtub. The winter swimming does trigger the good feelings, but it wasn't enough to keep me healthy in the winter.
>
> In my understanding of CT, we are trying to shrink the respiratory proteins in mitochondria to make them more efficient and make more ATP. Right?
>
> It looks to me like sitting motionless in a 50 deg. F. tub of water for up to an hour would be much better than a few seconds of swimming in 35 deg. F water.
>
> Comments? Clarifications? Suggestions?

We aren't trying to make ATP in the cold. We are holding onto your life source and attempting to accelerate it. The more accelerated your life force = the more you can catch the power of light. I suggest you do not swim however and just stay still in a pose. I couldn't swim until weeks after adaption. Now Tahoe swims are nothing.... I love cold lakes

### brent-next — 2017-04-08T16:35:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-winter-swimming-cold-thermogenesis.19082/post-212019>

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=211825)
>
> I suggest you do not swim however and just stay still in a pose.

I think that's good advice. For the last almost two months I have been living in a remote beach resort in northern Cyprus. I had hoped to be in the cold sea for a couple of hours per day. Unfortunately many days including today have been far too windy or had waves too big for me to be in the sea.

I think I would have been a lot better off to get a stock tank set up so I could reliably get in it, rather than waiting for calm enough sea conditions.

I've ended up being in the 62 deg. F sea about three times per week for about half an hour each time. Unfortunately that's not cold enough water and not enough time in it to make any noticeable progress.

# Thread: Classroom Ideas

**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### John G — 2017-02-17T21:01:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classroom-ideas.19092/post-208785>

Hi all,

I'm an educator of young adults, and I want to change the lighting environment of my classroom. The room is 9.5ft long x 9ft wide x 7ft high. The room has 8 squares on the ceiling that each have 3 long fluorescent tubes.

I'm thinking of getting a whole bunch of lamps that look something like this (maybe shorter ones too):
[https://www.walmart.com/ip/Mainstay...ll-Torchiere-Floor-Lamp-Black-Finish/54906105](https://www.walmart.com/ip/Mainstays-MS41-020-001-01-Mainstays-71-Scroll-Torchiere-Floor-Lamp-Black-Finish/54906105).

I'm thinking of plugging in something like this (might get several different types to test various lumens):
[https://www.bulbamerica.com/products/philips-exn-mr16-50w-12v-fl36-bulb-no-fg-1](https://www.bulbamerica.com/products/philips-exn-mr16-50w-12v-fl36-bulb-no-fg-1)

Would this be an effective way to light up a classroom while maintaining the guidelines set by this post? ([https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...or-you-in-purchasing-artificial-lights.17241/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-goal-for-you-in-purchasing-artificial-lights.17241/))

Do you think there might be a better way? The one issue I foresee is too many chords from the lamps tripping students....or not having enough lamps to be bright.

Thanks,

John G.

### fitness@home — 2017-02-17T21:08:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classroom-ideas.19092/post-208786>

# Thread: Lab tests to be done
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-tests-to-be-done.19097/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ayman — 2017-02-18T17:01:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-tests-to-be-done.19097/post-208837>

hello hope everyone is well, below are the tests mentioned in the Epi-paleo RX:

1\. VO2 Max assessment
2\. DEXA body composition scan
3\. Comprehensive blood chemistry panel (Chem-20)
4\. Liver functioning test
5\. Serum Iron, TIBC, Sesum Ferritin and Iron % Saturation
6\. Lipid Panel VAP
7\. Lipid Panel NMR
8\. Homocysteine
9\. Complete CBC with differential and platelet count
10\. Gender specific hormone panels, serum and salivary - (cortisol, total testosterone, free testosterone, Dihydrocortisone (DHT), Luteinizing Hormone (LH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), estradiol (E2), Dehydroepiandrosterone sulphate (DHEA-S), prostate-specific antigen (PSA), insulin-like growth factor (IGF-1), serum leptin level.
11\. Complete thyroid Panel with antibody screening (TPO Ab and Tg Ab)
12\. Highly Sensitive CRP (HS-CRP) -not a regular CRP
13\. Omega 6:3 Serum test (for severe findings consider tissue assay)
14\. Fasting serum insulin and HbA1c assessment
15\. Complete vitamin D3 Panel

Did I miss anything? I'm planning on going to the lab as soon as I get feedback from this forum. Thank you!

# Thread: Red Goggles, In-Door Light, Iodine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-goggles-in-door-light-iodine.19131/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Samuel Brunic — 2017-02-24T04:51:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-goggles-in-door-light-iodine.19131/post-209184>

I have been listening to Dr. Kruse for years.
I have lots of little questions. I would appreciate any advice or thoughts.

**Which googles block the most blue light?**
I have red, orange and amber UVEX goggles and I wear one at night. I hear most people use orange/amber but they don't seem to block much blue light-if I see blue, then blue gets through is my thinking. They all block out a blue LED I have. Red seems to block out more blue light. Dr. Kruse has said that red is the antidote to blue.
I also have JINS with amber lenses but they don't seem to block much. BluTech lenses are $400! I use NightShift, f.lux obviously.

**What kind of in-door lighting set-up do you have?**
I am trying to get UV, full spectrum and IR light in-doors. I have a full spectrum plant food bulb that is only visible. I need to find an IR bulb.
There are [mercury vapor UV bulbs](https://www.amazon.com/Exo-Terra-Solar-Glo-Intensity-Self-Ballasted/dp/B00101JIFG/ref=sr_1_5?ie=UTF8&qid=1487903589&sr=8-5&keywords=reptile+uv+bulbs) for reptiles-**is this harmful to our eyes?** I keep hearing UV is bad for our eyes i.e. nail curing lamps? I'm looking at the Exo Terra Solar-Glo High Intensity Self-Ballasted Uv/Heat Mercury Vapor Lamp.

**Thoughts on iodine supplementation?**
I take a few drops of Lugol's in a drink every few days. Dr. Kruse talks about structured water in the CSF around iodine so I take it as a supplement. I don't believe kelp or seafood has enough. I eat fresh wild salmon daily. How do you get your iodine?

### Debntx — 2017-02-27T03:44:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-goggles-in-door-light-iodine.19131/post-209358>

i have no advice - just thoughts - i am new here as well - edit: educated thoughts much better-

another option for computer is iristech software for eye protection-which i think is better - i use iris mini and set it low - i use the paid version lifetime is $2 (i think) worth it to me - just keep my monitor set at 2300K - since i do nothing that requires anything exact in the way of color rendering

I use the uvex orange goggles at night and at work but recently i have been toying with the idea about using various levels of blocking blue for different times of the day at work under fluorescent lighting-starting out with light yellow - a graduated blocking of blue - pulling out the big guns at night if needed - i just ordered the carbonshades - and dewalt red safety glasses on a whim - will see how they do -I remember Dr Kruse saying that Red is the antidote to blue but I didn't make that connection until you wrote it above in regards to using a "redder" goggle to block more of the blue light - even though i did order some - that thought was not part of my reasoning process. i also rec'd a quote for some bluTech lenses of $299 and i think i will pass on that- Additionally, i would say nightshift does NOT do enough to block blue but i did learn something about a feature on my iphone - ios10 - about setting your color filter on your phone - and i set mine on red - but it looks funky so i will have to play around with it - edit: maybe it looks funky for a reason

at work- i use the nightlight Red- reptile bulb since i'm at work i can get away with that but not sure about the other options for UV light -

As for iodine i used to use Iodoral 12.5 mg - but in the last year started using Nature's Way Kelp 1-2 capsules after reading a little of Dr Wilson's work -

I also read about sauna therapy from Dr Wilson's site but now I use my pocket sauna not to sweat out toxins but to "pre-treat" myself before I head into work under my fluorescent light working conditions using rubylux red light array - 5-10mins only and i don't actually do it daily but a few times a week - i got the idea from a podcast where Dr Kruse spoke of a study about mice pre-treated with red light... and that gave me the idea... i am not a mouse but that idea may just work for me.

good luck to you.

### Sue-UK — 2017-02-27T12:02:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-goggles-in-door-light-iodine.19131/post-209366>

> [Samuel Brunic said:](/goto/post?id=209184)
>
> I have been listening to Dr. Kruse for years.
> I have lots of little questions. I would appreciate any advice or thoughts.
>
> **Which googles block the most blue light?**
> I have red, orange and amber UVEX goggles and I wear one at night. I hear most people use orange/amber but they don't seem to block much blue light-if I see blue, then blue gets through is my thinking. They all block out a blue LED I have. Red seems to block out more blue light. Dr. Kruse has said that red is the antidote to blue.
> I also have JINS with amber lenses but they don't seem to block much. BluTech lenses are $400! I use NightShift, f.lux obviously.

I bought a pair of red, but intuitively didn't like them. A clue came when I found this article
[http://www.healingtheeye.com/Articles/AJO\_IOP\_ light.pdf](http://www.healingtheeye.com/Articles/AJO_IOP_%20light.pdf) about red increasing intra ocular pressure. From the article it seems that darkness naturally increases IOP, but adding red increases it further, with green having the opposite effect, i.e. in this scenario green is the antidote to red, and in natural sunlight green is present. Red glasses may not have the chronological disadvantage of blue and green at night, i.e. be chronologically neutral, but may have other photodynamic disadvantages (such as increasing my risk of glaucoma) if used chronically as a lifestyle choice.

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-01T20:22:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209485>

The entire earth is not "dirty EMF's". Transformers are never buried so it is very easy to avoid those. As far as power lines you just need to stay away from them. You can buy a meter to detect EMF's and check the location of where you bury a grounding stake to be sure it is okay. The earth provides a very powerful ground and you have to be close to buried power lines to get dirty EMF's. If you are not able to do this another option is ground your shoes with one of several options on the market and go walking in a park or out in the country well away from any buildings, buried power lines or other power sources.

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-02T04:43:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209512>

So it is not an issue at all.

No, that is not a problem. An EMF meter tests both (E) electrical and (M) magnetic (F) fields. If you are not able to purchase an EMF meter and they are very available on the internet at low cost then you should not be doing any grounding.

### Scompy — 2017-03-04T19:29:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209621>

> [Mountain Man said:](/goto/post?id=209620)
>
> No, that is not a problem. An EMF meter tests both (E) electrical and (M) magnetic (F) fields. If you are not able to purchase an EMF meter and they are very available on the internet at low cost then you should not be doing any grounding.

For clarity, I'm wanting to show the subtlety of what I'm saying. If I am grounded to the earth, and I am also in a magnetic field at the same time (like lets say a 60 millGauss field) due to a wire running through soil where I am standing grounded to the earth, yes, I am grounded. However, the AC generated magnetic field would still be penetrating my body doing some damage.

### JoeT — 2017-03-04T20:17:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209624>

Excellent point Scompy. To drive it home....I've had an EMF expert recently advise me to find an area with .1 mg or less to ground in. Obtaining an accurate measurement down to .1 requires a dedicated magnetic meter...which start at $275-$300.

In addition, grounding should also be done in an RF environment of .01 mw/m2 or less. Such a measurement can be easily taken w/ a Cornet though.

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-04T21:14:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209628>

> [Scompy said:](/goto/post?id=209621)
>
> For clarity, I'm wanting to show the subtlety of what I'm saying. If I am grounded to the earth, and I am also in a magnetic field at the same time (like lets say a 60 millGauss field) due to a wire running through soil where I am standing grounded to the earth, yes, I am grounded. However, the AC generated magnetic field would still be penetrating my body doing some damage.

That's why you need an EMF meter to make sure you are not in an AC generated magnetic field. If you are in a home with the electricity turned on at the breaker box you are in higher than a .1 MG environment even if everything like TV, computer, space heaters, etc are turned off so that is not an achievable goal unless you shut off all electricity to the entire house at the box. That means you will have nothing electrical including your gas furnace functional.

### JoeT — 2017-03-06T04:39:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209727>

> [Mountain Man said:](/goto/post?id=209628)
>
> That's why you need an EMF meter to make sure you are not in an AC generated magnetic field. If you are in a home with the electricity turned on at the breaker box you are in higher than a .1 MG environment even if everything like TV, computer, space heaters, etc are turned off so that is not an achievable goal unless you shut off all electricity to the entire house at the box. That means you will have nothing electrical including your gas furnace functional.

All of this being said...I've confused myself, maybe one of you can help. Specifically, how is it then advisable to ground in airplanes and cars etc.?

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-06T07:07:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209732>

> [JoeT said:](/goto/post?id=209727)
>
> All of this being said...I've confused myself, maybe one of you can help. Specifically, how is it then advisable to ground in airplanes and cars etc.?

My posts have been about earthing which is grounding yourself by walking barefoot, using special shoes or shoe attachments or putting a grounded sheet on your bed. You do not need to be grounded 100% of the time in fact even 15 minutes a day can work wonders for your health. So while flying or driving you do not worry about it. There are loose straps that go under a car that ground it by attaching to the body of the car with a conducting strap that is long enough to scrap along on the street below if you want to go that far.

### JoeT — 2017-03-06T14:37:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209742>

> [Mountain Man said:](/goto/post?id=209732)
>
> My posts have been about earthing which is grounding yourself by walking barefoot, using special shoes or shoe attachments or putting a grounded sheet on your bed. You do not need to be grounded 100% of the time in fact even 15 minutes a day can work wonders for your health. So while flying or driving you do not worry about it. There are loose straps that go under a car that ground it by attaching to the body of the car with a conducting strap that is long enough to scrap along on the street below if you want to go that far.

Understood. My question was more conceptual. We are all making the case that grounding should be done in a very low nnEMF environment. So, for folks who are grounding themselves in planes or cars... they presumably believe that grounding themselves even in those high nnEMF environments is still a net positive ? So, I'm trying to reconcile everything that we have said in this thread...to that.

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-06T17:31:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209761>

You would not be able to ground yourself in a plane since you would be so high above the ground, have no access to the outside and are traveling too fast anyway. In a car it is not the EMF of the car that is the issue it is the whether the road underneath the car is grounded without EMF pollution from power lines and other electrical sources below. Usually when people ground a car they do not ground themselves so the purpose is just to dissipate static electricity from the body of the car.

### Samy Colddigger — 2017-03-07T05:24:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209808>

..

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-07T19:42:59+0200

> [Samy Colddigger said:](/goto/post?id=209808)
>
> Mountain Man would that grounding of the car also create a better environment for any biological beings in the car?

I do not know. Perhaps grounding the car lowers the EMF 's produced by the electrical system of the car which would benefit the occupants. I have not done any measurements on this. In order for the "biological beings" to be grounded they would have to be connected to the frame of the car by some means that can conduct electricity. Just sitting on a seat you would not be grounded even if the car was.

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-07T19:44:46+0200

### av8r — 2017-03-07T20:01:15+0200

### Mountain Man — 2017-03-07T21:19:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-209837>

> [av8r said:](/goto/post?id=209830)
>
> Its called a floating ground if it is not physically connected to the earth ground... [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floating\_ground](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floating_ground)

For purposes of health a floating ground is very different than an actual ground connected to the earth and it is not accurate to refer to them as equivalent. In the Wikipedia.org article for instance it states "Floating grounds can be dangerous if they are caused by failure to properly ground equipment that was designed to require grounding". Humans evolved with an actual connection to the earth not a floating ground. I have not seen any published research on whether there are any benefits or dangers for people grounded to a plane.

### P. Yoshi M. — 2024-09-05T02:53:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-sleep-wow.19132/post-339295>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=209473)
>
> Forget ground in house wiring, dirty electricity.
> This days one have to think twice before he/she put a grounding rod into ground.
> Power lines, transformers and what not else are in the ground.
>
> .....

l’d hypothesize that this situation describes an overloaded building-ground circuit. In that case, I’d further hypothesize that increasing such a building’s connection to earth’s electric field—i.e. lowering that AC **impedance** (& DC _resistance_)—would cause the excess dirty electricity to dissipate more efficiently &/or completely for any given moment in time.
I’d speculate that augmenting/supplementing the total wire gauge\[-to-length ratio\] connecting the building’s ground circuit to the directly earthed electrode(s) would accomplish this effect. Additionally, I’d presume adding to the total volume (_and surface area_) of ground-contacting electrodes bonded to the system would effect the same outcome. Can any physics savant weigh in on this possibility?

# Thread: best treatment for fungal skin infections
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-treatment-for-fungal-skin-infections.19135/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### John Smith — 2017-02-25T01:22:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-treatment-for-fungal-skin-infections.19135/post-209256>

What is the best treatment for fungal skin infections other than ketoconazole or prescription medicines?

### JanSz — 2017-02-25T16:43:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-treatment-for-fungal-skin-infections.19135/post-209271>

> [John Smith said:](/goto/post?id=209256)
>
> What is the best treatment for fungal skin infections other than ketoconazole or prescription medicines?

ketoconazole

interesting beast

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ketoconazole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ketoconazole)
Antiandrogenic and antiglucocorticoid
CYP17A1 inhibitor

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CYP17A1\_inhibitor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CYP17A1_inhibitor)
A **CYP17A1 inhibitor** is a type of [drug](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drug) which [inhibits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor) the [enzyme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme) [CYP17A1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CYP17A1). It may inhibit both of the functions of the enzyme, [17α-hydroxylase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/17%CE%B1-hydroxylase) and [17,20-lyase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/17,20-lyase), or may be selective for inhibition of one of these two functions (generally 17,20-lyase). These drugs **prevent the conversion of [pregnane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnane) [steroids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steroid) into [androgens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Androgen) like [testosterone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Testosterone) and therefore are [androgen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Androgen) [biosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biosynthesis) inhibitors and functional [antiandrogens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antiandrogen).** Examples of CYP17A1 inhibitors include the older drug [ketoconazole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ketoconazole) and the newer drugs [abiraterone acetate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abiraterone_acetate), [orteronel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orteronel), [galeterone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galeterone), and [seviteronel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seviteronel).

''''''''''''
My friend, with late stages of prostate cancer, (when cancer is able to make its own testosterone)
is considering
[abiraterone acetate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abiraterone_acetate)

tough decision.

//

### Discalced — 2022-10-24T04:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-treatment-for-fungal-skin-infections.19135/post-316859>

I've got some fungal skin stuff happening, would like to know what people have tried that works. I assume it's poor immune functioning.

I'm getting on that sun and light stuff all day, as well as cold. Hesitant to take any anti-fungal medications because of the effect on immune system, or cremes just masking the symptoms

### Marina C — 2018-08-23T20:39:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibody-ranges.19141/post-249208>

I think its better to test for thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) to confirm Hashi's rather than use antibodies. Antibodies cause the issues but because they appear in such a large variety it is hard to use them to test for it as the test could just come back with higher numbers if you're fighting an unrelated illness. They do test for anitbodies to help confirm, but it is better to test TSH first. Here's a few links with some information I was able to find:

[https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/hashimotos-disease/symptoms-causes/syc-20351855](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/hashimotos-disease/symptoms-causes/syc-20351855)

[https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/endocrine-diseases/hashimotos-disease](https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/endocrine-diseases/hashimotos-disease)

[http://www.genetex.com/TSH-receptor-antibody-GTX33561.html](http://www.genetex.com/TSH-receptor-antibody-GTX33561.html)

[https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/hashimotos-disease/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20351860](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/hashimotos-disease/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20351860)

# Thread: Is PPG Efficacious for Sleep Monitoring?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mebstyne — 2017-03-05T23:39:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/post-209717>

After reading John Ott's "Health and Light" (Great recommendation Jack! Thank you.), I found myself thinking at the world from a different perspective. The story about the green filtered light being associated with the classroom of children with the highest rates of leukemia in the country stuck with me. Now, it's important to remember that Ott was not claiming that the green light was causal. But, he did not the association.

Meanwhile, each night, I tend to strap on a device to my wrist to monitor my sleep. And, this device uses Photoplethysmography (PPG) for monitoring sleep. Every minute it wakes up and flashes two bright (light up the room bright) green LEDs against my skin at my wrist and takes a reading.

Now, there are three question I want to raise:

1) "**Is monitoring sleep via bright green LEDs strapped to the wrist a good idea from a health perspective?"** First, I want to thank you all, for challenging me and helping me grow. I would never have asked this question, if I hadn't been researching light, EMF, and other aspects of environment that impact health. Now, I'd love help reigning in research-based and leading theory-based thinking on what the answer to that question may be.

2) Simplifying assumption #1: Assume for a moment that my PPG device used blue LEDs on my wrist. (Let's ignore that they are green for a moment.) Keep in mind, for 8 hours of sleep, the LEDs are "on" for about 2.6 hours (turning on about 20 seconds every minute). Again, they are right against my skin. Clearly, we know from devices like the re-timer that blue LEDs in the nose or ears can have a physiological effect. How about the wrist? How about this 20 second blast every 8 hours for 2.6 hours through the night? What stress might this induce? How severe is that stress, in terms of other health conditions, measurable biomarkers, in terms of studies done, etc.?

3) Simplifying assumption #2: Assume for a moment that instead of a wrist device with 2 LEDs, I had an incandescent green light that flashed on my entire body every minute for 20 seconds (about 2.6 hours each night). What stress might this pose to the system? How might we characterize the severity of that stress in terms of measurable studies, etc.?

Just food for thought. Comments welcomed.

### OptimalSkr — 2017-03-06T01:26:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/post-209719>

The best sleep hack is to have absolute darkness. I don't think the information about your sleep could be as beneficial as just not using it. It seems like it will screw up circadian timing.

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-06T13:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/post-209738>

> [mebstyne said:](/goto/post?id=209717)
>
> After reading John Ott's "Health and Light" (Great recommendation Jack! Thank you.), I found myself thinking at the world from a different perspective. The story about the green filtered light being associated with the classroom of children with the highest rates of leukemia in the country stuck with me. Now, it's important to remember that Ott was not claiming that the green light was causal. But, he did not the association.
>
> Meanwhile, each night, I tend to strap on a device to my wrist to monitor my sleep. And, this device uses Photoplethysmography (PPG) for monitoring sleep. Every minute it wakes up and flashes two bright (light up the room bright) green LEDs against my skin at my wrist and takes a reading.
>
> Now, there are three question I want to raise:
>
> 1) "**Is monitoring sleep via bright green LEDs strapped to the wrist a good idea from a health perspective?"** First, I want to thank you all, for challenging me and helping me grow. I would never have asked this question, if I hadn't been researching light, EMF, and other aspects of environment that impact health. Now, I'd love help reigning in research-based and leading theory-based thinking on what the answer to that question may be.
>
> 2) Simplifying assumption #1: Assume for a moment that my PPG device used blue LEDs on my wrist. (Let's ignore that they are green for a moment.) Keep in mind, for 8 hours of sleep, the LEDs are "on" for about 2.6 hours (turning on about 20 seconds every minute). Again, they are right against my skin. Clearly, we know from devices like the re-timer that blue LEDs in the nose or ears can have a physiological effect. How about the wrist? How about this 20 second blast every 8 hours for 2.6 hours through the night? What stress might this induce? How severe is that stress, in terms of other health conditions, measurable biomarkers, in terms of studies done, etc.?
>
> 3) Simplifying assumption #2: Assume for a moment that instead of a wrist device with 2 LEDs, I had an incandescent green light that flashed on my entire body every minute for 20 seconds (about 2.6 hours each night). What stress might this pose to the system? How might we characterize the severity of that stress in terms of measurable studies, etc.?
>
> Just food for thought. Comments welcomed.

With the veins and arteries being close to the surface, the wrist can be a “window” of opportunity (like the Quantlet), or a window to chaos, (chronic exposure to pulsed or continuous unopposed single or narrow band frequencies with no “balancing” frequencies that would occur in natural light). In context, it might be beneficial to have a short duration LED treatment of a particular frequency as a systemic “tonic” dose of light into the bloodstream through the nose, ears or wrists, taking into account its chronobiological significance, but I think that the same treatment used chronically, at the wrong dose, and/or at the wrong timing, can turn it into a poison.

This article is about wound healing using green LED’s at 518nm. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22380691](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22380691). So in principle and in its context, right dose and duration, potential benefits. Directed at wound, local and secondary systemic effects, but when the wound heals, no further treatment so no chronic systemic exposure. But what would happen systemically if after wound healing, the treatments were continued chronically? What drew my attention was the mention of it raising VEGF, and the wiki entry for VEGF said “_When VEGF is overexpressed, it can contribute to disease. Solid cancers cannot grow beyond a limited size without an adequate blood supply; cancers that can express VEGF are able to grow and metastasize. Overexpression of VEGF can cause vascular disease in the retina of the eye and other parts of the body”.
_
So apart from circadian mismatches, the effects of pulsing, nnemf etc, from that one study, indicating green as pro growth, I think that having that amount of any unopposed green frequency pulsed into my wrist chronically at night is not a risk I’d be willing to take.

### Andrea — 2017-03-06T16:47:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/twitching-while-sleeping.19178/post-209752>

1.
Why do some twitch during sleep? In different muscles, throughout the whole body. Twitches can come a couple minutes apart, sometimes several times a minute.

2.
Once when I was close to someone while they were sleeping and I was wide awake, I got the idea to send them some warm good energy. I had my hand on their chest, making sure my fingertips too touched their skin. I breathed in deep, imagining getting filled with energy, and as I breathed out I imagined loving, warm energy flowing through my arm and fingertips into their heart. They twitched in their upper body. So I tried it again. They twitched again. And again.

What's going on?

im using psyllium husk fibre and love it

### JanSz — 2017-03-08T17:48:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fiber-options.19183/post-209896>

This looks to me as a fiber from a wheat.
That would be the outside shell of a grain.
This days there is a problem with that outside shell.
Historically, that shell was rather soft and pliable.
Now, Monsanto re-engineered seed of wheat so that shell is hard.
It is done because it is easier to grow that kind of wheat.
When someone eats it, it is similar to sand paper, it scratches and injuries intestinal walls.

If you must eat wheat, look for heritage varieties which were not altered.

heritage grains
ancient grains

I've woken up twice in the middle of the night know to ENORMOUS electrical sounds... think: Nikola Tesla experiments!

We don't lose power (although I do have a surge protector on the entire house)... It sounds like this: [Electricity Surge (HD sound effect) - YouTube](https://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=2&cad=rja&uact=8&ved=0ahUKEwiJ-bSM68bSAhXINiYKHZNtAcAQtwIIHzAB&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.youtube.com%2Fwatch%3Fv%3Dzkh5RvUjukg&usg=AFQjCNESD84cwoqLuK1aBAsKr5yXJAfzzg)

> Thanks for the video on pitfalls of EMF filters. Part 8 didn't give tons of info on noises (mine are shocking loud!) but I will tell you that the wiring in Costa Rica is never correct. if you sink 1"dia copper rod, 8-10 feet log, into damp ground.
Just make sure there are no electric wires burred in or near.

### Matt Wilson — 2017-03-08T16:35:16+0200

### JanSz — 2017-03-08T16:51:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greenwave-electric-filters-nikola-tesla-noises.19185/post-209888>

Ok, in USA, I live in the house build in 1960.
Decent neighborhood, good looking house, decent looking wires (on a casual look).
In my case all or most wires have ground. Black, white and ground. For three-ways, black, white, red, ground.
Most ground wires are connected, so if you push a three prong plug it connects to the ground wire.
My problem was that the main box was connected to 1/4" copper wire barely sunken in dry dirt.
So my house (electrically) was rather similar to flying airplane.
Floating ground.

Your mileage may vary.
..
